> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Financial Management Decisions: Time Value of Money - Computations Using Compounding and Discounting Techniques; Securities and Valuation of Securities; Major Financial Decision Areas - Factors Influencing Financial Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Financial Management – The Foundational Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Pandey, I.M. / KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> *Financial Management* is the operational activity concerned with the planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of the financial activities of an enterprise. It involves the procurement and utilization of funds with the primary objective of maximizing shareholder wealth while ensuring optimal risk-return trade-offs.

In simpler engineering-classroom terms, financial management is **"the science of deciding where to put money, where to get money, and how to make that money grow over time."** Every decision in an organization has a rupee attached to it, and financial management gives the structured framework to make those decisions rationally.

**Conceptual Analogy – The "Water Tank" Model**

Imagine your company is a water tank. Financial management answers four questions:
1. **Where does the water come from?** (Financing Decisions)
2. **Where should the water flow to?** (Investment Decisions)
3. **How much water should be stored vs. released?** (Dividend Decisions)
4. **What about the daily flow maintenance?** (Working Capital Decisions)

If the tank leaks (poor decisions), the business dries up. If you keep the tank well-managed, the company thrives.

## 1.2 The Time Value of Money (TVM) – Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> *The Time Value of Money (TVM)* is the financial principle that **a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received in the future**, because the money received today can be invested to earn interest, compounding, dividends, or appreciation. The worth of money changes with time due to its earning potential, inflation, and risk.

**Intuitive Analogy – "The Magic Mango Tree"**

Suppose a kind uncle offers you two options:
- **Option A:** ₹1,00,000 *right now*
- **Option B:** ₹1,00,000 *after 5 years*

Which one would you pick? Most people will pick **Option A** — even though the amount is identical. Why?
- You can invest ₹1,00,000 today and watch it grow.
- Prices of goods may rise due to **inflation** (so future money buys less).
- Future money carries **uncertainty risk** — what if uncle forgets?

This preference for *present money over future money* is the essence of TVM.

**Key Variables of TVM (Always memorize these):**
- **PV** = Present Value (value *today*)
- **FV** = Future Value (value *at a future date*)
- **r** = Rate of interest per period (in decimal, e.g., 10% = 0.10)
- **n** = Number of compounding periods (years, months, etc.)
- **A** = Annuity (equal periodic cash flow)

## 1.3 Securities and Their Valuation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A *Security* is a tradable financial instrument representing ownership (equity), creditor relationship (debt), or rights to ownership (derivatives). Valuation is the process of determining the *intrinsic worth* (fair present value) of a security based on expected future cash flows, discounted at an appropriate rate.

**Intuitive Analogy – "Buying a Cow for Its Milk"**

Imagine you buy a cow for ₹50,000. The cow produces milk worth ₹6,000 a year. The cow itself can be sold at the end of year 5 for ₹20,000. Would you pay ₹50,000? **Only if the present value of all the milk income plus the resale value ≥ ₹50,000.**

That calculation is *security valuation*. A **bond** is a cow that gives fixed milk. A **stock** is a cow whose milk output grows with care.

## 1.4 Major Financial Decision Areas

A finance manager constantly makes decisions in **four interconnected arenas**:

| # | Decision Area | Core Question | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Investment Decision (Capital Budgeting)** | Where should we deploy long-term funds? | Buying machinery vs. building a new plant |
| 2 | **Financing Decision (Capital Structure)** | How should we raise funds — debt or equity? | Taking a bank loan vs. bringing in a partner |
| 3 | **Dividend Decision (Profit Allocation)** | How much profit to distribute vs. retain? | Salary withdrawal vs. reinvestment by a freelancer |
| 4 | **Working Capital Decision (Liquidity Management)** | How to manage day-to-day cash flow? | Managing a household's monthly budget |

**Factors Influencing Financial Decisions** (KTU High-Yield): Risk, Return, Cost of Capital, Time Horizon, Taxation, Inflation, Market Conditions, Legal/Regulatory Environment, and Management Objectives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Engines of TVM: Compounding & Discounting

The entire TVM machinery is powered by **two opposite but symmetric processes**:

- **Compounding** → Moves money *forward* in time (today → future). Used to calculate the future value of a present amount.
- **Discounting** → Moves money *backward* in time (future → today). Used to calculate the present value of a future amount.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TVM matters in KTU exams:** ~30-40% of module 1 problems require you to compute FV, PV, annuity values, or compare alternatives across time. Mastering these formulas is non-negotiable.

### 2.1.1 Single Cash Flow – Compounding

A lump sum **PV** invested today at rate **r** for **n** periods grows to:

$$FV = PV \times (1 + r)^{n}$$

The factor $(1+r)^n$ is called the **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF)** or simply the **Future Value Interest Factor (FVIF)**.

### 2.1.2 Single Cash Flow – Discounting

A future lump sum **FV** is worth today (at discount rate **r**) the amount:

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r)^{n}} = FV \times (1 + r)^{-n}$$

The factor $(1+r)^{-n}$ is the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)** or **Present Value Interest Factor (PVIF)**.

### 2.1.3 Ordinary Annuity (Payments at End of Period)

An **ordinary annuity** is a series of equal payments **A** made at the *end* of each period for **n** periods.

**Future Value of Ordinary Annuity:**

$$FV_{OA} = A \times \frac{(1 + r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

**Present Value of Ordinary Annuity:**

$$PV_{OA} = A \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

### 2.1.4 Annuity Due (Payments at Beginning of Period)

When payments occur at the *beginning* of each period, multiply the ordinary annuity result by $(1 + r)$:

$$FV_{AD} = FV_{OA} \times (1 + r)$$

$$PV_{AD} = PV_{OA} \times (1 + r)$$

### 2.1.5 Perpetuity (Infinite Annuity)

A **perpetuity** is an annuity that continues forever (n → ∞). Its present value is:

$$PV_{perpetuity} = \frac{A}{r}$$

This is famously used for **perpetual bonds, preferred stock, and certain scholarship endowments**.

### 2.1.6 Net Present Value (NPV)

To compare alternatives with different cash flows across time:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}}$$

If **NPV > 0**, the project/asset is **acceptable**; if **NPV < 0**, **reject it**.

## 2.2 Effective Annual Rate (EAR) – Compounding Frequency

When interest compounds more than once a year (m times per year), the effective annual rate is:

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r_{nominal}}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$$

**Why this matters in KTU:** Banks may advertise a "12% annual rate" but compound it monthly — the *effective* rate is higher. Students often forget to convert.

## 2.3 Securities – Classification and Valuation Logic

A security is *anything tradable that has monetary value*. KTU focuses on three families:

### A. Bonds (Debt Securities)
A bond is an *I owe you* contract. The issuer promises:
- **Periodic coupon payments** (C) — usually semi-annual
- **Face value (Par value) redemption** (F) at maturity

The **value of a bond** is the present value of all its future cash flows, discounted at the bond's *Yield to Maturity (YTM = i)*:

$$B_{0} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1 + i)^{t}} + \frac{F}{(1 + i)^{n}}$$

This can be rewritten in compact form using annuity factors:

$$B_{0} = C \times \frac{1 - (1 + i)^{-n}}{i} + \frac{F}{(1 + i)^{n}}$$

**Bond Pricing Logic:**
- If **coupon rate > YTM** → Bond trades at a **premium** (B₀ > F)
- If **coupon rate < YTM** → Bond trades at a **discount** (B₀ < F)
- If **coupon rate = YTM** → Bond trades at **par** (B₀ = F)

### B. Preferred Stock (Hybrid Security)
Pays a *fixed* dividend (Dₚ) forever, but has no maturity. It's a perpetuity:

$$P_{0} = \frac{D_{p}}{r_{p}}$$

where $r_p$ is the required rate of return on the preferred stock.

### C. Common Stock (Equity)
Pays *variable* dividends. The simplest model is the **Dividend Discount Model (DDM)** with constant growth (Gordon Model):

$$P_{0} = \frac{D_{1}}{r_{s} - g}$$

where $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$, $r_s$ is the required return, and $g$ is the constant growth rate. **Validity condition:** $r_s > g$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Future Value (Single Sum) | $FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^{n}$ | Find FV given PV, r, n |
| Present Value (Single Sum) | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Find PV given FV, r, n |
| FV of Ordinary Annuity | $FV_{OA} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$ | End-of-period payments, find accumulated FV |
| PV of Ordinary Annuity | $PV_{OA} = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | End-of-period payments, find current value |
| FV of Annuity Due | $FV_{AD} = FV_{OA} \cdot (1+r)$ | Beginning-of-period payments |
| PV of Annuity Due | $PV_{AD} = PV_{OA} \cdot (1+r)$ | Beginning-of-period payments |
| Perpetuity PV | $PV = \dfrac{A}{r}$ | Infinite equal payments (preferred stock, consols) |
| Effective Annual Rate | $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{r_{nom}}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | Multi-period compounding |
| Bond Value | $B_{0} = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Coupon-bearing bond valuation |
| Zero-Coupon Bond | $Z_{0} = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Bond with no coupons |
| Preferred Stock Value | $P_{0} = \dfrac{D_{p}}{r_{p}}$ | Perpetual fixed dividend |
| Common Stock (Gordon Model) | $P_{0} = \dfrac{D_{1}}{r_{s} - g}$ | Constant dividend growth |
| NPV of Project | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Investment decision making |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of TVM in Real Life:** Engineers use these exact formulas for *project appraisal* (whether to build a bridge), *equipment replacement analysis*, *lease vs. buy decisions*, *pension planning*, and *NPV-based machinery selection*. KTU expects you to apply these to engineering management contexts, not just abstract sums.

## 2.5 Factors Influencing Financial Decisions – Deep Analysis

### 2.5.1 Risk
- The variability of expected returns. Higher risk → higher required return → lower valuation.
- **Systematic risk (market risk)** cannot be diversified.
- **Unsystematic risk (firm-specific risk)** can be reduced via diversification.

### 2.5.2 Return
- The reward for bearing risk. Total return = Capital gain + Dividend/Interest income.
- The *risk-return trade-off* is the central theme of financial management.

### 2.5.3 Time Horizon
- Longer time horizons increase both opportunity and uncertainty.
- TVM makes time explicit via the exponent n.

### 2.5.4 Cost of Capital
- The minimum rate of return a firm must earn to satisfy its investors.
- WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) is the discount rate used in NPV calculations.

### 2.5.5 External Environment
- **Inflation:** erodes the purchasing power of future cash flows.
- **Taxation:** alters after-tax cash flows (e.g., interest on debt is tax-deductible).
- **Market Conditions:** liquidity, interest rate cycles, and investor sentiment.
- **Regulatory Framework:** SEBI/RBI guidelines, Companies Act provisions, tax laws.

### 2.5.6 Internal Factors
- Firm size, capital structure, dividend policy, management quality, industry type.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Future Value of a Single Sum

We start with the most fundamental question of TVM: "If I invest ₹PV today at interest rate r per period, how much will I have after n periods?"

**Step 1: Define the value at end of period 1.**

The bank pays interest $r$ on the principal $PV$. So at the end of year 1:

$$V_{1} = PV + PV \cdot r = PV(1 + r)$$

**Step 2: Value at end of period 2.**

Now interest is earned on $V_1$ (interest on interest – this is the essence of compounding):

$$V_{2} = V_{1} + V_{1} \cdot r = V_{1}(1 + r) = PV(1 + r)(1 + r) = PV(1 + r)^{2}$$

**Step 3: Value at end of period 3.**

$$V_{3} = V_{2}(1 + r) = PV(1 + r)^{2}(1 + r) = PV(1 + r)^{3}$$

**Step 4: Generalize to n periods.**

By induction, after n periods:

$$FV = PV \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$$

This is the **fundamental compounding equation**. The factor $(1+r)^n$ is the geometric growth multiplier.

## 3.2 Derivation: Present Value of a Single Sum

This is the *inverse* of compounding. We solve the compounding equation for PV:

$$FV = PV \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$$

**Step 1: Divide both sides by $(1+r)^n$:**

$$\frac{FV}{(1 + r)^{n}} = PV$$

**Step 2: Write in standard PV form:**

$$PV = FV \cdot (1 + r)^{-n}$$

**Interpretation:** A future rupee is worth $(1+r)^{-n}$ rupees today. As $n$ increases, the present value shrinks (a far-future rupee is worth very little today).

## 3.3 Derivation: Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity

Let each payment be $A$, made at the end of each of $n$ periods. Each payment will be compounded for a different number of periods before reaching the future date.

**Step 1: The first payment at end of year 1 will compound for $(n - 1)$ years:**

$$FV_{1} = A \cdot (1 + r)^{n-1}$$

**Step 2: The second payment (end of year 2) compounds for $(n - 2)$ years:**

$$FV_{2} = A \cdot (1 + r)^{n-2}$$

**Step 3: Continue for all n payments...**

$$FV_{n} = A \cdot (1 + r)^{0} = A$$

**Step 4: Sum the geometric series:**

$$FV_{OA} = A(1+r)^{n-1} + A(1+r)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+r)^{0}$$

$$FV_{OA} = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+r)^{k}$$

**Step 5: Apply the geometric series sum formula:**

Recall that for any geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x^{k} = \dfrac{x^{n} - 1}{x - 1}$. Substituting $x = (1+r)$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+r)^{k} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{(1+r) - 1} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

**Step 6: Final result:**

$$FV_{OA} = A \cdot \frac{(1 + r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity

We discount each annuity payment back to today:

**Step 1: Discount each payment:**

$$PV = \frac{A}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

**Step 2: Factor out A:**

$$PV = A \sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t}$$

**Step 3: Apply the geometric sum formula for $x = (1+r)^{-1}$:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t} = \frac{(1+r)^{-1} \cdot [1 - (1+r)^{-n}]}{1 - (1+r)^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the denominator:**

$$1 - (1+r)^{-1} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+r} = \frac{(1+r) - 1}{1+r} = \frac{r}{1+r}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t} = \frac{(1+r)^{-1} \cdot [1 - (1+r)^{-n}]}{r / (1+r)} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 6: Final result:**

$$PV_{OA} = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Gordon Growth Model for Common Stock

**Assumption:** Dividends grow at constant rate $g$ forever. The next dividend is $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$.

**Step 1: Write the expected dividend stream:**

$$D_1, D_2 = D_1(1+g), D_3 = D_1(1+g)^{2}, \ldots, D_{n} = D_1(1+g)^{n-1}$$

**Step 2: Discount each dividend to present:**

$$P_{0} = \frac{D_{1}}{(1+r_{s})} + \frac{D_{1}(1+g)}{(1+r_{s})^{2}} + \frac{D_{1}(1+g)^{2}}{(1+r_{s})^{3}} + \cdots$$

**Step 3: Recognize this as an infinite geometric series with first term $\dfrac{D_{1}}{1+r_{s}}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{(1+g)}{(1+r_{s})}$.**

**Step 4: Sum the infinite series using $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{a}{1 - q}$ (valid when $\vert q \vert < 1$, i.e., $g < r_s$):**

$$P_{0} = \frac{\dfrac{D_{1}}{1+r_{s}}}{1 - \dfrac{(1+g)}{(1+r_{s})}} = \frac{D_{1}}{(1+r_{s}) - (1+g)} = \frac{D_{1}}{r_{s} - g}$$

This is the **Gordon Growth Model**, a cornerstone of equity valuation in KTU's syllabus.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Comprehensive):**
> *A firm receives an investment of ₹5,00,000 today. It earns 10% annual interest compounded yearly for 5 years. Additionally, it will receive ₹1,00,000 at the end of each year for 5 years. Find (a) the future value of the lump sum, (b) the future value of the annuity, and (c) the total FV.*

**Given:** $PV = 5{,}00{,}000$; $A = 1{,}00{,}000$; $r = 0.10$; $n = 5$.

### Part (a): FV of Lump Sum

$$FV_{lump} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1.10)^{5}$$

Compute $(1.10)^5$ step by step:

$$(1.10)^{1} = 1.10$$

$$(1.10)^{2} = 1.21$$

$$(1.10)^{3} = 1.331$$

$$(1.10)^{4} = 1.4641$$

$$(1.10)^{5} = 1.61051$$

Therefore:

$$FV_{lump} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.61051 = 8{,}05{,}255$$

### Part (b): FV of Ordinary Annuity

$$FV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{(1.10)^{5} - 1}{0.10}$$

$$FV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10}$$

$$FV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.61051}{0.10}$$

$$FV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1051 = 6{,}10{,}510$$

### Part (c): Total Future Value

$$FV_{total} = FV_{lump} + FV_{OA}$$

$$FV_{total} = 8{,}05{,}255 + 6{,}10{,}510 = 14{,}15{,}765$$

**Verification by Year-wise Compounding:**

| Year | Opening Balance | Annuity (end) | Interest @ 10% | Closing Balance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 50,000 | 6,50,000 |
| 2 | 6,50,000 | 1,00,000 | 65,000 | 8,15,000 |
| 3 | 8,15,000 | 1,00,000 | 81,500 | 9,96,500 |
| 4 | 9,96,500 | 1,00,000 | 99,650 | 11,96,150 |
| 5 | 11,96,150 | 1,00,000 | 1,19,615 | 14,15,765 |

The year-wise result **14,15,765** matches our formula result, confirming correctness.

## 3.7 Bond Valuation – Worked Example

> **Example:** A 10-year bond has a face value of ₹1,000 and pays an annual coupon of ₹90. The YTM is 8%. Find the bond price.

**Given:** $F = 1{,}000$; $C = 90$; $i = 0.08$; $n = 10$.

**Step 1: Compute PV of coupon annuity.**

$$PV_{coupons} = 90 \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{10}$:

$$(1.08)^{10} = 2.15892$$

Therefore:

$$(1.08)^{-10} = \frac{1}{2.15892} = 0.46319$$

$$PV_{coupons} = 90 \times \frac{1 - 0.46319}{0.08} = 90 \times \frac{0.53681}{0.08}$$

$$PV_{coupons} = 90 \times 6.71008 = 603.91$$

**Step 2: Compute PV of face value redemption.**

$$PV_{face} = \frac{1{,}000}{(1.08)^{10}} = \frac{1{,}000}{2.15892} = 463.19$$

**Step 3: Total bond price.**

$$B_{0} = 603.91 + 463.19 = 1{,}067.10$$

**Interpretation:** Since ₹1,067.10 > ₹1,000 (par), the bond trades at a **premium** because the coupon rate (9%) exceeds the YTM (8%).

## 3.8 Common Stock Valuation – Worked Example

> **Example:** A firm's last dividend was ₹5 per share. Dividends are expected to grow at 6% perpetually. Required return is 12%. Find the share price.

**Given:** $D_0 = 5$; $g = 0.06$; $r_s = 0.12$.

**Step 1: Compute next year's dividend.**

$$D_{1} = D_{0} \times (1 + g) = 5 \times 1.06 = 5.30$$

**Step 2: Apply Gordon Growth Model.**

$$P_{0} = \frac{D_{1}}{r_{s} - g} = \frac{5.30}{0.12 - 0.06} = \frac{5.30}{0.06}$$

$$P_{0} = 88.33$$

**Interpretation:** The intrinsic value of one share is approximately ₹88.33. If the market price is lower, the stock is undervalued (a *buy* signal in financial decision-making).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart – The TVM Engine

This diagram maps the *operational logic* of TVM computations that KTU expects you to follow step-by-step in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Identify the Cash Flow Pattern]) --> nodeB{Is it a single lump sum?}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC{Find PV or FV?}
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD{Equal periodic payments?}
    nodeC -- Future Value --> nodeE[Apply FV = PV times one plus r to the power n]
    nodeC -- Present Value --> nodeF[Apply PV = FV divided by one plus r to the power n]
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeG{Ordinary or Annuity Due?}
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeH[Uneven Cash Flow: Discount each individually and sum]
    nodeG -- Ordinary --> nodeI[Use OA formulas without the one plus r shift]
    nodeG -- Annuity Due --> nodeJ[Multiply OA result by one plus r]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Compare with alternatives using NPV]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL([Decision: Accept if NPV is positive])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart – Financial Decision Tree

This block-level architecture map shows the interrelationship between the four major financial decision areas.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Finance Manager at the Helm]) --> nodeInv[Investment Decision]
    nodeStart --> nodeFin[Financing Decision]
    nodeStart --> nodeDiv[Dividend Decision]
    nodeStart --> nodeWC[Working Capital Decision]
    
    nodeInv --> nodeInvA[Capital Budgeting using NPV and IRR]
    nodeInv --> nodeInvB[Asset Selection: Replace vs. Retain]
    
    nodeFin --> nodeFinA[Debt vs. Equity Mix]
    nodeFin --> nodeFinB[Cost of Capital Minimization]
    
    nodeDiv --> nodeDivA[Dividend Payout Ratio Setting]
    nodeDiv --> nodeDivB[Retention for Growth Funding]
    
    nodeWC --> nodeWCA[Cash and Liquidity Management]
    nodeWC --> nodeWCB[Inventory and Receivables Control]
    
    nodeInvA --> nodeObj([Goal: Maximize Shareholder Wealth])
    nodeFinA --> nodeObj
    nodeDivA --> nodeObj
    nodeWCA --> nodeObj
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart – Security Valuation Logic

This schematic shows how different securities flow into a unified valuation framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSec([Security to be Valued]) --> nodeType{Type of Security?}
    
    nodeType -- Debt: Bond --> nodeBond[Identify Coupon C, Face F, Maturity n, YTM i]
    nodeBond --> nodeBondF[Compute PV of Coupon Annuity]
    nodeBond --> nodeBondG[Compute PV of Face Value Redemption]
    nodeBondF --> nodeBondH[Sum: Bond Price B0]
    nodeBondG --> nodeBondH
    
    nodeType -- Perpetual: Preferred Stock --> nodePref[Identify Annual Dividend Dp and Required Return rp]
    nodePref --> nodePrefF[Apply Perpetuity Formula: P0 = Dp over rp]
    
    nodeType -- Equity: Common Stock --> nodeCom[Identify D1, g, rs]
    nodeCom --> nodeComF{Constant Growth Assumed?}
    nodeComF -- YES --> nodeComG[Apply Gordon Model: P0 = D1 divided by rs minus g]
    nodeComF -- NO --> nodeComH[Use Multi-Stage DDM or FCFE Model]
    
    nodeBondH --> nodeOut([Intrinsic Value Determined])
    nodePrefF --> nodeOut
    nodeComG --> nodeOut
    nodeComH --> nodeOut
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – Factors Influencing Financial Decisions

A block-level functional architecture mapping the *internal* and *external* factors that feed into the financial decision-making engine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph extA[External Environment]
        fA1[Inflation]
        fA2[Taxation Policies]
        fA3[Market Interest Rates]
        fA4[Regulatory Framework: SEBI, RBI, Companies Act]
    end
    
    subgraph intA[Internal Firm Factors]
        fB1[Risk Appetite of the Firm]
        fB2[Cost of Capital WACC]
        fB3[Management Objectives]
        fB4[Capital Structure Stage]
    end
    
    subgraph goalA[Financial Decisions]
        gA1[Investment]
        gA2[Financing]
        gA3[Dividend]
        gA4[Working Capital]
    end
    
    fA1 --> gA1
    fA1 --> gA3
    fA2 --> gA2
    fA2 --> gA3
    fA3 --> gA2
    fA3 --> gA1
    fA4 --> gA2
    fB1 --> gA1
    fB2 --> gA1
    fB2 --> gA2
    fB3 --> gA3
    fB4 --> gA2
    
    gA1 --> goal([Maximize Shareholder Wealth])
    gA2 --> goal
    gA3 --> goal
    gA4 --> goal
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A1: Define the Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee today worth more than a rupee in the future? Mention any two reasons.

**Model Answer:**

> *The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the financial principle that a rupee received today is worth more than the same rupee received at a future date, due to its earning potential, inflation, and uncertainty.*
>
> *Two reasons a present rupee is worth more than a future rupee:*
> - ***Earning Potential:*** *Money available today can be invested to earn interest, dividends, or capital gains, growing in value over time.*
> - ***Inflation:*** *The general price level tends to rise over time, eroding the purchasing power of future money; thus, a future rupee will buy fewer goods than a present rupee.*

*(Marks distribution: Definition – 1 mark; Two reasons – 1 mark each = 2 marks. Total: 3 marks)*

### Question A2: Distinguish between an Ordinary Annuity and an Annuity Due with a suitable example of each.

**Model Answer:**

> | Basis | Ordinary Annuity | Annuity Due |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Timing of Payment** | Payments occur at the *end* of each period | Payments occur at the *beginning* of each period |
> | **FV Formula** | $A \times \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$ | $A \times \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r} \times (1+r)$ |
> | **PV Formula** | $A \times \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | $A \times \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r} \times (1+r)$ |
> | **Example** | Monthly EMI paid at the *end* of the month | Annual insurance premium paid at the *start* of the policy year |
> | **Value** | Lower (payments have less time to compound) | Higher (payments have one extra period to compound) |

*(Marks distribution: Tabular distinction with formula + example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, End-Semester Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks): Time Value of Money and Compounding

**(a)** Explain the concept of compounding in Time Value of Money. Derive the formula for the future value of a single sum and an ordinary annuity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company invests ₹2,00,000 today at 9% per annum compounded annually. In addition, it deposits ₹25,000 at the end of each year for the next 10 years into the same account. Calculate:
   - (i) The future value of the lump sum at the end of 10 years.
   - (ii) The future value of the annuity at the end of 10 years.
   - (iii) The total accumulated amount at the end of 10 years.
   *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Concept of Compounding (2 marks):** Compounding is the process by which a sum of money grows over time because interest is earned not only on the original principal but also on the accumulated interest from prior periods. The frequency of compounding (annual, semi-annual, quarterly) affects the total accumulation.
>
> **Derivation of FV of Single Sum (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:
>
> At the end of year 1: $V_1 = PV(1+r)$
>
> At the end of year 2: $V_2 = V_1(1+r) = PV(1+r)^2$
>
> Continuing this pattern for n periods yields:
>
> $$FV = PV \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$$
>
> **Derivation of FV of Ordinary Annuity (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3, summing each end-of-period payment compounded to the future date:
>
> $$FV_{OA} = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+r)^{k} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Given:** $PV = 2{,}00{,}000$; $A = 25{,}000$; $r = 0.09$; $n = 10$.
>
> **Step 1: Compute $(1.09)^{10}$.**
>
> $$ (1.09)^{1} = 1.09 $$
>
> $$ (1.09)^{2} = 1.1881 $$
>
> $$ (1.09)^{5} \approx 1.5386 $$
>
> $$ (1.09)^{10} = (1.5386)^{2} \approx 2.3674 $$
>
> **Part (i) – FV of Lump Sum (2 marks):**
>
> $$ FV_{lump} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.3674 = 4{,}73{,}480 $$
>
> **Part (ii) – FV of Annuity (3 marks):**
>
> $$ FV_{OA} = 25{,}000 \times \frac{(1.09)^{10} - 1}{0.09} $$
>
> $$ FV_{OA} = 25{,}000 \times \frac{2.3674 - 1}{0.09} $$
>
> $$ FV_{OA} = 25{,}000 \times \frac{1.3674}{0.09} $$
>
> $$ FV_{OA} = 25{,}000 \times 15.1929 = 3{,}79{,}823 $$
>
> **Part (iii) – Total Accumulated Amount (2 marks):**
>
> $$ FV_{total} = 4{,}73{,}480 + 3{,}79{,}823 = 8{,}53{,}303 $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Securities Valuation and Financial Decision Areas

**(a)** What is a security? Explain the valuation of the following with suitable formulas and assumptions:
   - (i) Bonds
   - (ii) Preferred Stock
   - (iii) Common Stock (Gordon Growth Model)
   *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **four major financial decision areas** of a firm. Explain any **five factors** that influence these financial decisions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A security is a tradable financial instrument — such as a stock, bond, or debenture — that represents an ownership position, a creditor relationship, or a right to ownership. Valuation is the process of determining the intrinsic (fair) present value of a security.
>
> **(i) Bond Valuation (2 marks):** A bond is a debt instrument paying periodic coupons (C) and returning face value (F) at maturity (n). Its value is the PV of all future cash flows at the Yield to Maturity (YTM = i):
>
> $$B_{0} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1 + i)^{t}} + \frac{F}{(1 + i)^{n}}$$
>
> **Assumption:** The investor holds the bond to maturity, and coupons are reinvested at the YTM.
>
> **(ii) Preferred Stock (2 marks):** Pays a *fixed* dividend $D_p$ in perpetuity (no maturity). The required return is $r_p$. Hence:
>
> $$P_{0} = \frac{D_{p}}{r_{p}}$$
>
> **Assumption:** Dividends are constant and paid forever; the firm is a going concern.
>
> **(iii) Common Stock – Gordon Model (2 marks):** For a stock with constant dividend growth rate $g$, where $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$:
>
> $$P_{0} = \frac{D_{1}}{r_{s} - g}$$
>
> **Assumption:** $r_s > g$; growth $g$ continues forever; dividends follow a smooth geometric pattern.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Four Major Financial Decision Areas (3.5 marks):**
>
> - **1. Investment Decisions (Capital Budgeting):** Allocation of long-term funds to profitable projects (e.g., plant, machinery, R&D). Evaluated using NPV, IRR, Payback Period.
> - **2. Financing Decisions (Capital Structure):** Determining the optimal mix of debt and equity to minimize the cost of capital. Influences leverage and financial risk.
> - **3. Dividend Decisions:** Deciding how much of net profit to distribute to shareholders vs. retain for reinvestment. Affects share price and growth.
> - **4. Working Capital Decisions:** Managing short-term assets (cash, receivables, inventory) and short-term liabilities to ensure liquidity and operational smoothness.
>
> **Five Factors Influencing Financial Decisions (3.5 marks):**
>
> 1. **Risk:** Higher uncertainty demands higher returns and conservative financing.
> 2. **Return:** Projects and securities are chosen based on maximizing expected return per unit of risk.
> 3. **Cost of Capital:** Acts as the benchmark discount rate in NPV-based decisions.
> 4. **Inflation:** Erodes the value of future cash flows and influences interest rate structures.
> 5. **Government Policy & Taxation:** Tax shields on debt, dividend taxation, and regulatory ceilings affect capital structure and dividend payout.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Formula Misapplication (Common – 2 to 3 marks lost):** Students often confuse *annuity due* with *ordinary annuity* but apply only the ordinary formula. Always re-read whether the payment timing is "beginning" or "end" of the period.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Error:** In NPV, the *initial investment* is a cash *outflow* and must carry a negative sign. Many students forget this and compute inflated NPVs.
>
> 3. **Bond Premium/Discount Confusion:** If a student writes "bond trades at premium" without comparing the coupon rate with the YTM, marks are deducted. Always state both rates and compare.
>
> 4. **Gordon Model Validity:** Applying the Gordon Growth Model when $g \geq r_s$ yields a meaningless negative or infinite price. Always state the assumption $r_s > g$.
>
> 5. **Factor Notation Error:** Mixing up $(1+r)^n$ with $(1+r)^{-n}$ in the annuity formula. Memorize: *F*-actor has positive exponent, *P*-factor has negative exponent.
>
> 6. **Neglecting Compounding Frequency:** When interest is compounded semi-annually or quarterly, students forget to convert the rate and adjust the number of periods — leading to wrong answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **TVM Core Idea:** Money has time value because it can earn, be eroded by inflation, and carries risk. A *present rupee > a future rupee*.
- **Two Pillars of TVM:** *Compounding* (PV → FV, forward in time) and *Discounting* (FV → PV, backward in time).
- **SPCAF / FVIF:** The factor $(1+r)^n$ — used to grow a lump sum forward.
- **SPPWF / PVIF:** The factor $(1+r)^{-n}$ — used to discount a lump sum backward.
- **Ordinary Annuity vs. Annuity Due:** Ordinary payments are at *end*; annuity due payments are at *beginning*. Annuity due = Ordinary × $(1+r)$.
- **Perpetuity:** A never-ending annuity. PV = A/r. Used for preferred stock, consols, certain endowments.
- **Effective Annual Rate (EAR):** $EAR = (1 + r_{nom}/m)^m - 1$. Always convert nominal to effective for fair comparison.
- **Bond Pricing Rule:** Coupon > YTM → Premium; Coupon < YTM → Discount; Coupon = YTM → Par.
- **Bond Formula:** $B_0 = C \cdot PVIFA(i, n) + F \cdot PVIF(i, n)$.
- **Zero-Coupon Bond:** Pays no coupons; $Z_0 = F / (1+i)^n$.
- **Preferred Stock:** $P_0 = D_p / r_p$ (a perpetuity formula).
- **Common Stock (Gordon):** $P_0 = D_1 / (r_s - g)$. Condition: $r_s > g$.
- **Four Major Financial Decisions:** Investment, Financing, Dividend, Working Capital Management.
- **Five Key Factors Influencing Decisions:** Risk, Return, Cost of Capital, Inflation, Taxation & Regulatory Environment.
- **Wealth Maximization:** The supreme objective of financial management — superior to profit maximization because it accounts for time, risk, and cash flow size.
- **Time Value in Engineering:** NPV is the bedrock of *equipment selection*, *lease vs. buy*, *replacement analysis*, and *project feasibility* — all KTU-favored engineering applications.
- **Watch-Outs in Exam:** (i) Verify whether compounding is annual or otherwise. (ii) Don't forget the negative sign on initial outflows. (iii) Always state assumptions before applying Gordon Model. (iv) Show step-by-step factor calculation for compound powers like $(1.10)^5$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal and External Factors; Financial Management for Engineers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal and External Factors; Financial Management for Engineers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Financial Management for Engineers

**Financial Management** is the strategic planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of financial activities in an organization. For engineers, it is the application of financial principles and techniques to make optimal engineering project decisions, capital allocation choices, and operational decisions that align technical goals with monetary objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Financial Management for Engineers* refers to the discipline of planning, acquiring, allocating, and controlling financial resources within engineering projects, firms, and technology-driven enterprises, ensuring maximum shareholder wealth creation while balancing risk, liquidity, profitability, and sustainability.

### 1.2 What Are Internal and External Factors?

**Internal Factors** are variables, conditions, and forces that exist **within the organization** and are largely controllable by management. These arise from the firm's own operations, structure, policies, and resources.

**External Factors** are variables, conditions, and forces that exist **outside the organization** and are **not directly controllable** by management. These arise from the macro and micro environment in which the firm operates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 explicitly requires students to understand how internal firm-specific factors (size, ownership, capital structure, working capital) and external environmental factors (economic, political, legal, technological, socio-cultural) collectively influence financial decision-making in engineering organizations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine driving a car on a highway:**

- **Internal Factors** are like the *car's own systems* — engine health, fuel level, tyre pressure, steering alignment, and brake condition. The driver (manager) can directly control, repair, or adjust these to optimize performance.

- **External Factors** are like the *highway environment* — weather conditions, traffic congestion, road construction, traffic signals, and speed limits. The driver cannot control these but must constantly adapt the driving strategy to reach the destination safely and efficiently.

**The Destination** represents the firm's financial goals — profitability, liquidity, growth, and wealth maximization.

Just as a skilled driver must balance both the car's internal condition and external road conditions, a competent financial manager in an engineering enterprise must balance internal capabilities with external realities to achieve financial success.

## 1.4 Core Financial Management Objectives (KTU Framework)

The two primary objectives under KTU 2024 Scheme are:

1. **Profit Maximization** — Increasing the firm's earnings (older, narrower view).
2. **Wealth Maximization** — Maximizing the market value of shareholders' equity (modern, superior view used by KTU).

| Objective | Focus | Time Horizon | Risk Consideration |
|-----------|-------|--------------|-------------------|
| Profit Maximization | Short-term earnings | Short-run | Ignores risk |
| Wealth Maximization | Market value of shares | Long-run | Considers risk, time value of money |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners consistently prefer **Wealth Maximization** as the primary objective. Always justify financial decisions using this principle in exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Internal Factors

Internal factors are firm-specific elements that financial managers can influence. They operate at the micro-organizational level.

### A. Organizational Structure and Size
- **Firm Size:** Large firms have better access to capital markets, economies of scale, and diversified risk portfolios compared to SMEs.
- **Ownership Structure:** Sole proprietorship, partnership, private limited, public limited, and multinational corporations each have unique financial implications.
- **Life Cycle Stage:** Start-up, growth, maturity, and decline stages each demand different financial strategies.

### B. Financial Structure and Capital Composition
- **Capital Structure Decision:** Mix of debt and equity used to finance operations.
- **Working Capital Management:** Management of current assets and current liabilities.
- **Retained Earnings Policy:** Ploughing back profits versus distributing as dividends.

### C. Operational Factors
- **Cost Structure:** Fixed vs Variable cost ratio influences break-even and leverage.
- **Operating Efficiency:** Asset turnover, inventory turnover, and receivables management.
- **Production Technology:** Capital-intensive vs Labour-intensive technologies.

### D. Human Resource and Managerial Factors
- **Quality of Management:** Competence of financial decision-makers.
- **Employee Skills and Productivity:** Affects operational efficiency and costs.
- **Corporate Governance:** Board composition, audit quality, transparency standards.

## 2.2 Classification of External Factors

External factors operate at the macro-environmental level and are largely uncontrollable. They are systematically analyzed using frameworks like **PESTEL** and **SWOT**.

### A. Economic Factors (Most Critical)
- **Inflation Rate:** Erodes purchasing power and real returns.
- **Interest Rates:** Affect cost of capital and investment decisions.
- **Exchange Rates:** Impact import/export and foreign investments.
- **Economic Growth (GDP):** Influences demand and market size.
- **Tax Policies and Government Spending:** Direct financial implications.

### B. Political and Legal Factors
- **Political Stability:** Affects investor confidence.
- **Government Regulations:** SEBI, RBI, Companies Act, FEMA guidelines.
- **Trade Policies:** Tariffs, import duties, free trade agreements.
- **Legal Framework:** Contract laws, labour laws, environmental laws.

### C. Technological Factors
- **Rate of Technological Change:** Affects product obsolescence.
- **R&D Capabilities:** Determines innovation pipeline.
- **Automation and Digitalization:** Alters cost structures.
- **Cybersecurity Risks:** Modern financial vulnerability.

### D. Socio-Cultural Factors
- **Consumer Preferences:** Drive product design and marketing spend.
- **Demographic Shifts:** Workforce availability and market segments.
- **Ethical Investing Trends:** ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) considerations.

### E. Environmental Factors
- **Climate Change Risks:** Carbon taxes, sustainability mandates.
- **Resource Scarcity:** Raw material costs and availability.
- **Waste Management Regulations:** Compliance costs.

### F. Competitive Factors
- **Industry Competition:** Porter's Five Forces impact pricing power.
- **Market Position:** Market share influences bargaining power.
- **New Entrants and Substitutes:** Threat to revenue streams.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description | KTU Application |
|---------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Wealth Maximization** | $W = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Present value of future cash flows minus initial cost | Primary financial goal |
| **Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = w_d k_d (1-t) + w_e k_e$ | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | Discount rate for projects |
| **Operating Leverage (DOL)** | $DOL = \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F}$ | Measures sensitivity of EBIT to sales | Risk analysis |
| **Financial Leverage (DFL)** | $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$ | Sensitivity of EPS to EBIT changes | Capital structure decisions |
| **Combined Leverage (DCL)** | $DCL = DOL \times DFL$ | Combined business and financial risk | Total firm risk |
| **Current Ratio** | $CR = \frac{Current\ Assets}{Current\ Liabilities}$ | Short-term liquidity measure | Working capital analysis |
| **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E = \frac{Total\ Debt}{Total\ Equity}$ | Capital structure indicator | Financial risk assessment |
| **Return on Equity (ROE)** | $ROE = \frac{Net\ Income}{Shareholders'\ Equity}$ | Profitability from equity perspective | Performance evaluation |
| **Inflation Adjustment** | $Real\ Rate = \frac{1+Nominal}{1+Inflation} - 1$ | Fisher equation for real returns | External factor adjustment |
| **Break-Even Point** | $BEP = \frac{Fixed\ Costs}{Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost}$ | Units needed to cover costs | Internal cost planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Leverage Formulas:** All leverage formulas assume linear cost behaviour, constant selling price, and independent interest/fixed cost values. They break down during extreme sales volatility or multiple product scenarios.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

**Internal factors analysis** helps engineers in:
- Selecting optimal technology investments (capex decisions)
- Managing project budgets effectively
- Determining make-or-buy decisions
- Setting R&D allocation priorities

**External factors analysis** helps engineers in:
- Forecasting demand for new products
- Compliance with environmental and safety regulations
- Strategic location decisions for manufacturing plants
- International market entry decisions for engineering firms

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, always cite at least **two internal and two external factors** with engineering industry examples (e.g., L&T, Tata Steel, ISRO, BHEL) to score full marks on application-based questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Leverage Formula

The Combined Leverage (DCL) measures the total risk faced by a firm due to both its operating cost structure and its financial capital structure. It is derived from the fundamental definitions of DOL and DFL.

### Step 1: Define Operating Leverage (DOL)

Operating leverage is the ratio of percentage change in Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT) to the percentage change in Sales (Q).

$$DOL = \frac{\%\ \Delta\ EBIT}{\%\ \Delta\ Sales}$$

### Step 2: Express DOL in Terms of Fundamental Variables

Let:
- $S$ = Sales Revenue
- $V$ = Variable Cost per unit $\times$ Quantity Sold
- $F$ = Fixed Operating Costs
- $Q$ = Quantity Sold

Then:
- Contribution Margin $= S - V$
- $EBIT = S - V - F = Q(S-V) - F$

For a marginal change in quantity $Q$:

$$\%\ \Delta\ EBIT = \frac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT} = \frac{(S-V)\ \Delta Q}{Q(S-V)-F}$$

$$\%\ \Delta\ Sales = \frac{\Delta S}{S} = \frac{\Delta Q \cdot P}{Q \cdot P} = \frac{\Delta Q}{Q}$$

Therefore:

$$DOL = \frac{\frac{(S-V)\ \Delta Q}{Q(S-V)-F}}{\frac{\Delta Q}{Q}}$$

$$DOL = \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F}$$

### Step 3: Define Financial Leverage (DFL)

$$DFL = \frac{\%\ \Delta\ EPS}{\%\ \Delta\ EBIT}$$

Where:
- $EPS$ = Earnings Per Share
- $I$ = Interest on Debt
- $T$ = Tax Rate
- $N$ = Number of Equity Shares

$$EBT = EBIT - I$$

$$EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-T)}{N}$$

For marginal change:

$$\%\ \Delta\ EPS = \frac{(1-T)\ \Delta EBIT}{N \cdot EPS} = \frac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT - I}$$

$$\%\ \Delta\ EBIT = \frac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT}$$

Therefore:

$$DFL = \frac{\frac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT-I}}{\frac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT}}$$

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$$

### Step 4: Combine to Derive DCL

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL$$

$$\begin{aligned}
DCL &= \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F} \times \frac{EBIT}{EBIT-I} \\
&= \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F} \times \frac{Q(S-V)-F}{Q(S-V)-F-I} \\
&= \frac{Q(S-V)}{Q(S-V)-F-I}
\end{aligned}$$

The **numerator** $Q(S-V)$ represents the Contribution Margin, and the **denominator** $Q(S-V)-F-I$ represents the Earnings Before Tax (EBT). Hence DCL can also be expressed as:

$$DCL = \frac{Contribution\ Margin}{EBT} = \frac{\%\ \Delta\ EPS}{\%\ \Delta\ Sales}$$

### Step 5: Verification with Numerical Example

Given: $S = \text{Rs. } 10{,}00{,}000$, $V = \text{Rs. } 6{,}00{,}000$, $F = \text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000$, $I = \text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000$

$$\begin{aligned}
EBIT &= 10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000 \\
EBT &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000 \\
DOL &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.0 \\
DFL &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 2.0 \\
DCL &= 2.0 \times 2.0 = 4.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1% change in sales will cause a 4% change in EPS. This is the total combined risk exposure of the firm.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Internal vs External Factors

The following matrix systematically contrasts the two factor categories across multiple analytical dimensions:

| Dimension | Internal Factors | External Factors |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Origin** | Within the firm | Outside the firm |
| **Controllability** | Largely controllable | Mostly uncontrollable |
| **Scope** | Micro-environmental | Macro-environmental |
| **Time Horizon Impact** | Short to medium term | Medium to long term |
| **Examples** | Capital structure, management quality, cost structure | Inflation, regulations, technology shifts |
| **Analysis Framework** | Value Chain Analysis, VRIO, SWOT (Internal) | PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces |
| **Data Source** | Internal accounting records, MIS reports | Government publications, industry reports |
| **Decision Authority** | Management / Board of Directors | Government, Markets, International Bodies |
| **Risk Category** | Business Risk, Financial Risk | Systematic Risk, Market Risk |
| **Mitigation Strategy** | Strategic planning, operational excellence | Hedging, diversification, scenario planning |
| **KTU Module 1 Weightage** | ~50% syllabus emphasis | ~50% syllabus emphasis |

## 3.3 PESTEL Analytical Framework Derivation

The **PESTEL** framework emerged from the evolution of environmental scanning models:

$$\text{ETPS} \rightarrow \text{PEST} \rightarrow \text{PESTEL} \rightarrow \text{PESTLE-STEEP}$$

Where each expansion represented academic recognition of additional external force categories:

$$\text{External\ Factor\ Coverage} = \{P, E, S, T, En, L\}$$

- **P** = Political forces
- **E** = Economic forces
- **S** = Social forces
- **T** = Technological forces
- **En** = Environmental forces
- **L** = Legal forces

The **SWOT** framework integrates both internal and external analysis:

$$\text{Strategic\ Position} = f(\text{Strengths},\ \text{Weaknesses},\ \text{Opportunities},\ \text{Threats})$$

Where Strengths and Weaknesses are **internal**, while Opportunities and Threats are **external**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark answers, structure SWOT tables as a 2×2 matrix and explicitly label each quadrant as "Internal" or "External" to demonstrate conceptual clarity and earn full analytical marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Internal-External Factor Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Financial Management Decision System]:::core
    nodeB[Internal Factor Module]:::internal
    nodeC[External Factor Module]:::external
    nodeD[Strategic Output: Wealth Maximization]:::output

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD

    subgraph INTERNAL_BLOCK[Internal Factors Within Firm]
        nodeB1[Capital Structure and Debt Equity Mix]:::internal
        nodeB2[Working Capital Management]:::internal
        nodeB3[Operating Cost Structure]:::internal
        nodeB4[Management Quality and Governance]:::internal
        nodeB5[Firm Size and Life Cycle Stage]:::internal
    end

    subgraph EXTERNAL_BLOCK[External Factors Outside Firm]

        nodeC5[Environmental Sustainability Pressures]:::external
        nodeC6[Competitive Industry Dynamics]:::external
    end

    nodeB --> INTERNAL_BLOCK
    nodeC --> EXTERNAL_BLOCK

    INTERNAL_BLOCK --> nodeE[Synthesis Layer: PESTEL plus SWOT plus Leverage Analysis]:::synthesis
    EXTERNAL_BLOCK --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeD

    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef internal fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Factor Classification Tree

```mermaid

    rootA --> int1[Internal Factors]:::internal
    rootA --> ext1[External Factors]:::external

    int1 --> iB[Financial Factors]
    int1 --> iC[Operational Factors]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow Diagram: Factor Impact on Financial Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Identify Decision Context]:::step
    stepA --> stepB{Is factor Internal or External?}:::decision
    stepB -->|Internal| stepC[Apply Value Chain or VRIO Analysis]:::internal
    stepB -->|External| stepD[Apply PESTEL Framework]:::external

    stepC --> stepE[Quantify using Leverage Ratios]:::internal
    stepE --> stepF[Compute DOL DFL DCL]:::internal
    stepF --> stepG[Internal Decision Matrix]:::step

    stepD --> stepH[Scenario Analysis and Sensitivity]:::external
    stepH --> stepI[Stress Test Financial Outcomes]:::external
    stepI --> stepJ[External Risk Assessment Matrix]:::step

    stepG --> stepK[Integrate via SWOT Synthesis]:::synthesis

    stepK --> stepL[Final Financial Decision: Invest, Reject, Defer]:::output

    classDef step fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef output fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description for Complex Visual Concepts

For engineering students, the interaction between internal and external factors can be visualized as a **Force Field Analysis**:

- **Driving Forces (positive):** Internal strengths + External opportunities → Push toward goal achievement
- **Restraining Forces (negative):** Internal weaknesses + External threats → Push against goal achievement

**Equilibrium Point** represents the current financial position of the firm. Financial managers aim to **increase driving forces** and **reduce restraining forces** to shift equilibrium toward wealth maximization.

$$\text{Strategic\ Equilibrium\ Shift} = \frac{\sum Driving\ Forces}{\sum Restraining\ Forces} \rightarrow \text{Maximize}$$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Financial Management for Engineers. State its primary objective.**

**Model Answer:**

Financial Management for Engineers is the discipline concerned with the planning, procurement, allocation, and control of financial resources within engineering projects and technology-driven organizations. It integrates financial principles with engineering decision-making processes.

The **primary objective** of financial management is **Wealth Maximization**, which means maximizing the market value of shareholders' equity over the long term. This objective considers the time value of money, risk, and sustainability, unlike the narrower objective of profit maximization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Primary Objective with justification (1.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between internal and external factors influencing financial management with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Internal Factors | External Factors |
|-------|------------------|------------------|
| Origin | Originate within the organization | Originate outside the organization |
| Controllability | Largely controllable by management | Beyond management's control |
| Examples | (i) Capital structure, (ii) Management quality | (i) Inflation rate, (ii) Government regulations |

Internal factors are firm-specific conditions such as **capital structure decisions** and **managerial competence** that management can directly influence.

External factors are environmental conditions such as **inflation rates** and **government tax policies** that the firm must adapt to but cannot control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Clear differentiation table (2 marks) + One example each (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **internal factors** that influence financial management decisions in an engineering organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable **SWOT analysis framework**, demonstrate how internal and external factors collectively impact financial decision-making for an Indian engineering firm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):** Internal factors are firm-specific variables that directly affect financial management. They originate within the organization and can be controlled to varying degrees by management.

**Detailed Explanation (6 marks):**

1. **Capital Structure Decisions:** The mix of debt and equity determines the firm's financial risk. A higher debt ratio increases financial leverage and tax shield benefits but also increases bankruptcy risk.

2. **Working Capital Management:** Efficient management of current assets (cash, receivables, inventory) and current liabilities determines short-term liquidity and operational smoothness.

3. **Operating Cost Structure:** The proportion of fixed costs to variable costs determines the firm's operating leverage. Capital-intensive engineering firms have higher fixed costs and operating risk.

4. **Management Quality and Corporate Governance:** Skilled financial managers and transparent governance structures lead to better investment decisions and stakeholder confidence.

5. **Firm Size and Life Cycle Stage:** Start-ups, growth firms, mature firms, and declining firms each require different financial strategies regarding dividend policy, capital raising, and reinvestment.

6. **Dividend Policy:** The decision to distribute profits versus retain earnings directly affects internal financing capacity and shareholder satisfaction.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**SWOT Analysis for an Indian Engineering Firm (e.g., Larsen & Toubro):**

| Category | Factor Type | Description |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Strengths** (Internal +) | Internal | Strong brand reputation, diversified portfolio, skilled workforce |
| **Weaknesses** (Internal −) | Internal | High dependency on government projects, working capital intensive |
| **Opportunities** (External +) | External | Smart cities mission, infrastructure boom, Make in India initiative |
| **Threats** (External −) | External | Raw material price volatility, foreign exchange risk, intense competition |

**Integration with Financial Decision-Making (3 marks):**

- **Strengths + Opportunities** → Aggressive expansion and R&D investment strategies
- **Weaknesses + Threats** → Defensive financial restructuring and cost optimization
- **Strengths + Threats** → Diversification of revenue streams
- **Weaknesses + Opportunities** → Strategic partnerships and joint ventures

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2-3 marks by (1) failing to clearly label SWOT categories as Internal or External, (2) not providing industry-specific examples, and (3) omitting the integration step that links SWOT outcomes to actual financial decisions. Always conclude with specific financial action recommendations.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **PESTEL framework** for analyzing external factors affecting engineering firms. Illustrate with at least three examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An engineering firm has the following data: Sales = Rs. 50,00,000, Variable Costs = Rs. 30,00,000, Fixed Costs = Rs. 10,00,000, Interest = Rs. 4,00,000. Calculate **DOL, DFL, and DCL**, and interpret the results in terms of internal and external risk exposure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**PESTEL Framework Analysis (7 marks):**

The PESTEL framework systematically analyzes six categories of external factors:

1. **Political Factors (1 mark):** Government stability, trade policies, and political ideology directly affect engineering firms through project approvals, defence contracts, and infrastructure spending. Example: Changes in government infrastructure budgets impact order books for L&T and BHEL.

2. **Economic Factors (1 mark):** Inflation, interest rates, GDP growth, and exchange rates are the most critical external factors. Example: A rise in RBI repo rates increases the cost of capital for engineering firms borrowing for expansion projects.

3. **Social Factors (1 mark):** Demographic changes, consumer preferences, and lifestyle shifts influence demand patterns. Example: Growing urbanisation drives demand for smart housing, metro systems, and sustainable infrastructure.

4. **Technological Factors (1 mark):** Industry 4.0, automation, AI, and IoT disruption reshape competitive landscapes. Example: Automation in manufacturing reduces labour costs but requires significant capex investment decisions.

5. **Environmental Factors (1 mark):** Climate change regulations, carbon taxes, and sustainability mandates. Example: New emission norms compel automobile and heavy engineering firms to redesign products and invest in green technology.

6. **Legal Factors (1 mark):** Companies Act, SEBI regulations, FEMA guidelines, labour laws, and contract laws. Example: SEBI's revised ESG disclosure norms mandate transparent reporting, affecting engineering firms' compliance costs.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The PESTEL framework provides a comprehensive macro-environmental scanning tool, enabling engineering firms to anticipate external risks and align financial strategies accordingly.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Sales ($S$) = Rs. 50,00,000
- Variable Costs ($V$) = Rs. 30,00,000
- Fixed Costs ($F$) = Rs. 10,00,000
- Interest ($I$) = Rs. 4,00,000

**Step 1: Calculate Contribution Margin (1 mark)**

$$Contribution\ Margin = S - V = 50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 20{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Calculate EBIT and EBT (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
EBIT &= S - V - F = 20{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 10{,}00{,}000 \\
EBT &= EBIT - I = 10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Operating Leverage DOL (1 mark)**

$$DOL = \frac{Contribution\ Margin}{EBIT} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 2.0$$

**Step 4: Calculate Financial Leverage DFL (1 mark)**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 1.667$$

**Step 5: Calculate Combined Leverage DCL (1 mark)**

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL = 2.0 \times 1.667 = 3.333$$

**Step 6: Interpretation in Terms of Risk Exposure (2 marks)**

- **DOL = 2.0:** A 1% change in sales causes a 2% change in EBIT. This indicates **moderate operating risk** due to the presence of significant fixed costs (Rs. 10,00,000). This is an **internal factor** — the firm controls its cost structure and technology choices.

- **DFL = 1.667:** A 1% change in EBIT causes a 1.667% change in EPS. This indicates **moderate financial risk** due to the interest burden of Rs. 4,00,000. This reflects the **capital structure decision** — an internal factor.

- **DCL = 3.333:** A 1% change in sales causes a 3.333% change in EPS, demonstrating **amplified total risk**. External factors like demand fluctuations and interest rate changes (external factor) would multiply their impact through these leverage effects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes in leverage calculations include (1) forgetting to subtract fixed costs before computing EBIT, (2) using EBT instead of EBIT in the DOL formula, and (3) not interpreting the final values in terms of risk. Always end leverage problems with a clear risk interpretation paragraph referencing both internal and external factors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- [x] **Financial Management** = Planning, organizing, directing, controlling financial resources in engineering organizations.
- [x] **Primary Objective** = Wealth Maximization (not profit maximization) — this is KTU's preferred framework.
- [x] **Internal Factors** include: Capital structure, working capital, cost structure, management quality, firm size, life cycle stage, dividend policy.
- [x] **External Factors** include: Economic (inflation, interest, GDP, exchange rate), Political, Legal, Technological, Socio-cultural, Environmental, Competitive.
- [x] **PESTEL** is the standard framework for external analysis (P-P, E-E, S-S, T-T, En-E, L-L).
- [x] **SWOT** integrates both internal (S-W) and external (O-T) factors for strategic synthesis.
- [x] **DOL** = $Q(S-V) / [Q(S-V) - F]$ — measures operating risk (internal).
- [x] **DFL** = $EBIT / (EBIT - I)$ — measures financial risk (internal capital structure).
- [x] **DCL** = $DOL \times DFL$ — measures total firm risk.
- [x] **WACC** = $w_d k_d (1-t) + w_e k_e$ — used as discount rate in investment decisions.
- [x] **Real Rate (Fisher)** = $(1+Nominal)/(1+Inflation) - 1$ — adjusts for external inflation impact.
- [x] Always cite **engineering industry examples** (L&T, BHEL, ISRO, Tata Steel) to score application marks.
- [x] In exam answers, **separate internal and external factors** into distinct sections/paragraphs for clarity.
- [x] Conclude with **risk mitigation recommendations** specific to the engineering context.
- [x] Use **headings, sub-points, and tables** — KTU examiners reward well-structured answers with high marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for External Factors: PESTEL** → *Please Eat Sour Tasty Mangoes Late* (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Statements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Financial Statements

# 📘 MODULE 2 — FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Statements** are structured, formal, written records that quantify the **financial strength, performance, and liquidity** of an organization. They present the **financial position** of a business entity at a specific point in time and the **operating results** over a period of time, prepared in accordance with **Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)** or **Ind AS / IFRS** as applicable.

For KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2), the formal definition per the syllabus reads:

> *"Financial statements are the end-products of the accounting process. They provide a summarized view of the financial affairs of a business and include the Income Statement (Trading & P&L A/c), the Position Statement (Balance Sheet), and supporting schedules & notes to accounts."*

The four primary financial statements recognized globally and expected in KTU examinations are:

| # | Statement | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Income Statement** (Profit & Loss A/c) | Measures profitability over a period |
| 2 | **Balance Sheet** (Position Statement) | Shows financial position at a point in time |
| 3 | **Cash Flow Statement** | Tracks cash inflows & outflows |
| 4 | **Statement of Changes in Equity** | Reconciles opening & closing owner's equity |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Report Card" 🩺

Imagine a human body undergoing an annual medical check-up.

- The **Balance Sheet** is like a **snapshot photograph** 📸 — "What does the patient own (heart, lungs) and what does he owe (loans, bills) right now?"
- The **Income Statement** is the **fitness diary** 🏃 — "Over the past year, how much did the patient earn (salary) versus how much did he spend (food, rent)?"
- The **Cash Flow Statement** is the **bank account statement** 💰 — "Where did actual cash come from, and where did it actually go?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Tip for KTU Students:** A Balance Sheet is a **still photograph** (point-in-time), while an Income Statement and Cash Flow Statement are **videos** (over a period). This is the #1 conceptual mistake students make — confusing the time dimension.

---

### 1.3 Users of Financial Statements

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Learning Outcome Mapping (CO2):**
> *Identify and analyze the components of financial statements for managerial decision-making.*

| Stakeholder | Primary Information Need |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Shareholders / Owners** | Profitability & return on investment |
| **Creditors / Banks** | Solvency & debt-servicing capacity |
| **Management** | Operational efficiency, planning |
| **Tax Authorities** | Taxable income compliance |
| **Employees / Trade Unions** | Wage-paying capacity |
| **Government / Regulators** | Compliance & macro-economic data |
| **Investors (Prospective)** | Future growth & risk assessment |

---

### 1.4 Qualities of Useful Financial Statements (GAAP Framework)

For KTU 2024 Scheme, every financial statement must satisfy these qualitative characteristics:

1. **Understandability** — Comprehensible to users with reasonable business knowledge.
2. **Relevance** — Material to decision-making.
3. **Reliability** — Free from bias & error.
4. **Comparability** — Across periods & across firms.
5. **Faithful Representation** — Complete, neutral, error-free.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the KTU 2024 NEP framework, financial statements are expected to align with **Ind AS (Indian Accounting Standards)** for Indian companies, with **Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013** prescribing the standard format.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Income Statement (Profit & Loss Account)

The Income Statement follows the matching concept — matching **revenues** of a period with the **expenses** incurred to earn them.

### 2.1.1 Structure (Vertical Format — Ind AS Compliant)

```
Revenue from Operations (Net Sales)
   (Less) Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
   ─────────────────────────────────
   = GROSS PROFIT
   (Less) Operating Expenses:
       • Selling, General & Admin Expenses
       • Depreciation & Amortization
       • Finance Costs (Interest)
   ─────────────────────────────────
   = OPERATING PROFIT (EBIT)
   (±) Non-Operating Income / Expenses
   ─────────────────────────────────
   = PROFIT BEFORE TAX (PBT)
   (Less) Tax Expense
   ─────────────────────────────────
   = PROFIT AFTER TAX (PAT) / NET PROFIT
```

### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Income Statement Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **HIGH-YIELD** — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 numerical.

| Metric | Formula | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **Gross Profit (GP)** | $\text{GP} = \text{Net Sales} - \text{COGS}$ | ₹ (Currency) |
| **Gross Profit Margin** | $\text{GP Margin} = \dfrac{\text{GP}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | $\text{EBIT} = \text{GP} - \text{Operating Expenses}$ | ₹ |
| **Operating Margin** | $\text{Op Margin} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| **Net Profit (PAT)** | $\text{PAT} = \text{PBT} - \text{Tax}$ | ₹ |
| **Net Profit Margin (NPM)** | $\text{NPM} = \dfrac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| **EBITDA** | $\text{EBIT} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Amortization}$ | ₹ |
| **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{PAT} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$ | ₹/share |

---

## 2.2 The Balance Sheet (Position Statement)

The Balance Sheet is governed by the fundamental accounting equation:

$$\boxed{\text{ASSETS} = \text{LIABILITIES} + \text{EQUITY (OWNERS' FUNDS)}}$$

### 2.2.1 Classification of Assets

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|------|------------|----------|
| **Current Assets** | Convertible to cash within **12 months** | Cash, Debtors, Inventory, Marketable Securities, Prepaid Expenses |
| **Non-Current Assets (Fixed)** | Long-term usage > 1 year | Land, Building, Plant & Machinery, Goodwill, Patents |
| **Intangible Assets** | Non-physical, long-term value | Goodwill, Trademarks, Software, Copyrights |

### 2.2.2 Classification of Liabilities & Equity

| Side | Component | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Equity (Shareholders' Funds)** | Owners' capital | Equity Share Capital, Preference Share Capital, Reserves & Surplus |
| **Non-Current Liabilities** | Long-term obligations (>1 yr) | Debentures, Long-term Loans, Bonds |
| **Current Liabilities** | Short-term obligations (≤1 yr) | Creditors, Bills Payable, Short-term Loans, Outstanding Expenses |

---

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Balance Sheet Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use |
|--------|---------|-----|
| **Working Capital (WC)** | $\text{WC} = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Short-term liquidity |
| **Net Worth** | $\text{Net Worth} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}$ | Owner's stake |
| **Debt-to-Equity Ratio** | $\text{D/E} = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | Capital structure |
| **Current Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Liquidity |
| **Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio** | $\text{QR} = \dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}}{\text{CL}}$ | Immediate liquidity |
| **Proprietary Ratio** | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Equity proportion |
| **Asset Turnover** | $\text{AT} = \dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Average Total Assets}}$ | Efficiency |

---

## 2.3 The Cash Flow Statement (CFS)

Per **Ind AS 7**, the Cash Flow Statement classifies cash movements into three activities:

### 2.3.1 The Three Activities

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. OPERATING ACTIVITIES                 │
│     → Day-to-day business transactions   │
│     → Cash from customers, paid to       │
│       suppliers & employees              │
│                                          │
│  2. INVESTING ACTIVITIES                 │
│     → Acquisition / sale of fixed assets │
│     → Purchase / sale of investments     │
│                                          │
│  3. FINANCING ACTIVITIES                 │
│     → Issue / repayment of shares & debt │
│     → Dividend payment                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula — Net Cash Position

$$\boxed{\text{Net Change in Cash} = \text{Cash from Operations} + \text{Cash from Investing} + \text{Cash from Financing}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Closing Cash} = \text{Opening Cash} + \text{Net Change in Cash}}$$

---

## 2.4 Tools of Financial Statement Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently tested section** in KTU Module 2 — board examiners LOVE these.

| Technique | What it Does | KTU Frequency |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Horizontal Analysis** (Trend Analysis) | Compares same item across multiple years | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Vertical Analysis** (Common-Size) | Each item expressed as % of a base | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Ratio Analysis** | Quantitative relationship between items | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Funds Flow Analysis** | Tracks movement of working capital | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cash Flow Analysis** | Tracks actual cash movements | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 2.4.1 KTU Formula — Common-Size Statement

For Income Statement (vertical): Each item as % of **Net Sales**.

$$\text{Common-Size \%} = \dfrac{\text{Individual Item}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$$

For Balance Sheet (vertical): Each item as % of **Total Assets / Total Liabilities**.

$$\text{Common-Size \% (Asset)} = \dfrac{\text{Asset Item}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times 100$$

---

### 2.4.2 KTU Formula — Trend Analysis (Index Number)

$$\text{Trend \%} = \dfrac{\text{Current Year Value}}{\text{Base Year Value}} \times 100$$

> Base year is always assigned an index of **100**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For engineering managers, financial statements serve critical operational purposes:

1. **Project Appraisal** — NPV & IRR calculations depend on forecasted cash flows derived from financial statements.
2. **Make-or-Buy Decisions** — Cost statements inform outsourcing.
3. **Capital Budgeting** — Free cash flow estimates drive machinery purchase decisions.
4. **Working Capital Management** — Liquidity ratios guide inventory & receivables policy.
5. **Cost Control** — Variance analysis uses actual vs. standard cost statements.

---
