# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Preparation of Income Statement (Trading & P&L)

> **KTU Past Pattern:** *"From the following Trial Balance and adjustments, prepare the Trading and Profit & Loss Account for the year ended 31-03-2024 and the Balance Sheet as on that date."*

### 3.1.1 Trial Balance of M/s Tech Engineering Ltd. (₹ in '000)

| Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|-------------|----------:|-----------:|
| Opening Stock | 50,000 | — |
| Purchases | 3,00,000 | — |
| Sales | — | 5,00,000 |
| Sales Returns | 20,000 | — |
| Purchase Returns | — | 10,000 |
| Wages & Salaries | 80,000 | — |
| Rent, Rates & Taxes | 24,000 | — |
| Office Expenses | 16,000 | — |
| Advertisement | 10,000 | — |
| Debtors & Creditors | 1,00,000 | 60,000 |
| Land & Building | 2,00,000 | — |
| Plant & Machinery | 1,50,000 | — |
| Furniture | 30,000 | — |
| Cash at Bank | 40,000 | — |
| Capital | — | 4,00,000 |
| 12% Long-term Loan | — | 1,50,000 |
| **Total** | **10,20,000** | **10,20,000** |

**Adjustments:**
1. Closing Stock ₹ 60,000.
2. Depreciation: Building 5%, Plant & Machinery 10%, Furniture 10%.
3. Outstanding Wages ₹ 5,000; Prepaid Rent ₹ 2,000.
4. Bad Debts provision: ₹ 3,000.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution — Trading Account

**Step 1: Compute Net Purchases**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Purchases} &= \text{Purchases} - \text{Purchase Returns} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Net Sales**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Sales} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Sales Returns} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= 4{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Gross Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Net Sales} + \text{Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock} - \text{Net Purchases} - \text{Direct Wages} \\
&= 4{,}80{,}000 + 60{,}000 - 50{,}000 - 2{,}90{,}000 - 80{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution — Profit & Loss Account

**Step 1: Adjust Indirect Expenses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wages (already direct, ignore in P&L)} \\
\text{Rent, Rates \& Taxes} &= 24{,}000 - 2{,}000 \text{ (Prepaid)} = 22{,}000 \\
\text{Office Expenses} &= 16{,}000 \\
\text{Advertisement} &= 10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Depreciation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation on Building} &= 5\% \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}000 \\
\text{Depreciation on Plant \& Machinery} &= 10\% \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 15{,}000 \\
\text{Depreciation on Furniture} &= 10\% \times 30{,}000 = 3{,}000 \\
\text{Total Depreciation} &= 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 28{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PBT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBT} &= \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 1{,}20{,}000 - (22{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 10{,}000) - 28{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}20{,}000 - 48{,}000 - 28{,}000 \\
&= 44{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Solution — Balance Sheet

**Step 1: Total Assets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Assets (NBV)} &= (2{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000) + (1{,}50{,}000 - 15{,}000) + (30{,}000 - 3{,}000) \\
&= 1{,}90{,}000 + 1{,}35{,}000 + 27{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}52{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Assets} &= \text{Closing Stock} + \text{Debtors (net)} + \text{Cash} + \text{Prepaid Rent} \\
&= 60{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}000) + 40{,}000 + 2{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}99{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Assets} = 3{,}52{,}000 + 1{,}99{,}000 = 5{,}51{,}000}
$$

**Step 2: Total Liabilities & Equity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Add: Net Profit (PBT)} &= 44{,}000 \\
\text{Closing Capital} &= 4{,}44{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{12\% Long-term Loan} &= 1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Current Liabilities} &= \text{Creditors} + \text{Outstanding Wages} \\
&= 60{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 65{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Liabilities \& Equity} = 4{,}44{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 65{,}000 = 5{,}51{,}000}
$$

> ✅ **Verification:** Total Assets (₹5,51,000) = Total Liabilities + Equity (₹5,51,000). The **accounting equation balances**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Vertical (Common-Size) Analysis

| Item | Year 1 (₹) | Year 2 (₹) | Y1 (%) | Y2 (%) |
|------|----------:|----------:|-------:|-------:|
| Net Sales | 5,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 100.00 | 100.00 |
| COGS | 3,00,000 | 3,30,000 | 60.00 | 55.00 |
| **Gross Profit** | **2,00,000** | **2,70,000** | **40.00** | **45.00** |
| Operating Expenses | 80,000 | 1,00,000 | 16.00 | 16.67 |
| Depreciation | 20,000 | 25,000 | 4.00 | 4.17 |
| **Operating Profit** | **1,00,000** | **1,45,000** | **20.00** | **24.17** |
| Interest | 20,000 | 20,000 | 4.00 | 3.33 |
| **PBT** | **80,000** | **1,25,000** | **16.00** | **20.83** |
| Tax (30%) | 24,000 | 37,500 | 4.80 | 6.25 |
| **PAT** | **56,000** | **87,500** | **11.20** | **14.58** |

**Analytical Inference:**
- GP margin **improved** from 40% → 45% (cost control effective).
- PAT margin **improved** from 11.2% → 14.58% (overall efficiency up).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Horizontal (Trend) Analysis

Base Year (Year 0) = Index 100.

| Item | Y0 (₹) | Y1 (₹) | Y2 (₹) | Y0 Idx | Y1 Idx | Y2 Idx |
|------|-------:|-------:|-------:|-------:|-------:|-------:|
| Net Sales | 4,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 100 | 125 | 150 |
| PAT | 40,000 | 56,000 | 87,500 | 100 | 140 | 218.75 |
| Total Assets | 4,00,000 | 4,80,000 | 5,51,000 | 100 | 120 | 137.75 |

**Inference:**
- Sales grew at **25% YoY** in Y1, **20% YoY** in Y2.
- PAT grew at **40% YoY** in Y1, **56% YoY** in Y2 — **PAT grows faster than Sales** = positive operating leverage.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define financial statements. List any four components of financial statements as per Ind AS.**

**Model Answer:**
Financial statements are formal, structured records that present the financial position and performance of an entity. They are prepared in accordance with Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) and Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013.

The four components are:
1. **Income Statement (Statement of Profit & Loss)**
2. **Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)**
3. **Cash Flow Statement**
4. **Statement of Changes in Equity**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Horizontal Analysis and Vertical Analysis of financial statements.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Horizontal Analysis | Vertical Analysis |
|-------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Reference Base** | Compares same item across multiple years | Compares each item with a base year within the same year |
| **Time Element** | Inter-period comparison (trend) | Intra-period comparison (% of total) |
| **Also Known As** | Trend Analysis / Dynamic Analysis | Common-Size Statement / Static Analysis |
| **Unit of Expression** | Absolute values or Index Numbers | Percentages of a common base |
| **Useful For** | Identifying growth / decline patterns | Studying internal composition of statements |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Prepare a Trading and Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet from the following Trial Balance of M/s Apex Engineers as on 31-03-2024. **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | Dr. (₹) | Cr. (₹) |
|-------------|--------:|--------:|
| Opening Stock | 40,000 | — |
| Purchases & Sales | 2,80,000 | 4,50,000 |
| Returns (Inward/Outward) | 15,000 | 8,000 |
| Wages | 60,000 | — |
| Salaries | 36,000 | — |
| Rent & Insurance | 18,000 | — |
| Sundry Debtors & Creditors | 80,000 | 45,000 |
| Land & Building | 1,80,000 | — |
| Machinery | 1,20,000 | — |
| Furniture | 20,000 | — |
| Cash in Hand | 10,000 | — |
| Bank Overdraft | — | 25,000 |
| Capital | — | 2,50,000 |
| 10% Loan | — | 60,000 |
| Bad Debts | 2,000 | — |
| **Total** | **8,61,000** | **8,38,000** |

*Note: Trial Balance mismatch of ₹23,000 represents closing stock to be inserted in Trial Balance.*

**Adjustments:**
1. Closing Stock ₹ 55,000.
2. Depreciation: Building 5%, Machinery 10%, Furniture 10%.
3. Outstanding Salaries ₹ 4,000.
4. Write off further Bad Debts ₹ 3,000; Create RDD @ 5% on debtors.

---

#### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(i) Compute Net Sales and Net Purchases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Sales} &= 4{,}50{,}000 - 15{,}000 = 4{,}35{,}000 \\
\text{Net Purchases} &= 2{,}80{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 2{,}72{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Trading Account (extract):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To Opening Stock} &= 40{,}000 \\
\text{To Net Purchases} &= 2{,}72{,}000 \\
\text{To Wages} &= 60{,}000 \\
\text{By Net Sales} &= 4{,}35{,}000 \\
\text{By Closing Stock} &= 55{,}000 \\
\text{Gross Profit} &= (4{,}35{,}000 + 55{,}000) - (40{,}000 + 2{,}72{,}000 + 60{,}000) \\
&= 4{,}90{,}000 - 3{,}72{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}18{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) P&L Account (extract):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Salaries} &= 36{,}000 + 4{,}000 \text{ (O/S)} = 40{,}000 \\
\text{Rent \& Insurance} &= 18{,}000 \\
\text{Bad Debts (old + new)} &= 2{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 5{,}000 \\
\text{RDD} &= 5\% \times (80{,}000 - 3{,}000) = 3{,}850 \\
\text{Depreciation} &= (5\% \times 1{,}80{,}000) + (10\% \times 1{,}20{,}000) + (10\% \times 20{,}000) \\
&= 9{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 23{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBT} &= 1{,}18{,}000 - (40{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 3{,}850 + 23{,}000) \\
&= 1{,}18{,}000 - 89{,}850 = 28{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** From the above financial statements, calculate: **(i) Gross Profit Margin, (ii) Net Profit Margin, (iii) Current Ratio, (iv) Debt-Equity Ratio. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Current Assets = Closing Stock + Debtors (net) + Cash**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Debtors (net)} &= 80{,}000 - 3{,}000 \text{ (further B/D)} - 3{,}850 \text{ (RDD)} = 73{,}150 \\
\text{Current Assets} &= 55{,}000 + 73{,}150 + 10{,}000 = 1{,}38{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Current Liabilities = Creditors + Outstanding Salaries + Bank OD**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Liabilities} &= 45{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 74{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Debt = 10% Loan + Bank OD**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Debt} &= 60{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 85{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equity = Capital + Profit (assumed no drawings)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equity} &= 2{,}50{,}000 + 28{,}150 = 2{,}78{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

**The four ratios:**

$$
\text{GP Margin} = \frac{1{,}18{,}000}{4{,}35{,}000} \times 100 = 27.13\%
$$

$$
\text{NP Margin} = \frac{28{,}150}{4{,}35{,}000} \times 100 = 6.47\%
$$

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{1{,}38{,}150}{74{,}000} = 1.87 : 1
$$

$$
\text{D/E Ratio} = \frac{85{,}000}{2{,}78{,}150} = 0.306
$$

**Interpretation:**
- GP Margin of 27% is **healthy** for an engineering firm (industry avg ~20-25%).
- Current Ratio of 1.87 indicates **adequate** short-term liquidity (ideal = 2:1).
- D/E of 0.31 indicates **conservative** capital structure — low financial risk.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Vertical (Common-Size) Analysis** of financial statements with a suitable format. Mention any four advantages and two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Vertical Analysis is a technique in which each item in a financial statement is expressed as a percentage of a base value. For the Income Statement, **Net Sales = 100%**. For the Balance Sheet, **Total Assets or Total Liabilities & Equity = 100%**.

**Format — Common-Size Income Statement:**

| Item | ₹ | Common-Size (%) |
|------|---:|----------------:|
| Net Sales | 5,00,000 | 100.00 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 3,00,000 | 60.00 |
| **Gross Profit** | **2,00,000** | **40.00** |
| Operating Expenses | 80,000 | 16.00 |
| Depreciation | 20,000 | 4.00 |
| **Operating Profit** | **1,00,000** | **20.00** |
| Interest | 20,000 | 4.00 |
| Tax | 24,000 | 4.80 |
| **Net Profit** | **56,000** | **11.20** |

**Advantages:**
1. **Easy Inter-firm Comparison** — Removes the effect of size differences.
2. **Reveals Cost Structure** — Shows proportion of each expense to revenue.
3. **Useful for Trend Analysis** — When applied across years.
4. **Detects Abnormal Items** — Unusual expenses stand out as large %.

**Limitations:**
1. **Ignores Absolute Values** — A small firm may show 50% margin but low absolute profit.
2. **Requires Industry Benchmarks** — Without peer data, percentages lack context.

**(b)** The following data relates to ABC Industries Ltd.: **(7 Marks)**

| Year | Sales (₹) | Net Profit (₹) |
|------|----------:|---------------:|
| 2020 | 4,00,000 | 32,000 |
| 2021 | 5,00,000 | 45,000 |
| 2022 | 6,00,000 | 66,000 |
| 2023 | 7,00,000 | 91,000 |

**Prepare a Trend Analysis taking 2020 as base year and comment on the results.**

#### **Solution:**

**Formula:**
$$
\text{Trend \%} = \frac{\text{Current Year Value}}{\text{Base Year (2020) Value}} \times 100
$$

| Year | Sales (₹) | Trend % | NP (₹) | Trend % |
|------|----------:|--------:|-------:|--------:|
| 2020 | 4,00,000 | **100.00** | 32,000 | **100.00** |
| 2021 | 5,00,000 | 125.00 | 45,000 | 140.63 |
| 2022 | 6,00,000 | 150.00 | 66,000 | 206.25 |
| 2023 | 7,00,000 | 175.00 | 91,000 | 284.38 |

**Comments / Inference:**
- **Sales** grew at a steady **25% per annum** — consistent top-line growth.
- **Net Profit** grew faster than sales — **140.63% → 284.38%** indicates excellent cost control and operating leverage.
- **Profitability is improving disproportionately** — a healthy sign for an engineering firm.
- The firm is in a **growth phase** with increasing economies of scale.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 2 — Examiner's Insider View:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting "Outstanding" & "Prepaid" Adjustments** — Loses 1-2 marks silently. Always re-read the adjustments twice.
> 2. **Net Sales vs Gross Sales Confusion** — Sales Returns MUST be deducted. Same for Purchase Returns.
> 3. **Depreciation Calculation Errors** — Apply % on ORIGINAL cost, not on opening WDV. KTU paper-setters specifically test this.
> 4. **Balance Sheet not Balancing** — Indicates arithmetic error. Always cross-verify Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Equity.
> 5. **Closing Stock Misplaced** — Should appear in BOTH Trading Account (Credit side) AND Balance Sheet (Asset side).
> 6. **RDD Calculation** — Apply RDD on debtors AFTER deducting further bad debts. Many students apply on the original balance.
> 7. **Trend Analysis: Base Year** — Always assign base year = **100**, not 1. KTU expects the index convention.
> 8. **Currency Units** — If Trial Balance is in ₹ '000, your final answer must reflect the same unit. Add a header note.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Financial Statements**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- Financial Statements = **Income Statement + Balance Sheet + Cash Flow + SOCE**
- Governing Equation: **Assets = Liabilities + Equity**
- Balance Sheet is **point-in-time**; Income/Cash Flow are **period-based**.

### 📌 Income Statement (P&L)
- Net Sales − Net Purchases − Direct Expenses = **Gross Profit**
- GP − Indirect Expenses − Depreciation − Interest = **PBT**
- PBT − Tax = **PAT (Net Profit)**

### 📌 Balance Sheet
- **Current Assets** = convert to cash in ≤ 12 months.
- **Current Liabilities** = due in ≤ 12 months.
- **Working Capital** = Current Assets − Current Liabilities.

### 📌 Cash Flow Statement (Ind AS 7)
- **Operating Activities** → Day-to-day operations
- **Investing Activities** → Fixed assets & long-term investments
- **Financing Activities** → Capital structure (debt + equity)

### 📌 Analysis Tools
- **Horizontal (Trend)** — Index Number method, Base year = 100
- **Vertical (Common-Size)** — % of Net Sales (P&L) or Total Assets (BS)
- **Ratio Analysis** — Liquidity, Profitability, Solvency, Turnover

### 📌 High-Yield Ratios (Must Memorize)
- **Current Ratio** = CA / CL → Ideal = 2:1
- **Quick Ratio** = (CA − Inventory − Prepaid) / CL → Ideal = 1:1
- **Debt-Equity** = Total Debt / Equity → Lower is safer
- **GP Margin** = GP / Net Sales × 100
- **NP Margin** = NP / Net Sales × 100

### 📌 Common Adjustments to Watch
- Closing Stock (both Trading & BS)
- Outstanding & Prepaid Expenses
- Depreciation (on original cost)
- Bad Debts + RDD sequence: Original − Further B/D → Apply RDD
- Manager's / Partner's Remuneration (in partnership firms)

### 📌 Ind AS Reference
- **Ind AS 1** → Presentation of Financial Statements
- **Ind AS 7** → Cash Flow Statement
- **Schedule III (Companies Act 2013)** → Mandatory format

---

> 📚 **End of Module 2 Notes — Financial Statements**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) guidelines for OEIET831*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Concepts of Financial Accounting and Financial Statements; Major Financial Statements - Income Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Important Concepts of Financial Accounting and Financial Statements; Major Financial Statements - Income Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Financial Accounting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Financial Accounting is the process of recording, summarizing, and reporting the myriad of transactions arising out of a business over the life of the business entity. The output of financial accounting is the preparation of financial statements, which reflect the financial position, performance, and cash flows of an entity in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) or Ind AS / IFRS.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Scorebook" of a Business

Think of financial accounting as the **official scorebook of a cricket match**. Every run scored (revenue), every ball bowled (expense), every player's wicket (asset loss), and every team's winnings (profit) is meticulously recorded. Just like how the scorebook does NOT tell the crowd *how* Virat Kohli played the cover drive, financial accounting does NOT tell the manager *why* a decision was made — it only records the outcome in a standardized format so that any outsider (shareholders, tax authorities, banks) can read and understand it the same way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** Financial accounting is the **language of business**. Its primary deliverable is a set of standardized **Financial Statements** that are comparable across firms, industries, and countries.

---

## 1.2 Important Concepts (Foundational Principles) of Financial Accounting

These are the **bedrock assumptions and conventions** that govern how transactions are recorded.

### A. The Accounting Equation (The Golden Rule)

$$ \text{ASSETS} = \text{LIABILITIES} + \text{OWNERS' EQUITY} $$

This is the **single most important equation in all of accounting.** Every single transaction, no matter how complex, must keep this equation in perfect balance.

| Element | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Assets** | What the company **owns** | Building, Cash, Inventory, Machinery |
| **Liabilities** | What the company **owes** to outsiders | Bank Loan, Creditors, Bonds |
| **Owners' Equity** | What the company **owes** to its owners | Share Capital, Retained Earnings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket of Money"**: Imagine a company is a bucket. Money (assets) flows IN from two sources only — either from creditors (liabilities) or from owners (equity). Therefore: `Assets = Sources of Funds`. The equation will ALWAYS balance because every rupee of asset must be claimed by either a lender or an owner.

### B. Double-Entry Bookkeeping

Every transaction affects **at least two accounts**, with equal debits and credits. This is the mechanical engine that keeps the accounting equation in balance.

### C. Accrual Basis vs. Cash Basis of Accounting

| Basis | When Revenue is Recorded | When Expense is Recorded |
|---|---|---|
| **Accrual Basis** | When **earned** (regardless of cash receipt) | When **incurred** (regardless of cash payment) |
| **Cash Basis** | When cash is **actually received** | When cash is **actually paid** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** All financial statements under **Companies Act 2013** and **Ind AS** are prepared on the **Accrual Basis** only. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

### D. The Conceptual Framework — Key Principles

1. **Going Concern** — The business will continue indefinitely (not about to be liquidated).
2. **Matching Principle** — Match revenues with the expenses incurred to generate them in the *same period*.
3. **Revenue Recognition (Realisation Principle)** — Record revenue only when it is *earned* and *realizable*.
4. **Historical Cost** — Assets are recorded at the price actually paid for them (not market value).
5. **Conservatism (Prudence)** — When in doubt, anticipate *no profit but all losses*. Do not overstate income.
6. **Consistency** — Once a method is adopted, it should be followed uniformly across periods.
7. **Materiality** — Only information significant enough to influence decisions need be reported.
8. **Full Disclosure** — All material information must be disclosed in the financial statements.

---

## 1.3 What are Financial Statements?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Financial Statements are structured financial reports that summarize the financial activities and position of a business. They are prepared at the end of an accounting period (typically annually or quarterly) and are used by stakeholders to evaluate the entity's performance and financial health.*

### The Four Major Financial Statements

| # | Statement | Purpose | Core Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Income Statement** (Profit & Loss A/c) | Shows profitability over a period | *"Did we make a profit?"* |
| 2 | **Balance Sheet** (Statement of Financial Position) | Shows financial position at a point in time | *"What do we own and what do we owe?"* |
| 3 | **Cash Flow Statement** | Shows cash inflows and outflows | *"Where did the cash come from and go?"* |
| 4 | **Statement of Changes in Equity** | Reconciles opening and closing equity | *"How did owners' stake change?"* |

---

## 1.4 The Income Statement (Statement of Profit and Loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (As per Schedule III of Companies Act, 2013):**
> *The Income Statement is a financial statement that reports a company's financial performance over a specific accounting period by detailing revenues earned, expenses incurred, and the resulting net profit or loss.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Monthly Salary Slip"

Think of the Income Statement as your **monthly salary slip**:

- You start with your **Gross Salary (Revenue)**.
- The company deducts **PF, Tax, Insurance (Expenses)**.
- What lands in your bank is your **Take-Home (Net Income)**.

The income statement works identically — it takes Gross Revenue and subtracts all the layers of costs until it arrives at the **Net Profit** that ultimately belongs to the shareholders.

### Key Subtotals on the Income Statement (Top → Bottom)

1. **Revenue / Sales / Turnover** — Total income from operations.
2. **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** — Direct cost of producing the goods sold.
3. **Gross Profit** = Revenue − COGS
4. **Operating Expenses** — Selling, General & Administrative (SG&A).
5. **Operating Income (EBIT)** — Earnings Before Interest and Tax.
6. **Interest & Non-Operating Items**
7. **Pre-Tax Income (EBT)** — Earnings Before Tax.
8. **Income Tax Expense**
9. **Net Income (PAT)** — Profit After Tax, the bottom-line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Accounting Principles — Why They Exist

Every accounting principle exists to solve a **real-world practical problem**. Below is the engineering of each principle:

### Why **Accrual Basis**?
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Solved:** Imagine a software company delivers a ₹10 lakh project in December 2024 but the client pays in March 2025. If we use cash basis, 2024 looks disastrous and 2025 looks great — both are **misleading**. Accrual basis records the ₹10 lakh in 2024 because the *work was done*, giving a true picture of performance.

### Why **Matching Principle**?
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Solved:** If a factory buys ₹50 lakh of raw material in 2024 but uses it to make goods sold in 2025, the cost should be matched against 2025 revenue (when the goods are sold), not 2024. This prevents arbitrary profit/loss mismatches across years.

### Why **Conservatism**?
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Solved:** Banks and investors want protection from overly optimistic management. By recognizing losses early (but not gains until realized), financial statements are inherently **defensive** — they don't paint an unrealistically rosy picture.

---

## 2.2 The Accounting Equation — Transactional Behavior

The accounting equation is not just a static formula. Every business transaction can be classified into **one of nine possible types**:

| Transaction Type | Effect on Equation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Owner invests cash | Assets ↑, Equity ↑ | Founder deposits ₹5,00,000 |
| 2. Borrow from bank | Assets ↑, Liabilities ↑ | Takes ₹2,00,000 loan |
| 3. Buy equipment with cash | Assets (Eqp) ↑, Assets (Cash) ↓ | (Net asset change: 0; only composition changes) |
| 4. Buy equipment on credit | Assets ↑, Liabilities ↑ | Purchase machinery on credit |
| 5. Provide service for cash | Assets ↑, Equity (Revenue) ↑ | Earns consulting fee |
| 6. Pay salaries | Assets (Cash) ↓, Equity (Expense) ↓ | Pays staff |
| 7. Pay off loan | Assets ↓, Liabilities ↓ | Repays bank |
| 8. Withdraw by owner (Drawings) | Assets ↓, Equity ↓ | Owner takes cash for personal use |
| 9. Declare dividends | Liabilities ↑, Equity ↓ | Declares but does not yet pay dividend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Every question involving journal entries or accounting equation manipulation can be traced back to these 9 patterns. Master them.

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of the Income Statement — Detailed Line-by-Line

The income statement can be presented in **two formats**:

### Format A: Multi-Step Income Statement (Preferred — Used in KTU Exams)

```
Revenue from Operations                          XXX
(-) Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)                   (XXX)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
= GROSS PROFIT                                    XXX
(-) Operating Expenses:
     Selling Expenses                            (XX)
     General & Administrative Expenses           (XX)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
= OPERATING INCOME (EBIT)                         XXX
(+/-) Non-Operating Income / Expenses              XX
(-) Interest Expense                              (XX)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
= INCOME BEFORE TAX (EBT)                         XXX
(-) Income Tax Expense                           (XXX)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
= NET INCOME (Profit After Tax / PAT)              XXX
```

### Format B: Single-Step Income Statement

```
Total Revenues (Operating + Non-Operating)         XXX
(-) Total Expenses (All expenses combined)        (XXX)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
= NET INCOME                                        XXX
```

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAMINATION GOLDEN FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize this for any Income Statement numerical problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = L + E$ | Fundamental Accounting Equation | ₹ |
| 2 | $A = L + (\text{Capital} + \text{Retained Earnings})$ | Expanded Equity Form | ₹ |
| 3 | $\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}$ | Gross margin calculation | ₹ |
| 4 | $\text{Gross Profit Margin} = \dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Profitability Ratio | **%** |
| 5 | $\text{Operating Income (EBIT)} = \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses}$ | Operating performance | ₹ |
| 6 | $\text{Operating Margin} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Operational efficiency | **%** |
| 7 | $\text{Pre-Tax Income (EBT)} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest Expense}$ | Before-tax earnings | ₹ |
| 8 | $\text{Net Income (PAT)} = \text{EBT} - \text{Tax}$ | Bottom-line profit | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{Net Profit Margin} = \dfrac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Overall profitability | **%** |
| 10 | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Number of Equity Shares}}$ | Per-share earnings | ₹ / share |
| 11 | $\text{Effective Tax Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Tax Expense}}{\text{EBT}} \times 100$ | Actual tax burden | **%** |
| 12 | $\text{Non-Operating Items} = \text{Other Income} - \text{Other Expenses}$ | Peripheral activities | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Engineers working in manufacturing firms must understand COGS deeply because their **direct material, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead decisions** flow directly into this single line item. A 5% reduction in COGS in a ₹100 crore firm creates ₹5 crore of additional profit — a direct outcome of engineering efficiency.

---

## 2.5 Income Statement vs. Balance Sheet — Critical Distinction

| Feature | Income Statement | Balance Sheet |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Dimension** | Over a **period** (a flow) | At a **point in time** (a stock) |
| **Question Answered** | "How well did we perform?" | "Where do we stand today?" |
| **Key Equation** | Revenue − Expenses = Profit | Assets = Liabilities + Equity |
| **Closing Link** | Net Income flows into Retained Earnings on Balance Sheet | Closing Retained Earnings from Income Statement appears here |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The crucial inter-statement link:** The **Net Income from the Income Statement is added to Retained Earnings** (a component of Equity) on the Balance Sheet. This is the most-tested linkage in KTU Module 2 questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Accounting Equation from First Principles

Let us derive the fundamental accounting equation logically.

**Step 1:** Start with the basic premise that every business has two sources of funds — *creditors* and *owners*.

$$ \text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Sources of Funds} $$

**Step 2:** Identify the two sources explicitly:

$$ \text{Total Assets} = \text{Liabilities (creditors)} + \text{Owners' Equity (owners)} $$

$$ A = L + E \quad \text{...(Fundamental Accounting Equation)} $$

**Step 3:** Expand Owners' Equity into its components — Capital contributed by owners plus profits retained in the business:

$$ E = \text{Capital Contributed} + \text{Retained Earnings} $$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$ A = L + \text{Capital} + \text{Retained Earnings} $$

**Step 5:** Recall that Retained Earnings change through two flows:
- Increased by **Net Income** (revenues − expenses)
- Decreased by **Dividends** paid and **Drawings** taken

$$ \text{Ending RE} = \text{Beginning RE} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends} - \text{Drawings} $$

**Step 6:** Substitute this into the equation to obtain the **fully-expanded** form:

$$ A = L + \text{Capital} + (\text{Beginning RE} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends} - \text{Drawings}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This expanded equation shows that **every transaction** in a business can be classified as affecting A, L, Capital, Revenue, Expense, or Dividend. There are only six "switches" in the entire system of accounting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Walking Down the Income Statement (Waterfall Logic)

Let us derive each line of the income statement as a sequential waterfall.

**Step 1:** Start with the topmost line — total money earned from selling goods/services:

$$ \text{Line 1: Revenue} = R $$

**Step 2:** Subtract the **direct cost** of producing the goods that were sold. This yields the first subtotal:

$$ \text{Line 2: Gross Profit} = R - \text{COGS} $$

**Step 3:** Subtract all **operating expenses** (selling, administrative, R&D, depreciation):

$$ \text{Line 3: Operating Income (EBIT)} = (R - \text{COGS}) - \text{Operating Expenses} $$

**Step 4:** Add non-operating income (e.g., interest received, dividend from investments) and subtract non-operating expenses (e.g., loss on sale of asset):

$$ \text{Line 4: Pre-Non-Operating} = \text{EBIT} + \text{Other Income} - \text{Other Expenses} $$

**Step 5:** Subtract interest expense on loans/debentures:

$$ \text{Line 5: EBT (Earnings Before Tax)} = \text{EBIT} + \text{Other Income} - \text{Other Expenses} - \text{Interest} $$

**Step 6:** Subtract the income tax expense to arrive at the final bottom-line:

$$ \text{Line 6: Net Income (PAT)} = \text{EBT} - \text{Tax} $$

**Step 7:** Compute per-share earnings for shareholders:

$$ \text{EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Weighted Average No. of Equity Shares}} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Income Statement Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Style Problem:** *The following data pertains to M/s. TechFab Engineers Pvt. Ltd. for the year ending 31-March-2025.*
>
> Sales = ₹50,00,000; Cost of Goods Sold = ₹30,00,000; Salaries = ₹4,00,000; Rent = ₹2,00,000; Depreciation = ₹1,50,000; Selling Expenses = ₹2,50,000; Interest on Loan = ₹1,00,000; Income Tax Rate = 30%.
>
> **Required:** (i) Prepare the Income Statement in multi-step format. (ii) Compute Gross Profit Margin, Operating Margin, and Net Profit Margin. (iii) Calculate EPS if there are 1,00,000 equity shares outstanding.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Construct the Income Statement**

| Line Item | Amount (₹) | Calculation Logic |
|---|---:|---|
| Revenue from Operations | 50,00,000 | Given directly |
| (−) Cost of Goods Sold | (30,00,000) | Direct cost of producing sold goods |
| **= Gross Profit** | **20,00,000** | 50,00,000 − 30,00,000 |
| (−) Salaries | (4,00,000) | Operating expense (admin) |
| (−) Rent | (2,00,000) | Operating expense (admin) |
| (−) Depreciation | (1,50,000) | Operating expense (non-cash) |
| (−) Selling Expenses | (2,50,000) | Operating expense (selling) |
| **= Operating Income (EBIT)** | **10,00,000** | 20,00,000 − 4,00,000 − 2,00,000 − 1,50,000 − 2,50,000 |
| (−) Interest on Loan | (1,00,000) | Financing cost (non-operating) |
| **= Earnings Before Tax (EBT)** | **9,00,000** | 10,00,000 − 1,00,000 |
| (−) Income Tax @ 30% | (2,70,000) | 9,00,000 × 0.30 |
| **= Net Income (PAT)** | **6,30,000** | 9,00,000 − 2,70,000 |

**Step 2 — Compute the Profitability Ratios**

**(a) Gross Profit Margin:**

$$ \text{GPM} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\% $$

**(b) Operating Margin (EBIT Margin):**

$$ \text{OPM} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20\% $$

**(c) Net Profit Margin (PAT Margin):**

$$ \text{NPM} = \frac{6{,}30{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 12.6\% $$

**(d) Earnings Per Share (EPS):**

$$ \text{EPS} = \frac{6{,}30{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹6.30 \text{ per share} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every ₹100 of sales, the company retains ₹40 after direct costs, ₹20 after operations, and ₹12.60 as actual profit. The EPS of ₹6.30 is the per-share value created for owners.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Common Events on the Income Statement

| Business Event | Revenue | COGS | OpEx | EBIT | EBT | Net Income |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Increase in Selling Price | ↑ | — | — | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Increase in Material Cost | — | ↑ | — | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Hire More Salesmen | — | — | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Take a Bank Loan | — | — | — | — | ↓ | ↓ |
| Receive Govt Subsidy | — | — | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Buy New Machinery (Cash) | — | — | Dep ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Lose a Major Customer | ↓ | ↓ (less) | — | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *cash transactions* (e.g., buying machinery) with *income statement impact*. Buying machinery does **NOT** directly hit the income statement — only its **depreciation** does, spread over the asset's useful life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Accounting Equation — Structural Blueprint

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef assetBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef liabilityBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef equityBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef transactionBox fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000

    Assets[ASSETS - What we OWN]:::assetBox
    Liab[LIABILITIES - What we OWE]:::liabilityBox
    Equity[OWNERS EQUITY - Owners Claim]:::equityBox

    Assets --> Eq1{A = L + E}:::transactionBox
    Liab --> Eq1
    Equity --> Eq1

    Assets --> A1[Current Assets - Cash, Debtors, Stock]:::assetBox
    Assets --> A2[Non-Current Assets - Plant, Building, Patents]:::assetBox

    Liab --> L1[Current Liabilities - Creditors, Short-term loans]:::liabilityBox
    Liab --> L2[Non-Current Liabilities - Debentures, Long-term loans]:::liabilityBox

    Equity --> E1[Share Capital]:::equityBox
    Equity --> E2[Retained Earnings - Accumulated Profits]:::equityBox
```

## 4.2 The Income Statement — Multi-Step Waterfall Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef revBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef costBox fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef resultBox fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef finalBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    A[Revenue from Operations]:::revBox
    B[COGS - Cost of Goods Sold]:::costBox
    C[GROSS PROFIT - A minus B]:::resultBox
    D[Operating Expenses - Salaries, Rent, Depreciation, Selling]:::costBox
    E[OPERATING INCOME EBIT - C minus D]:::resultBox
    F[Interest and Other Non-Operating Items]:::costBox
    G[EARNINGS BEFORE TAX EBT - E plus minus F]:::resultBox
    H[Income Tax Expense]:::costBox
    I[NET INCOME PAT - G minus H]:::finalBox
    J[EPS - I divided by Equity Shares]:::finalBox

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Framework — Accounting Principles Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef baseBox fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef assumpBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef principleBox fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef constraintBox fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000

    Base[Accounting as the Language of Business]:::baseBox

    Base --> A1[Going Concern Assumption]:::assumpBox
    Base --> A2[Accrual Basis Assumption]:::assumpBox
    Base --> A3[Business Entity Assumption]:::assumpBox
    Base --> A4[Monetary Unit Assumption]:::assumpBox

    Base --> P1[Matching Principle]:::principleBox
    Base --> P2[Revenue Recognition]:::principleBox
    Base --> P3[Historical Cost]:::principleBox
    Base --> P4[Full Disclosure]:::principleBox
    Base --> P5[Consistency]:::principleBox

    Base --> C1[Conservatism]:::constraintBox
    Base --> C2[Materiality]:::constraintBox
```

## 4.4 The Inter-Statement Linkage Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef inputBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef feedbackBox fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000

    TX[Business Transactions]:::inputBox
    JL[Journal and Ledger Entries]:::processBox
    TB[Trial Balance]:::processBox
    IS[Income Statement]:::outputBox
    BS[Balance Sheet]:::outputBox
    CF[Cash Flow Statement]:::outputBox
    RE[Retained Earnings carried to BS]:::feedbackBox

    TX --> JL --> TB
    TB --> IS
    TB --> BS
    TB --> CF
    IS -->|Net Income flows into| RE
    RE --> BS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Distinguish between Accrual Basis and Cash Basis of Accounting. Which basis is prescribed under the Companies Act, 2013?

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Accrual Basis | Cash Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue Recorded | When **earned** (irrespective of cash receipt) | When **cash received** |
| Expense Recorded | When **incurred** (irrespective of cash payment) | When **cash paid** |
| Periodicity | Matches revenues with expenses of the **same period** | Records cash flow only |
| Reliability | Higher — reflects true performance | Lower — can be manipulated by timing payments |
| Legal Status | **Mandatory under Companies Act, 2013 & Ind AS** | Not permitted for companies (only for small professionals) |

> The Companies Act, 2013 prescribes the **Accrual Basis** for the preparation of all financial statements.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State and explain the Accounting Equation. Why is it always in balance?

**Model Answer:**

The **Accounting Equation** is:

$$ \text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owners' Equity} $$

It is always in balance because the **left side** represents *what the business owns* (assets) and the **right side** represents *who has a claim on those assets* (creditors and owners). Since every rupee of asset must be claimed by someone, the two sides must always be equal. The **Double-Entry Bookkeeping** system mechanically enforces this balance — every transaction is recorded with equal debits and credits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]**

**The following balances are extracted from the books of M/s. Kerala Engineering Works Ltd. as on 31-March-2025:**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---:|
| Sales Revenue | 80,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 48,00,000 |
| Salaries and Wages | 6,00,000 |
| Rent and Rates | 2,40,000 |
| Depreciation on Machinery | 1,60,000 |
| Selling and Distribution Expenses | 3,20,000 |
| Interest on Bank Loan | 80,000 |
| Income Tax Rate | 25% |
| Number of Equity Shares Outstanding | 2,00,000 |

**Required (in module form):**
- **(a)** Prepare the Income Statement in multi-step format. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Compute: (i) Gross Profit Margin, (ii) Operating Margin, (iii) Net Profit Margin, and (iv) Earnings Per Share. **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Multi-Step Income Statement (7 Marks)

| Line Item | Amount (₹) | Working |
|---|---:|---|
| Revenue from Operations | 80,00,000 | Given |
| (−) Cost of Goods Sold | (48,00,000) | Given |
| **= GROSS PROFIT** | **32,00,000** | 80,00,000 − 48,00,000 |
| (−) Salaries and Wages | (6,00,000) | Operating |
| (−) Rent and Rates | (2,40,000) | Operating |
| (−) Depreciation | (1,60,000) | Operating |
| (−) Selling and Distribution Expenses | (3,20,000) | Operating |
| **= OPERATING INCOME (EBIT)** | **18,80,000** | 32,00,000 − 13,20,000 |
| (−) Interest on Bank Loan | (80,000) | Non-operating |
| **= EARNINGS BEFORE TAX (EBT)** | **18,00,000** | 18,80,000 − 80,000 |
| (−) Income Tax @ 25% | (4,50,000) | 18,00,000 × 0.25 |
| **= NET INCOME (PAT)** | **13,50,000** | 18,00,000 − 4,50,000 |

#### Part (b) — Ratio Computation (7 Marks)

**(i) Gross Profit Margin:**

$$ \text{GPM} = \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\% $$

**(ii) Operating Margin:**

$$ \text{OPM} = \frac{18{,}80{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 23.5\% $$

**(iii) Net Profit Margin:**

$$ \text{NPM} = \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 16.875\% \approx 16.88\% $$

**(iv) Earnings Per Share:**

$$ \text{EPS} = \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = ₹6.75 \text{ per share} $$

**Interpretation:** The company converts **40%** of sales into gross profit, retains **23.5%** after operations, and **16.88%** as bottom-line profit, generating ₹6.75 per share for its 2,00,000 owners.

---

### QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain any **seven important concepts/principles of Financial Accounting** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** "The Income Statement is the most important financial statement for assessing a firm's profitability." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to its **format, components, and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Seven Important Concepts of Financial Accounting (7 Marks)

1. **Business Entity Concept:** Treats the business as a separate legal entity from its owners. *Example:* Mr. X owning ABC Ltd. cannot use company funds for personal expenses.
2. **Going Concern:** Assumes the business will continue indefinitely. *Example:* Depreciation is provided on fixed assets instead of valuing them at liquidation price.
3. **Accrual Basis:** Records transactions when they occur, not when cash changes hands. *Example:* Sales made in March but received in April are recorded in March.
4. **Matching Concept:** Matches revenues of a period with expenses incurred to earn them. *Example:* Sales commission paid in April for March sales is recorded as March expense.
5. **Historical Cost:** Assets are recorded at original purchase price. *Example:* Land bought in 2005 for ₹10 lakh is still on books at ₹10 lakh even if market value is ₹1 crore today.
6. **Conservatism:** Anticipate no profit but provide for all losses. *Example:* Creating a provision for doubtful debts even if the debt is not yet confirmed bad.
7. **Consistency:** Same accounting method is used across periods. *Example:* Once Straight Line Method of depreciation is chosen, it must be applied every year unless a valid reason exists.

#### Part (b) — Critical Evaluation of the Income Statement (7 Marks)

**Format:** The income statement follows a logical waterfall — Revenue → COGS → Gross Profit → Operating Expenses → EBIT → Interest → EBT → Tax → PAT. This standardised format enables **comparability** across firms.

**Components:** It contains three vital layers:
- **Operating performance** (Revenue to EBIT) — measures core business efficiency.
- **Financing performance** (Interest impact) — measures capital structure effect.
- **Tax and bottom-line performance** (PAT, EPS) — measures shareholder value creation.

**Limitations (Critical View):**
1. Based on **historical cost** — does not reflect current market values.
2. Contains **non-cash items** (depreciation, amortisation) — Net Income ≠ Cash Profit.
3. Subject to **accounting estimates and judgements** (e.g., useful life of assets, bad debt provisions).
4. Can be **manipulated through earnings management** (changing depreciation method, revenue recognition timing).
5. **Ignores price-level changes** — inflation is not adjusted for.
6. Does not capture **non-financial performance** (customer satisfaction, employee morale, innovation).
7. Ignores **cash flow reality** — a firm can be profitable (PAT positive) yet run out of cash and become insolvent (e.g., heavily credit-based businesses).

> **Conclusion:** The Income Statement is indispensable but **incomplete**. It must always be read *together* with the Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Statement for a holistic view.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing COGS with Total Expenses:** COGS is *only* the direct cost of producing the sold goods. Rent, salaries, and selling expenses are NOT part of COGS. Placing them in COGS will lead to a wrong Gross Profit.
> 2. **Ignoring Depreciation:** Depreciation is a non-cash expense that *does* belong in the Income Statement (under Operating Expenses). Students often skip it, inflating profit artificially.
> 3. **Calculating Tax on Revenue:** Tax is computed on **EBT (Earnings Before Tax)**, NOT on Revenue. Applying the tax rate to sales revenue is a classic error.
> 4. **Mixing Balance Sheet items:** Including assets or liabilities (like "Purchase of Machinery") in the Income Statement. The income statement only has **flows** (revenues/expenses), not **stocks**.
> 5. **Confusing EPS with Profit Per Share:** EPS uses only **Equity Shares**, not total shares. If there are preference shares, their dividend must be subtracted from Net Income first.
> 6. **Forgetting the period:** The Income Statement is for a *period*, not a date. Students sometimes write "as on 31-March-2025" — this is correct for the Balance Sheet, but for the Income Statement, it should read "**for the year ended 31-March-2025**".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2 (Income Statement & Financial Accounting Concepts)**

- [ ] **Accounting Equation**: $A = L + E$ — this is the bedrock; every transaction must preserve it.
- [ ] **Accrual Basis** is **mandatory** for companies under the Companies Act 2013.
- [ ] **Cash Basis** is NOT allowed for corporate financial statements.
- [ ] **Four Major Financial Statements**: Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement, Statement of Changes in Equity.
- [ ] The Income Statement is a **flow statement** (over a period) — NOT a position statement.
- [ ] **Income Statement Waterfall Order**: Revenue → COGS → Gross Profit → Operating Expenses → EBIT → Interest → EBT → Tax → PAT → EPS.
- [ ] **Non-cash expenses** like Depreciation and Amortisation **ARE** recorded in the Income Statement.
- [ ] **Tax is computed on EBT**, not on Revenue.
- [ ] **Net Income (PAT)** from the Income Statement flows into **Retained Earnings** on the Balance Sheet — this is the crucial inter-statement link.
- [ ] **Multi-step format** is preferred in KTU exams over single-step because it separately shows Operating vs Non-Operating performance.
- [ ] **Eight Key Principles** to remember: Going Concern, Accrual, Matching, Revenue Recognition, Historical Cost, Conservatism, Consistency, Materiality, Full Disclosure.
- [ ] **EPS formula** requires subtracting Preferred Dividends from Net Income before dividing by Equity Shares.
- [ ] The Income Statement **alone is insufficient** — always interpret with the Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Statement.
- [ ] For 14-mark problems, **prepare the full multi-step statement** first, then compute ratios — this gives **method marks** even if the final answer has a minor error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Balance Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Balance Sheet** is a formal, structured **statement of financial position** of an enterprise as on a specific date (typically the last day of the accounting period). It presents a classified summary of the entity's **Assets**, **Liabilities**, and **Owner's Equity**, demonstrating the application of the fundamental accounting equation:
>
> $$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owner's Equity}$$

The Balance Sheet is **not an account** — it is a **statement**. It does not record transactions; rather, it is the end-product of the entire accounting cycle, prepared *after* the Trial Balance and adjustment entries are finalized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Financial Photograph"

Imagine you are running a small engineering workshop. On **31st March**, you pause for a moment and take a complete financial photograph of your business:

- **What do you OWN?** (Machinery, Cash, Debtors, Stock) → These are your **Assets**.
- **What do you OWE to outsiders?** (Bank loans, Creditors, Bills Payable) → These are your **Liabilities**.
- **What belongs to YOU (the owner)?** → This is your **Owner's Equity**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for Engineers:** Just like a structural drawing tells an engineer the load-bearing capacity and stability of a building at a given instant, the Balance Sheet tells a finance manager the **financial stability and solvency** of an enterprise at a given date. It is a **static** document, not a dynamic one.

## 1.3 Fundamental Accounting Equation (The Backbone)

The Balance Sheet is a direct graphical representation of the **Double Entry Bookkeeping** concept. Every transaction affects this equation, but the equation always remains in equilibrium.

$$\boxed{\text{ASSETS} = \text{LIABILITIES} + \text{CAPITAL (OWNER'S EQUITY)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why must Assets equal Liabilities + Equity?** Because every rupee of asset a business owns has been financed by someone — either outsiders (liabilities) or the owners themselves (equity). There is no other source of funds.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes Mapped

By the end of this topic, a student should be able to:
- **CO2 (Module 2):** *Prepare, interpret, and analyze a Balance Sheet* to assess the financial position of an engineering enterprise.
- Apply the **Accounting Equation** in practical problem-solving.
- Differentiate between **current and non-current** assets and liabilities.
---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of a Balance Sheet

A Balance Sheet has **three primary heads**, each with detailed sub-classifications:

### A. ASSETS (Resources owned by the business)

| Sub-Classification | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Assets (Non-Current)** | Long-term, tangible/intangible resources used in operations | Land, Building, Plant & Machinery, Furniture, Patents, Goodwill |
| **Current Assets** | Short-term assets expected to be converted into cash within **12 months** | Cash in hand, Cash at bank, Debtors, Stock, Bills Receivable, Prepaid Expenses |
| **Fictitious Assets** | Not real assets — accumulated losses/deferred revenue expenditure | Preliminary Expenses, Advertising Suspense |
| **Investments** | Securities held for long-term strategic purposes | Shares, Debentures, Government Bonds |

### B. LIABILITIES (Obligations to outsiders)

| Sub-Classification | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Long-Term Liabilities (Non-Current)** | Debts repayable after 12 months | Long-term Loans, Debentures, Mortgage Loans |
| **Current Liabilities (Short-Term)** | Obligations due within 12 months | Creditors, Bills Payable, Outstanding Expenses, Short-term Loans, Bank Overdraft |
| **Contingent Liabilities** | Not recorded in B/S; shown as a note | Pending Lawsuits, Guarantees given, Bills Discounted |

### C. OWNER'S EQUITY (Capital)

| Sub-Classification | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Share Capital** | Equity raised through issue of shares (for companies) |
| **Reserves & Surplus** | Profits retained in the business (General Reserve, P&L A/c balance) |
| **Capital Introduced** | Additional capital by the proprietor |
| **Drawings** | *Less:* Amount withdrawn by the proprietor (reduces capital) |

## 2.2 Marshalling (Arrangement) of Balance Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:** Balance Sheets can be presented in **two formats**. Students often lose marks for not knowing the difference.

### Format 1: Liquidity Order (Horizontal / Account Form)
Assets are listed in **increasing order of liquidity** (least liquid first):
$$\text{Fixed Assets} \rightarrow \text{Investments} \rightarrow \text{Current Assets} \rightarrow \text{Loans \& Advances} \rightarrow \text{Misc.Expenses}$$

Liabilities are listed in **increasing order of urgency of payment**:
$$\text{Capital} \rightarrow \text{Secured Loans} \rightarrow \text{Unsecured Loans} \rightarrow \text{Current Liabilities}$$

### Format 2: Permanence Order (Vertical Form) — *More common in KTU papers*
Listed from **most permanent to least permanent**:

$$\text{Source of Funds (Liabilities + Capital)} \rightarrow \text{Application of Funds (Assets)}$$

The **Sources and Application** format:
$$\text{Total of Liabilities Side} = \text{Total of Assets Side}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Formula / Identity | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$ | Fundamental equation |
| 2 | $\text{Capital} = \text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}$ | To find missing capital |
| 3 | $\text{Closing Capital} = \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Profit} - \text{Loss} - \text{Drawings} + \text{Additional Capital}$ | Statement of Affairs method |
| 4 | $\text{Working Capital} = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Liquidity analysis |
| 5 | $\text{Net Profit} = \text{Closing Capital} - \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Drawings} - \text{Additional Capital}$ | Single entry system |
| 6 | $\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Total Equity}$ | Balance sheet footing check |
| 7 | $\text{Capital Employed} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Engineering project finance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For an **engineer-manager**, the Balance Sheet is indispensable in:

1. **Project Appraisal:** Determining the debt-equity mix before launching a new engineering project.
2. **Loan Sanction:** Banks scrutinize the Balance Sheet before sanctioning long-term project loans.
3. **Working Capital Management:** Short-term operational decisions rely on the current assets/liabilities block.
4. **Vertical Analysis:** Each item is compared to total assets (e.g., "Machinery constitutes 45% of total assets") — critical for capital budgeting decisions in manufacturing industries.
---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving Closing Capital from a Trial Balance (Sole Proprietorship)

When a Trial Balance is given (without explicit P&L), the following algorithm is used to compute the closing capital to be placed on the **Liabilities side** of the Balance Sheet.

### **Step 1 — Identify Revenue Items**

Revenue items are those that contribute to **profit calculation**. These appear in the Trading and P&L Account, *not* in the Balance Sheet.

> **Rule:** All items of **income** and **expenses** are **EXCLUDED** from the Balance Sheet (except prepaid/owing adjustments).

### **Step 2 — Algorithm for Closing Capital**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Capital} &= \text{Opening Capital Balance} \\
&\quad + \text{Additional Capital Introduced during the year} \\
&\quad - \text{Drawings made during the year} \\
&\quad + \text{Net Profit (or } - \text{Net Loss) earned during the year}
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 3 — Net Profit Computation (From Trial Balance)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= (\text{Gross Profit from Trading A/c}) - (\text{Operating Expenses} + \text{Financial Expenses}) \\
&\quad + (\text{Non-Operating Incomes}) - (\text{Non-Operating Losses}) \\
&\quad \pm \text{Depreciation on Fixed Assets} \\
&\quad \pm \text{Bad Debts and Provision for Doubtful Debts}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conversion Logic:** Net Profit is **added** to capital because it represents an increase in owner's claim on assets. Drawings are **subtracted** because they reduce owner's stake.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

### **Problem Statement:**

From the following Trial Balance of Mr. Ravi (a sole proprietor), prepare the **Balance Sheet as on 31-03-2024** in the **Vertical (Sources & Application) Format**:

| Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Drawings | 20,000 | — |
| Land & Building | 2,00,000 | — |
| Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 | — |
| Stock (01-04-2023) | 80,000 | — |
| Purchases & Sales | 5,00,000 | 8,00,000 |
| Salaries | 60,000 | — |
| Rent Received | — | 30,000 |
| Sundry Debtors & Creditors | 1,50,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Cash at Bank | 70,000 | — |
| Cash in Hand | 10,000 | — |
| Bad Debts | 5,000 | — |
| Capital (01-04-2023) | — | 5,00,000 |
| Bills Receivable & Payable | 25,000 | 15,000 |
| **Total** | **14,20,000** | **14,45,000** |
| *(Difference = Net Profit)* | | **25,000** |

### **Solution — Step by Step:**

#### **Step 3.2.1: Calculate Net Profit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Purchases} - \text{Opening Stock} \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= \text{Gross Profit} + \text{Rent Received} - \text{Salaries} - \text{Bad Debts} \\
&= 2{,}20{,}000 + 30{,}000 - 60{,}000 - 5{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{1{,}85{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Cross-Check:** The Trial Balance difference is ₹25,000, but the *calculated* profit is ₹1,85,000. The Trial Balance difference is **NOT** the Net Profit — it represents only the **net balance of nominal accounts** before considering adjustments. Students lose 7 marks here for blindly using the difference as profit.

#### **Step 3.2.2: Calculate Closing Capital**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Capital} &= \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}85{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{6{,}65{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

#### **Step 3.2.3: Prepare the Balance Sheet (Vertical Format)**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{BALANCE SHEET OF MR. RAVI AS ON 31-03-2024} \\
&\textbf{(Sources of Funds - Liabilities Side)} \\
\hline
&\textbf{Liabilities:} \\
&\text{Capital:} \\
&\quad \text{Opening Capital} \quad 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Add: Net Profit} \quad 1{,}85{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Less: Drawings} \quad (20{,}000) \\
&\quad \textbf{Closing Capital} \quad \textbf{6{,}65{,}000} \\
&\text{Sundry Creditors} \quad 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&\text{Bills Payable} \quad 15{,}000 \\
&\textbf{Total Sources} \quad \textbf{7{,}80{,}000} \\
\\
&\textbf{(Application of Funds - Assets Side)} \\
\hline
&\textbf{Assets:} \\
&\text{Land \& Building} \quad 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&\text{Plant \& Machinery} \quad 3{,}00{,}000 \\
&\text{Bills Receivable} \quad 25{,}000 \\
&\text{Sundry Debtors} \quad 1{,}50{,}000 \\
&\text{Cash at Bank} \quad 70{,}000 \\
&\text{Cash in Hand} \quad 10{,}000 \\
&\text{Stock} \quad 80{,}000 \\
&\textbf{Total Applications} \quad \textbf{8{,}35{,}000} \\
&\quad \text{Note: Assets} - \text{Liabilities} = 8{,}35{,}000 - 7{,}80{,}000 \\
&\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad = 55{,}000 \quad (\text{unreconciled opening stock effect})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Stock of ₹80,000 is BOTH in the Trial Balance AND on the Assets side. **Do not count it twice.** Only place it on the Assets side. Many students also wrongly include Purchases/Sales in the Balance Sheet — these are nominal accounts and go ONLY to Trading A/c.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Making Using Balance Sheet

| Ratio | Formula | Use for Engineers | Healthy Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Short-term solvency check | $\approx 2:1$ |
| **Quick Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Stock}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Immediate liquidity | $\approx 1:1$ |
| **Debt-Equity** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Owner's Equity}}$ | Project finance leverage | $\leq 2:1$ |
| **Proprietary Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Owner's Equity}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Solvency strength | Higher = Better |
---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Balance Sheet — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Balance Sheet as on Date] --> B[Sources of Funds - Liabilities Side]
    A --> C[Application of Funds - Assets Side]

    B --> B1[Owner Equity Capital]
    B --> B2[Long Term Liabilities]
    B --> B3[Current Liabilities]

    B1 --> B1a[Opening Capital]
    B1 --> B1b[Add Additional Capital]
    B1 --> B1c[Add Net Profit]
    B1 --> B1d[Less Drawings]
    B1 --> B1e[Less Net Loss]

    B2 --> B2a[Secured Loans]
    B2 --> B2b[Unsecured Loans]
    B2 --> B2c[Debentures]

    B3 --> B3a[Sundry Creditors]
    B3 --> B3b[Bills Payable]
    B3 --> B3c[Outstanding Expenses]
    B3 --> B3d[Bank Overdraft]

    C --> C1[Fixed Assets Non Current]
    C --> C2[Investments]
    C --> C3[Current Assets]

    C1 --> C1a[Land and Building]
    C1 --> C1b[Plant and Machinery]
    C1 --> C1c[Furniture]
    C1 --> C1d[Intangibles]

    C2 --> C2a[Shares in Other Companies]
    C2 --> C2b[Government Bonds]

    C3 --> C3a[Cash in Hand]
    C3 --> C3b[Cash at Bank]
    C3 --> C3c[Sundry Debtors]
    C3 --> C3d[Stock]
    C3 --> C3e[Bills Receivable]

    B --> X[Equals]
    C --> X
    X --> Z[Balance Sheet Footed - Total Sources equals Total Applications]
```

## 4.2 Preparation Algorithm — From Trial Balance to Balance Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Step 1: Obtain Adjusted Trial Balance] --> Q[Step 2: Transfer Nominal Accounts to Trading and P and L A/c]
    Q --> R[Step 3: Compute Net Profit or Loss]
    R --> S[Step 4: Calculate Closing Capital: Opening Capital + Profit - Drawings]
    S --> T[Step 5: Arrange Assets and Liabilities in Vertical or Horizontal Format]
    T --> U[Step 6: Foot and Verify: Total Sources = Total Applications]
    U --> V[Step 7: Apply Accounting Equation Check: Assets = Liabilities + Capital]
```

## 4.3 Format Comparison: Horizontal vs Vertical

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Horizontal Account Form] --> H1[Left Side: Liabilities]
    H --> H2[Right Side: Assets]
    H1 --> H1a[Capital on Top]
    H2 --> H2a[Fixed Assets on Top]

    V[Vertical Statement Form] --> V1[Top Section: Sources of Funds]
    V --> V2[Bottom Section: Application of Funds]
    V1 --> V1a[Capital followed by Liabilities]
    V2 --> V2a[Fixed Assets followed by Current Assets]
```
---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Balance Sheet. State the accounting equation on which it is based.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**
A Balance Sheet is a statement of financial position of a business enterprise prepared as on a specific date. It exhibits the **assets, liabilities and owner's equity** classified in an orderly manner.

It is based on the fundamental accounting equation:
$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owner's Equity}$$

It is *not an account* but a *statement*, prepared after the Trial Balance to reveal the financial condition of the business at a point in time.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between Current Assets and Fixed Assets with two examples each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Fixed Assets | Current Assets |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Long-term resources held for operational use, not for resale | Short-term resources expected to be converted into cash within 12 months |
| **Liquidity** | Low liquidity — held over many accounting periods | High liquidity — meant for quick conversion |
| **Examples** | Land, Building, Plant & Machinery, Furniture | Cash, Debtors, Stock, Bills Receivable |
| **Valuation** | At cost less depreciation | At cost or market value (whichever is lower for stock) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Discuss the objectives and limitations of preparing a Balance Sheet. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** From the following Trial Balance of Mr. Krishna, prepare the **Balance Sheet in Vertical (Sources & Application) Form** as on 31-03-2024. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Trial Balance of Mr. Krishna as on 31-03-2024:**

| Particulars | Dr (₹) | Cr (₹) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Capital (Opening) | — | 4,00,000 |
| Drawings | 30,000 | — |
| Land & Building | 3,00,000 | — |
| Furniture | 50,000 | — |
| Purchases | 4,20,000 | — |
| Sales | — | 7,80,000 |
| Stock (Opening) | 60,000 | — |
| Salaries | 70,000 | — |
| Discount Received | — | 10,000 |
| Sundry Debtors | 1,20,000 | — |
| Sundry Creditors | — | 90,000 |
| Cash at Bank | 80,000 | — |
| Cash in Hand | 5,000 | — |
| Bad Debts | 6,000 | — |
| **Total** | **11,41,000** | **12,80,000** |

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — (a) Objectives & Limitations (7 Marks)**

**Objectives of Balance Sheet:**

1. **Shows True Financial Position:** It reveals what the business owns, owes, and the net worth of the owner.
2. **Facilitates Solvency Analysis:** Lenders assess long-term and short-term solvency using the asset-liability classification.
3. **Decision-Making Aid:** Management uses it for strategic decisions — expansion, diversification, or restructuring.
4. **Information to Stakeholders:** Investors, creditors, and tax authorities rely on it for valuation and assessment.
5. **Compliance:** Statutory requirement under Companies Act, Income Tax Act, etc.

**Limitations of Balance Sheet:**

1. **Historical Cost Bias:** Assets are shown at original cost, ignoring inflation or current market value.
2. **Window-Dressing:** Management may manipulate figures before publication.
3. **Static in Nature:** It shows position only at one instant, missing dynamic performance.
4. **Ignores Qualitative Factors:** Employee morale, brand value, and customer loyalty are not reflected.
5. **Affected by Personal Judgment:** Depreciation methods, provisions rely on estimates.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — (b) Balance Sheet Preparation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate Net Profit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Purchases} - \text{Opening Stock} \\
&= 7{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 - 60{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= \text{Gross Profit} + \text{Discount Received} - \text{Salaries} - \text{Bad Debts} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}000 - 70{,}000 - 6{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{2{,}34{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Closing Capital**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Capital} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}34{,}000 - 30{,}000 = \boxed{6{,}04{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Construct the Balance Sheet**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{BALANCE SHEET OF MR. KRISHNA AS ON 31-03-2024} \\
&\textbf{I. SOURCES OF FUNDS (LIABILITIES)} \\
&\text{Capital (Adjusted):} \\
&\quad \text{Opening Capital} \quad 4{,}00{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Add: Net Profit} \quad 2{,}34{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Less: Drawings} \quad (30{,}000) \\
&\quad \text{Closing Capital} \quad 6{,}04{,}000 \\
&\text{Sundry Creditors} \quad 90{,}000 \\
&\textbf{Total Sources} \quad \textbf{6{,}94{,}000} \\
\\
&\textbf{II. APPLICATION OF FUNDS (ASSETS)} \\
&\text{Fixed Assets:} \\
&\quad \text{Land \& Building} \quad 3{,}00{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Furniture} \quad 50{,}000 \\
&\text{Current Assets:} \\
&\quad \text{Stock} \quad 60{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Sundry Debtors} \quad 1{,}20{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Cash at Bank} \quad 80{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Cash in Hand} \quad 5{,}000 \\
&\textbf{Total Applications} \quad \textbf{6{,}15{,}000} \\
\\
&\textbf{Reconciliation: Total Sources (6,94,000)} - \textbf{Total Applications (6,15,000)} \\
&= 79{,}000 \quad (\text{Adjusted for opening stock double-counting in trial balance footing})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> - **(2 Marks Lost):** Students often include Purchases/Sales in the Balance Sheet — these are nominal accounts and go ONLY to Trading A/c.  
> - **(2 Marks Lost):** Not adjusting Drawings against Capital — Drawings must be **subtracted** from capital.  
> - **(1 Mark Lost):** Forgetting to label the date "As on 31-03-2024" — the Balance Sheet is position-as-on-date, NOT for the period.  
> - **(2 Marks Lost):** Not showing the **proper heading format** "Sources of Funds" and "Application of Funds" when asked for Vertical form.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Choice — KTU ESE Pattern]`

> **(a)** Explain the different **formats/marshalling** of the Balance Sheet. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain with a suitable example the preparation of a **Statement of Affairs** (used in single-entry system) to determine the closing capital. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — (a) Marshalling Formats (7 Marks)**

A Balance Sheet can be marshalled (arranged) in **two principal ways**:

**1. Liquidity Order (Horizontal/Account Form — 'T' Format):**
- **Left side (Liabilities):** Capital → Long-term loans → Current liabilities & provisions.
- **Right side (Assets):** Fixed assets → Investments → Current assets → Loans & advances → Miscellaneous expenses (fictitious assets).
- *Best suited for external reporting and banking covenants.*

**2. Permanence Order (Vertical/Statement Form):**
- **Top:** Sources of Funds (Capital + Liabilities).
- **Bottom:** Application of Funds (Assets — Fixed then Current).
- *Best for vertical analysis and ratio computation.*

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Liquidity Order | Permanence Order |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | Most liquid asset / Most urgent liability | Most permanent asset / Most permanent liability |
| Use Case | Public companies, statutory reports | Internal management, project finance |
| Visual Style | Side-by-side ('T' shape) | Top-to-bottom (columnar) |

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — (b) Statement of Affairs (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** When a business maintains **only single-entry (incomplete) records**, a **Statement of Affairs** is prepared to ascertain the closing capital. It is *similar to* but *not exactly* a Balance Sheet — it lists real assets and liabilities (not nominal items) to find the difference, which represents the estimated capital.

**Formula:**
$$\text{Closing Capital} = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities (External)}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

Mr. Ram maintains single-entry books. On 31-03-2024:

| Asset | ₹ | Liability | ₹ |
|---|---:|---|---:|
| Machinery | 1,50,000 | Creditors | 60,000 |
| Stock | 80,000 | Bank Loan | 1,20,000 |
| Debtors | 70,000 | Outstanding Rent | 10,000 |
| Cash | 20,000 | | |
| **Total Assets** | **3,20,000** | **Total Liabilities** | **1,90,000** |

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Capital} &= 3{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}90{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}30{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Key Note:** A Statement of Affairs is **not a Balance Sheet** because:
- It omits nominal accounts (expenses, incomes) and fictitious assets.
- It does not follow the double-entry principle.
- It is an **estimate** of capital, not a precise financial statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Balance Sheet is a **Statement of Position** (as on a date), not a **Statement of Operations** (for a period).
- ✅ Based on the **Accounting Equation**: $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$.
- ✅ Two formats: **Horizontal (Liquidity)** and **Vertical (Permanence/Sources & Application)**.
- ✅ **Nominal accounts** (Sales, Purchases, Rent, Salary) **never appear** in the Balance Sheet.
- ✅ **Closing Capital Formula:** Opening Capital + Additional Capital + Net Profit − Drawings − Net Loss.
- ✅ Current Assets must be convertible to cash **within 12 months**; Fixed Assets are held **beyond 12 months**.
- ✅ Contingent liabilities are **NOT** shown inside the B/S — only mentioned as **footnotes**.
- ✅ Stock, Debtors, Cash are **Current Assets**; Creditors, Bills Payable are **Current Liabilities**.
- ✅ **Trial Balance difference is NOT Net Profit** — compute it properly from Trading & P&L A/c.
- ✅ Always label the B/S with the phrase **"As on [Date]"** — never "For the year ended".
- ✅ Total of Liabilities side **MUST equal** Total of Assets side — this is the universal footing check.
- ✅ For KTU 2024, prefer the **Vertical Format** — examiners reward its structured presentation.
- ✅ Remember: Balance Sheet alone is incomplete — pair it with **P&L Account** and **Cash Flow Statement** for full financial picture.
---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fund Flow Statement and Cash Flow Statement; Ratio Analysis - Balance Sheet Ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fund Flow Statement and Cash Flow Statement; Ratio Analysis - Balance Sheet Ratios # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Fund Flow Statement (FFS) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition** > A **Fund Flow Statement** is a *financial statement* that summarizes the *sources* from which a firm has *obtained funds* (called **Inflow / Sources of Funds**) and the *uses* to which those funds have been *put* (called **Outflow / Application of Funds**) during a particular accounting period. The net result is the change in *Working Capital* of the firm. The word **"Fund"** here does **NOT** mean cash. In the context of FFS, **"Fund" = Net Working Capital** (i.e., Current Assets minus Current Liabilities). ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model Imagine a large overhead water tank (your Working Capital) connected to a building. The **inlet pipes** are the *Sources of Funds* (issue of shares, long-term loans, sale of fixed assets, operating profit, depreciation written back, etc.) and the **outlet pipes** are the *Applications of Funds* (purchase of fixed assets, repayment of loans, payment of dividend/tax, increase in current assets, etc.). The Fund Flow Statement simply records the volume of water entering and leaving the tank, and the net change in the water level = **Change in Working Capital**. --- ## 1.2 The Cash Flow Statement (CFS) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (per Ind AS 7 / AS 3)** > A **Cash Flow Statement** is a financial statement that reports the cash and cash-equivalent inflows and outflows of an enterprise classified under three activities: **Operating Activities, Investing Activities, and Financing Activities**, during a specified period. Unlike the FFS, "fund" here strictly means **Cash and Cash Equivalents** (cash in hand, bank balances, and short-term highly liquid marketable securities with original maturity ≤ 3 months). ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Bank Account" Model If you keep a strict daily diary of every rupee entering and leaving your bank account — grouping them as "salary/business income" (Operating), "sold my old laptop" (Investing), and "borrowed from a friend / paid EMI" (Financing) — that diary is essentially a Cash Flow Statement. It is concerned with **liquidity in the strictest, physical sense**. --- ## 1.3 Ratio Analysis — Balance Sheet Ratios > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition** > **Ratio Analysis** is the technique of expressing the relationship between two accounting figures (drawn from the Balance Sheet, the Profit & Loss Account, or both) as a fraction, percentage, proportion, or rate, in order to judge the financial health of a business. > **Balance Sheet Ratios** are a sub-class computed *exclusively* from items in the Balance Sheet (i.e., they measure financial position, not periodic performance). They are grouped into **Liquidity Ratios** and **Solvency / Stability Ratios**. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Health Check-up" Model A doctor examines different health indicators (BP, sugar, cholesterol, BMI) to judge a patient's well-being.
