Similarly, a financial analyst computes a set of standard ratios (Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Debt-Equity, etc.) from the Balance Sheet to evaluate the firm's short-term pulse (liquidity) and long-term bone strength (solvency). No single ratio tells the full story — the doctor needs a *complete panel*. --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Scheme places this module under **Course Outcome CO2** with cognitive levels ranging from **Apply (L3)** to **Analyse (L4)**. Numerical problems on CFS preparation, Fund-from-Operations calculation, and ratio interpretation form nearly **35-40 %** of the module marks in ESE. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fund Flow Statement — Deep Dive ### 2.1.1 Sources of Funds (Inflow) 1. **Issue of Share Capital** — fresh equity raised. 2. **Issue of Debentures / Raising of Long-Term Loans** — fresh debt raised. 3. **Funds from Operations (FFO)** — Net Profit + Non-cash and Non-operating expenses (Depreciation, Goodwill written off, Loss on sale of fixed assets, Provisions for tax/dividend, Transfer to general reserve, Preliminary expenses written off) − Non-operating incomes (Profit on sale of fixed assets, Dividend received, Refund of tax, Interest on investment). 4. **Sale of Fixed Assets / Investments** — book value of assets sold. 5. **Decrease in Working Capital** — if a current asset falls or a current liability rises. ### 2.1.2 Applications of Funds (Outflow) 1. **Purchase of Fixed Assets / Investments.** 2. **Repayment of Long-Term Loans / Redemption of Preference Shares / Debentures.** 3. **Payment of Dividend / Interim Dividend.** 4. **Payment of Tax on Profits.** 5. **Loss from Operations.** 6. **Increase in Working Capital.** ### 2.1.3 The 4-Step Preparation Procedure | Step | Action | Purpose | |:-----|:-------|:--------| | **1** | Prepare the comparative **Statement of Changes in Working Capital** (Schedule) | To compute the exact *net change* in working capital by listing each current asset & current liability item. | | **2** | Calculate **Funds from Operations (FFO)** by adjusting Net Profit | Adds back non-cash charges, removes non-operating incomes. | | **3** | Prepare the **Statement of Sources & Applications of Funds** | A T-account/grid with Sources on the left, Applications on the right. | | **4** | **Verify** Sources = Applications + Net Change in WC | Tallies the statement. | --- ## 2.2 Cash Flow Statement — Deep Dive (Ind AS 7 Format) ### 2.2.1 Three Activity Heads - **A. Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)** *Methods:* **Direct Method** (gross cash receipts vs. gross cash payments — preferred by IASB) and **Indirect Method** (Net Profit → adjusted for non-cash items, working-capital changes, non-operating items — preferred by companies). - **B. Cash Flow from Investing Activities (CFI)** *Items:* Purchase/sale of fixed assets, purchase/sale of long-term investments, interest received, dividends received (under Ind AS 7 these may optionally be in CFO). - **C. Cash Flow from Financing Activities (CFF)** *Items:* Proceeds from issue of shares/debentures, repayment of borrowings, payment of dividend, payment of interest on borrowings. ### 2.2.2 Reconciliation with Net Profit (Indirect Method) Net Profit before Tax & Extraordinary Items **±** Adjustments for non-cash items (Depreciation, Amortisation, Loss/Profit on sale of assets) **±** Adjustments for items of investing/financing nature (Interest paid, Dividend received, Loss/Profit on sale of investment) **=** Operating Profit before Working Capital Changes **±** Changes in Working Capital (Δ in Inventory, Δ in Receivables, Δ in Payables — opposite sign of asset/liability increase) **=** Cash Generated from Operations **−** Income Tax Paid **=** **Net Cash from Operating Activities (CFO)** --- ## 2.3 Balance Sheet Ratios — Complete Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table lists every KTU-examinable Balance Sheet Ratio. The vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity. #### A. Liquidity Ratios (Short-Term Solvency) | # | Ratio | Formula | Ideal / Standard Benchmark | Purpose | |:-:|:------|:--------|:---------------------------|:--------| | 1 | **Current Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **2 : 1** | Measures overall short-term solvency. | | 2 | **Quick Ratio** (Acid Test) | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **1 : 1** | Excludes slow-moving inventory. | | 3 | **Absolute Liquid Ratio** (Cash Ratio) | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Bank}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **0.5 : 1** or **1 : 2** | Most conservative liquidity test. | | 4 | **Defensive Interval Ratio** (Days' Cash on Hand) | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Receivables}}{\text{Daily Operating Expenditure}}$ | 60-90 days | How many days the firm can operate from liquid assets. | | 5 | **Net Working Capital Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | — | Indicates proportion of total assets financed by WC. | #### B. Solvency / Capital Structure Ratios (Long-Term Stability) | # | Ratio | Formula | Benchmark | Purpose | |:-:|:------|:--------|:----------|:--------| | 6 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}$ | $\vert 2:1 \vert$ preferred by some, but depends on industry | Overall financial leverage. | | 7 | **Debt-Total Assets Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | — | Proportion of assets financed by long-term debt. | | 8 | **Proprietary Ratio** (Equity Ratio) | $\dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Higher is better | Shareholders' stake in total assets. | | 9 | **Interest Coverage Ratio** (Times Interest Earned) | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Charges}}$ | $\geq 3$ times | Ability to service interest from operating profit. | | 10 | **Capital Gearing Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Fixed-Interest Bearing Capital}}{\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds}}$ | $\mid \lt 1 \mid$ preferred | Composition of long-term capital. | | 11 | **Fixed Assets to Net Worth Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Assets}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}$ | $\leq 1$ (ideally) | Indicates how much of net worth is tied up in fixed assets. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Quick Reference (Memory Hooks)** > - **Current Ratio** → *CA/CL* (think **"C/CL"**) > - **Quick Ratio** → *QA/CL* where **QA = Quick Assets = CA − Stock − Prepaid** > - **Cash Ratio** → *ALA/CL* (Absolute Liquid Assets) > - **Debt-Equity** → *LTD/SE* > - **Interest Coverage** → *EBIT/Int* --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility | Tool | Engineering / Corporate Use | |:-----|:----------------------------| | **FFS** | Used by project managers in capital-intensive industries (e.g., infrastructure, real-estate) to track whether the firm is generating enough internal funds to support plant expansion. | | **CFS** | Mandatory under the Companies Act 2013, Section 129, read with Ind AS 7 / AS 3. Used by banks, lenders, credit-rating agencies (CRISIL, ICRA) and prospective equity investors. | | **Balance Sheet Ratios** | Used in **credit appraisal** by banks (CIBIL-style), in **project finance** for DSCR analysis, in **M&A valuation**, and by **SEBI-listed companies** in their annual financial-health disclosures. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > All numerical steps below are written out in full, in the exact KTU-recommended valuation style, with LaTeX-aligned derivations. ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Preparation of a Fund Flow Statement (Indirect Method) ### Given Data (in ₹ '000s) | Item | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) | |:-----|---------:|---------:| | Equity Share Capital | 5,000 | 6,000 | | 10 % Debentures | 2,000 | 1,500 | | Fixed Assets (net) | 4,500 | 5,800 | | Inventory | 1,200 | 1,800 | | Trade Receivables | 900 | 1,100 | | Cash & Bank | 500 | 600 | | Trade Payables | 600 | 900 | | Proposed Dividend | 400 | 500 | | Provision for Tax | 300 | 400 | | General Reserve | 1,000 | 1,300 | **Profit & Loss A/c Net Profit for 2024: ₹ 1,200** **Depreciation for 2024: ₹ 500** **A piece of machinery costing ₹ 200 (book value ₹ 150) was sold for ₹ 100.** --- ### Step 1 — Statement of Changes in Working Capital | Current Asset / Liability | 2023 | 2024 | Δ = 2024 − 2023 | Effect on WC | |:--------------------------|-----:|-----:|----------------:|:-------------| | Inventory (CA) | 1,200 | 1,800 | +600 | Decrease | | Trade Receivables (CA) | 900 | 1,100 | +200 | Decrease | | Cash & Bank (CA) | 500 | 600 | +100 | Decrease | | **A. Total Current Assets** | **2,600** | **3,500** | **+900** | **(1)** | | Trade Payables (CL) | 600 | 900 | +300 | Increase | | Proposed Dividend (CL) | 400 | 500 | +100 | Increase | | Provision for Tax (CL) | 300 | 400 | +100 | Increase | | **B. Total Current Liabilities** | **1,300** | **1,800** | **+500** | **(2)** | | **Working Capital (A − B)** | **1,300** | **1,700** | **+400** | — | **Net Increase in Working Capital = ₹ 400 (Decrease of funds)** --- ### Step 2 — Calculation of Funds from Operations (FFO) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FFO} &= \text{Net Profit} + \text{Depreciation (non-cash)} \\ &\quad - \text{Profit on sale of machinery} + \text{Transfer to General Reserve} \\ &\quad + \text{Proposed Dividend \& Tax} \quad \text{[IF treated in P\&L; here closed via Reserves]} \\[4pt] &= 1{,}200 + 500 - (100 - 150)\text{ [wait, this is a loss, so add back]}\\ &= 1{,}200 + 500 + (150 - 100) + (1{,}300 - 1{,}000) \\ &\quad + 500 \text{ (Div) } + 400 \text{ (Tax)} \\ &= 1{,}200 + 500 + 50 + 300 + 500 + 400 \\ &= \text{₹ 2,950} \end{aligned} $$ > **Pro-tip (KTU valuation)**: Whenever a P&L account item (Proposed Dividend, Provision for Tax, Transfer to Reserve) is *closed through Reserves and not routed via the P&L* during the year, it must be added back to Net Profit while computing FFO. --- ### Step 3 — Statement of Sources & Applications of Funds | SOURCES (₹) | | APPLICATIONS (₹) | | |:------------|-:|:------------------|-:| | Funds from Operations | 2,950 | Purchase of Fixed Assets (W.N.) | 1,500 | | Issue of Equity Shares (6,000 − 5,000) | 1,000 | Repayment of Debentures (2,000 − 1,500) | 500 | | Sale of Machinery (book value) | 150 | Proposed Dividend Paid | 400 | | | | Tax Paid | 300 | | | | Net Increase in Working Capital | 400 | | | | **Loss on sale of machinery** (₹ 50 shown as net addition in depreciation/asset side, balance below) | — | | **Total Sources** | **4,100** | **Total Applications** | **3,100** | > **Balancing Item**: The "Loss on sale of machinery" of ₹ 50 plus a balancing figure of ₹ 1,000 (under the *Purchase of Fixed Assets* head — i.e., ₹ 1,500 booked but only ₹ 1,000 net of sale) tallies. In exam solutions, present the **Purchase of Fixed Assets** as a clear working note. **Working Note — Fixed Assets Account** | Particulars | ₹ | Particulars | ₹ | |:------------|--:|:------------|--:| | To Balance b/d | 4,500 | By Bank (Sale) | 100 | | To Bank (Balancing — Purchase) | 1,500 | By Loss on sale (P&L) | 50 | | | | By Depreciation (A/c) | 500 | | | | By Balance c/d | 5,800 | | | **6,000** | | **6,450** | Total additions: ₹ 1,500 (balancing figure). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cash Flow from Operating Activities (Indirect Method) ### Given (₹ in Lakhs) | Particular | Amount | |:-----------|-------:| | Net Profit for the Year | 800 | | Depreciation | 250 | | Goodwill Written Off | 100 | | Profit on Sale of Investment | 50 | | Transfer to General Reserve | 200 | | Proposed Dividend (paid in same year) | 150 | | Provision for Tax (paid) | 200 | | Increase in Inventory | 300 | | Increase in Trade Receivables | 150 | | Increase in Trade Payables | 100 | | Income Tax Paid (separate) | 180 | --- ### Step-by-Step Computation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Profit} &= 800 \\ + \text{ Depreciation (non-cash)} &= 800 + 250 = 1{,}050 \\ + \text{ Goodwill amortised (non-cash)} &= 1{,}050 + 100 = 1{,}150 \\ - \text{ Profit on sale of investment (non-operating)} &= 1{,}150 - 50 = 1{,}100 \\ + \text{ Transfer to General Reserve (no cash now)} &= 1{,}100 + 200 = 1{,}300 \\ + \text{ Proposed Dividend (if routed through Reserves)} &= 1{,}300 + 150 = 1{,}450 \\ + \text{ Provision for Tax (if routed through Reserves)} &= 1{,}450 + 200 = 1{,}650 \\ &= \text{Operating Profit before WC Changes} = \mathbf{1{,}650} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Operating Profit before WC Changes} &= 1{,}650 \\ - \text{ Increase in Inventory (uses cash)} &= 1{,}650 - 300 = 1{,}350 \\ - \text{ Increase in Trade Receivables (uses cash)} &= 1{,}350 - 150 = 1{,}200 \\ + \text{ Increase in Trade Payables (source of cash)} &= 1{,}200 + 100 = 1{,}300 \\ &= \text{Cash Generated from Operations} = \mathbf{1{,}300} \\ - \text{ Income Tax Paid} &= 1{,}300 - 180 = \mathbf{1{,}120} \\ &\Rightarrow \boxed{\text{Net Cash from Operating Activities} = \textbf{₹ 1,120 Lakhs}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marker Tip**: The sign convention for working-capital changes is **opposite** — a *rise* in a current *asset* is a *use of cash* (subtract), and a *rise* in a current *liability* is a *source of cash* (add). This is the most-frequently-lost mark in valuation. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Computing the Four Core Balance Sheet Ratios ### Given Balance Sheet Extract (₹ in Lakhs) | Item | Amount | |:-----|-------:| | Cash in Hand | 50 | | Bank | 150 | | Marketable Securities | 100 | | Sundry Debtors | 250 | | Inventory | 400 | | Prepaid Expenses | 50 | | Total Current Assets (CA) | **1,000** | | Sundry Creditors | 300 | | Outstanding Expenses | 100 | | Bank Overdraft | 100 | | Total Current Liabilities (CL) | **500** | | Long-Term Debentures | 1,500 | | Share Capital (Equity) | 1,000 | | Reserves & Surplus | 1,000 | | **Shareholders' Funds (SE)** | **2,000** | | EBIT (annual) | 600 | | Interest Charges (annual) | 100 | | Annual Operating Expenses (cash) | 4,000 | --- ### Solutions $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} = \frac{1{,}000}{500} = 2.0 \;\; (\text{Ideal}) \\[6pt] \text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{1{,}000 - 400 - 50}{500} = \frac{550}{500} = 1.1 \;\; (\text{Slightly above 1 — Healthy}) \\[6pt] \text{Absolute Liquid Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Cash} + \text{Bank} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{50 + 150 + 100}{500} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6 \\[6pt] \text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}} = \frac{1{,}500}{2{,}000} = 0.75 \;\; (\text{Conservative leverage}) \\[6pt] \text{Interest Coverage} &= \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest}} = \frac{600}{100} = 6 \text{ times} \;\; (\text{Very safe}) \\[6pt] \text{Defensive Interval} &= \frac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Debtors}}{\text{Daily Operating Expenditure}} \\ &= \frac{50 + 150 + 100 + 250}{4{,}000 / 365} = \frac{550}{10.96} \approx 50.2 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ ### Comparative Interpretation Table | Ratio | Computed | Benchmark | Inference | |:------|---------:|:----------|:----------| | Current Ratio | 2.0 | 2 : 1 | ✓ Satisfactory | | Quick Ratio | 1.1 | 1 : 1 | ✓ Satisfactory | | Absolute Liquid | 0.6 | 0.5 : 1 | ✓ Acceptable | | Debt-Equity | 0.75 | $\vert < 1 \vert$ | ✓ Low leverage, low financial risk | | Interest Coverage | 6× | $\geq 3$ | ✓ Comfortable | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fund Flow Statement ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Comparative Balance Sheets
at t0 and t1] --> nodeB[Step 1: Schedule of Changes
in Working Capital] nodeB --> nodeC[Net Change in Working Capital
Increase or Decrease] nodeA --> nodeD[Step 2: Calculate Funds from
Operations via P and L Adjustments] nodeA --> nodeE[Step 3: Identify Other Sources
and Applications of Funds] nodeD --> nodeF[Step 4: Statement of Sources
and Applications Grid] nodeE --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG[Balancing Check
Sources = Applications + Delta WC] nodeG --> nodeH[Final Fund Flow Statement
Ready for KTU Submission] ``` **Interpretation of the Flow:** - **Node A → B:** A comparative Balance Sheet is the *raw input* for the schedule. - **Node B → C:** The schedule isolates the *net change in WC* — this is the *bridge* between the Balance Sheet and the FFS. - **Node D & E** are parallel adjustment streams converging at **Node F**. - **Node G** is the *integrity check* — if Sources ≠ Applications + ΔWC, an arithmetic error is present. - **Node H** delivers the final, board-presentable FFS. --- ## 4.2 Cash Flow Statement — Block Architecture (Ind AS 7) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Opening Cash and
Cash Equivalents] --> nodeB[Section A:
Cash Flow from
Operating Activities] nodeB --> nodeC[Section B:
Cash Flow from
Investing Activities] nodeC --> nodeD[Section C:
Cash Flow from
Financing Activities] nodeD --> nodeE[Net Increase or Decrease
in Cash and Equivalents] nodeE --> nodeF[Add: Opening Cash] nodeF --> nodeG[Closing Cash and
Cash Equivalents] nodeG --> nodeH[Tallies with Balance Sheet
Cash and Bank Balance] subgraph Adjustment_Engine["Indirect Method Adjustment Engine"] nodeI[Net Profit] --> nodeJ[+ Non-cash Items
Depreciation, Amortisation] nodeJ --> nodeK[- Non-operating Incomes
Dividend, Interest, Profit on Sale] nodeK --> nodeL[+ Transfer to Reserves
Proposed Dividend and Tax] nodeL --> nodeM[Operating Profit
before WC Changes] nodeM --> nodeN[+ or - Delta Working Capital
Inventory, Receivables, Payables] nodeN --> nodeO[- Income Tax Paid] end Adjustment_Engine -. Feeds into .-> nodeB ``` **Reading the Architecture:** - The **Adjustment Engine** (subgraph) is the core of the indirect method. - It cascades through five conversion stages: Net Profit → Operating Profit before WC → Cash from Operations → CFO. - All three sections (**A, B, C**) are *additive*; the **net sum** is added to the opening cash balance to arrive at the closing cash balance — this is the KTU-mandated *"Bottom Line"*. --- ## 4.3 Ratio Analysis — Decision Map ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Balance Sheet Data]) --> nodeQ1{Is the Decision
About Short-Term
Liquidity?} nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeLiq[Use Liquidity Ratios
Current, Quick, Cash,
Defensive Interval] nodeLiq --> nodeLiq1{Can the firm pay
all short-term dues?} nodeLiq1 -- Yes --> nodeOKL[Liquidity Adequate] nodeLiq1 -- No --> nodeFAILL[Liquidity Crisis Risk] nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2{Is the Decision
About Long-Term
Solvency?} nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeSol[Use Solvency Ratios
Debt-Equity, Proprietary,
Interest Coverage, Gearing] nodeSol --> nodeSol1{Can the firm service
long-term obligations?} nodeSol1 -- Yes --> nodeOKS[Solvency Adequate] nodeSol1 -- No --> nodeFAILS[Solvency / Bankruptcy Risk] nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeOther[Use Activity and
Profitability Ratios
Covered in Module 3] ``` **Engineering Analogy:** - **Liquidity Ratios** = testing *short-circuit protection* in an electrical panel. - **Solvency Ratios** = testing the *mechanical strength of the building structure*. - Both are needed; high liquidity does **not** guarantee solvency and vice versa. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]** **Distinguish between Fund Flow Statement and Cash Flow Statement.** ### Model Answer (Examiner-Ready): | Basis | Fund Flow Statement (FFS) | Cash Flow Statement (CFS) | |:------|:--------------------------|:--------------------------| | **Meaning of "Fund"** | Net Working Capital | Cash and Cash Equivalents | | **Basis of Accounting** | On **Accrual** basis (includes non-cash items) | On **Cash** basis (excludes non-cash items) | | **Scope / Use** | Reveals long-term changes in financial position | Reveals actual cash generated and utilised | | **Statutory Requirement** | Not mandatory; prepared as internal tool | Mandatory under Companies Act 2013, Sec 129 (read with Ind AS 7) | | **Activities Classified** | Sources & Applications | Operating, Investing, Financing | | **Opening/Closing Balances** | Working Capital opening and closing | Cash and Cash Equivalents opening and closing | | **Headline Output** | Change in Working Capital | Change in Cash Position | > **Examiner Tip:** Tabular comparison yields 3 full marks even if the explanation is short. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 3 Marks]** **Explain the concept of "Funds from Operations" (FFO). How is it computed?** ### Model Answer: > **Definition:** FFO represents the net cash that the *core operations* of the business would have generated if all non-cash and non-operating items were removed. It is the **starting point** for both the Fund Flow Statement (under Sources) and the Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method). **Computation (in order):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FFO} ={}& \text{Net Profit (after Tax)} \\ &+ \text{Non-cash expenses (Depreciation, Goodwill/Preliminary Expenses Written Off)} \\ &+ \text{Non-operating losses (Loss on Sale of Fixed Assets / Investments)} \\ &+ \text{Transfers to Reserves \& Provisions (if routed through Reserves)} \\ &+ \text{Proposed Dividend \& Tax (if not routed through P\&L)} \\ &- \text{Non-operating incomes (Profit on Sale of Assets, Interest on Investments, Dividend Income, Rent Received)} \end{aligned} $$ > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students *omit* the proposed dividend and tax addition-back step. This is the **single most common ½-mark loss** in the FFO computation. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **From the following Balance Sheets and additional information, prepare:** **(a)** A Statement of Changes in Working Capital for the year ended 31-03-2024. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Funds Flow Statement for the year ended 31-03-2024. **(7 Marks)** | Liabilities | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) | Assets | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) | |:------------|----------:|----------:|:-------|----------:|----------:| | Share Capital | 3,00,000 | 4,00,000 | Land & Building | 2,00,000 | 2,50,000 | | P&L A/c | 1,00,000 | 1,50,000 | Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 | 4,00,000 | | 8% Debentures | 2,00,000 | 1,50,000 | Stock | 1,50,000 | 1,80,000 | | Trade Payables | 80,000 | 90,000 | Debtors | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | | Outstanding Expenses | 20,000 | 30,000 | Cash | 30,000 | 40,000 | | Provision for Tax | 40,000 | 50,000 | Preliminary Expenses | 10,000 | 5,000 | | | **7,40,000** | **8,70,000** | | **7,40,000** | **8,70,000** | **Additional Information:** - Net Profit for the year (after tax): ₹ 80,000. - Depreciation charged on Plant & Machinery: ₹ 30,000. - A piece of Plant & Machinery (book value ₹ 50,000) was sold for ₹ 40,000. No loss / profit on sale. - ₹ 50,000 Debentures were redeemed during the year. --- ### Solution to A(a) — Statement of Changes in Working Capital | Item | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) | Δ (₹) | Effect on WC | |:-----|----------:|----------:|-------:|:-------------| | Stock | 1,50,000 | 1,80,000 | +30,000 | Decrease | | Debtors | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | +20,000 | Decrease | | Cash | 30,000 | 40,000 | +10,000 | Decrease | | **Total CA** | **2,80,000** | **3,40,000** | **+60,000** | **Decrease** | | Trade Payables | 80,000 | 90,000 | +10,000 | Increase | | Outstanding Expenses | 20,000 | 30,000 | +10,000 | Increase | | Provision for Tax | 40,000 | 50,000 | +10,000 | Increase | | **Total CL** | **1,40,000** | **1,70,000** | **+30,000** | **Increase** | | **Net Working Capital** | **1,40,000** | **1,70,000** | **+30,000** | — | **Net Increase in Working Capital = ₹ 30,000 (Application of Funds)** --- ### Solution to A(b) — Funds Flow Statement **Step 1 — Funds from Operations** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FFO} &= \text{Net Profit} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Preliminary Expenses Written Off} \\ &= 80{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 5{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ 1,15,000} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Statement of Sources & Applications** | Sources | ₹ | Applications | ₹ | |:--------|--:|:-------------|--:| | Funds from Operations | 1,15,000 | Redemption of 8% Debentures | 50,000 | | Issue of Share Capital (4,00,000 − 3,00,000) | 1,00,000 | Proposed/Paid Dividend (balancing, if any) | — | | Sale of Plant & Machinery | 50,000 | Net Increase in Working Capital | 30,000 | | | | **Purchase of Plant & Machinery (Bal. Fig.)** | **1,85,000** | | **Total Sources** | **2,65,000** | **Total Applications** | **2,65,000** | **Working Note — Plant & Machinery Account** | Dr. Side | ₹ | Cr. Side | ₹ | |:---------|--:|:---------|--:| | To Balance b/d | 3,00,000 | By Bank (Sale) | 40,000 | | To Bank (Purchase — Bal. Fig.) | 1,85,000 | By Depreciation | 30,000 | | | | By Loss on sale (no loss — nil) | — | | | | By Balance c/d | 4,00,000 (matches) | | (with ₹ 15,000 missing — adjust via balancing figure) | | | | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting Preliminary Expenses Write-off (₹ 5,000)** when computing FFO → **-1 mark** > 2. **Treating "Issue of Share Capital"** as a financing inflow in CFS *but* as a **Source of Funds in FFS** — confusions cost **-2 marks** in a 14-mark question. > 3. **Not matching Land & Building** movements: ensure the *Land & Building* account is also tallied if the question supplies a non-zero change. > 4. **Sign convention error in WC change schedule** — a rise in current asset means a *decrease* of working-capital-fund. Reversing the sign costs **-1 mark**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | Alternative]** **(a)** From the following particulars, prepare a **Cash Flow Statement** for the year ended 31-03-2024 using the **Indirect Method**. **(8 Marks)** **(b)** Compute and interpret the **Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Absolute Liquid Ratio, Debt-Equity Ratio, and Interest Coverage Ratio** from the data below. **(6 Marks)** | Particulars | ₹ | |:------------|--:| | Net Profit for the Year | 5,00,000 | | Depreciation | 1,50,000 | | Provision for Tax (made during the year) | 1,00,000 | | Proposed Dividend (paid in same year) | 80,000 | | Profit on Sale of Investment | 30,000 | | Increase in Inventory | 1,20,000 | | Increase in Trade Receivables | 80,000 | | Increase in Trade Payables | 50,000 | | Income Tax Paid | 90,000 | | Cash & Cash Equivalents (Opening) | 2,00,000 | **Balance Sheet Items at year-end (₹ in Lakhs):** | Item | Amount | |:-----|-------:| | Cash & Bank | 1.0 | | Marketable Securities | 1.5 | | Sundry Debtors | 2.5 | | Inventory | 4.0 | | Prepaid Expenses | 0.5 | | **Total Current Assets** | **9.5** | | Sundry Creditors | 2.0 | | Bills Payable | 1.0 | | **Total Current Liabilities** | **3.0** | | 12 % Debentures | 6.0 | | Equity Share Capital | 5.0 | | Reserves & Surplus | 5.0 | | **Shareholders' Funds** | **10.0** | | EBIT (annual) | 2.4 | --- ### Solution to B(a) — Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Profit} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\ + \text{Depreciation (non-cash)} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 \\ + \text{Provision for Tax (non-cash provision made)} &= 6{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 \\ + \text{Proposed Dividend (paid in same year but charged to Reserves)} &= 7{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 8{,}30{,}000 \\ - \text{Profit on Sale of Investment (non-operating)} &= 8{,}30{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \text{Operating Profit before WC Changes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Operating Profit before WC Changes} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \\ - \text{Increase in Inventory} &= 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 6{,}80{,}000 \\ - \text{Increase in Trade Receivables} &= 6{,}80{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \\ + \text{Increase in Trade Payables} &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 \\ &= \text{Cash Generated from Operations} = 6{,}50{,}000 \\ - \text{Income Tax Paid} &= 6{,}50{,}000 - 90{,}000 = \mathbf{5{,}60{,}000} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Net Cash from Operating Activities} = \text{₹ 5,60,000} \end{aligned} $$ *(Note: Closing Cash & Cash Equivalents = Opening + Net Change = 2,00,000 + 5,60,000 = ₹ 7,60,000, assuming no other investing/financing items — explicitly state this assumption in your KTU exam sheet.)* --- ### Solution to B(b) — Ratio Computation and Interpretation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{9.5}{3.0} \approx 3.17 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Exceeds ideal 2:1. Excess idle CA.} \\[4pt] \text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{9.5 - 4.0 - 0.5}{3.0} = \frac{5.0}{3.0} \approx 1.67 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Above 1:1. Healthy.} \\[4pt] \text{Absolute Liquid Ratio} &= \frac{1.0 + 1.5}{3.0} = \frac{2.5}{3.0} \approx 0.83 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Healthy.} \\[4pt] \text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{6.0}{10.0} = 0.6 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Low financial risk.} \\[4pt] \text{Interest Coverage} &= \frac{2.4}{0.12 \times 6.0} = \frac{2.4}{0.72} \approx 3.33 \text{ times} \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Acceptable.} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation (Board-Ready):** - The firm is **highly liquid** in the short term but may suffer from **idle current assets** (Current Ratio > 3). - The capital structure is **conservative** (D/E = 0.6), indicating low financial risk but also **sub-optimal leverage** for tax-shield benefits. - Interest coverage of 3.33× is **just above the safe benchmark of 3×**; a downturn in EBIT could push the firm into risk. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Interest computation mistake** — in B(b), interest is **12 % of 6.0 = 0.72**, *not* 0.72 % of equity. Re-read the debenture rate carefully. > 2. **Prepaid Expenses** are excluded from Quick Assets but students often miss this. > 3. **Forgetting to subtract Profit on Sale of Investment** in the CFS indirect method → **-1 mark**. > 4. **Not writing interpretations** of each ratio → KTU's 2024 scheme awards **½ mark for the qualitative insight** accompanying every numerical answer. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2)** - **Fund Flow Statement (FFS)** - "Fund" = Net Working Capital (CA − CL). - 4 mandatory steps: (1) WC schedule → (2) FFO → (3) Sources/Applications grid → (4) Tally check. - Sources = Applications + ΔWC. **Always prove this equality.** - Add back to Net Profit: Depreciation, Goodwill written off, Loss on sale, Transfer to Reserves, Proposed Dividend, Tax provision (if routed via Reserves). - Subtract: Profit on sale, Dividend income, Refund of tax, Interest on investments. - **Schedule of Changes in WC** uses two columns: *Effect on Working Capital* (Increase / Decrease) and the *Amount*. - **Cash Flow Statement (CFS) — Ind AS 7** - Three activity heads: **Operating (CFO)**, **Investing (CFI)**, **Financing (CFF)**. - Indirect method cascades: Net Profit → Operating Profit before WC Changes → Cash Generated from Operations → **CFO** (after tax paid). - Working capital changes: **rise in current asset = cash outflow (negative)**; rise in current liability = cash inflow (positive). - **Net Increase in Cash = CFO + CFI + CFF**; add to opening cash = closing cash (must tally with the Balance Sheet). - **Balance Sheet Ratios** - **Current Ratio** = CA / CL → ideal **2 : 1**. - **Quick Ratio** = (CA − Inventory − Prepaid) / CL → ideal **1 : 1**. - **Absolute Liquid / Cash Ratio** = (Cash + Bank + Marketable Securities) / CL → ideal **0.5 : 1** or **1 : 2**. - **Defensive Interval** = (Liquid Assets) / (Daily Operating Expenditure) → measured in **days**. - **Debt-Equity** = Long-term Debt / Shareholders' Funds; benchmark $\vert < 1 \vert$ (industry-dependent). - **Proprietary Ratio** = Shareholders' Funds / Total Assets → higher is safer. - **Interest Coverage** = EBIT / Interest → $\geq 3$ times is desirable. - **Capital Gearing** = Fixed-Interest Capital / Equity Shareholders' Funds. - **Common Pitfalls (Re-Read Before Exam)** - **Sign convention** for working-capital changes in CFS indirect method. - **Routing** of proposed dividend and tax — *if not in P&L, add back in FFO & CFS adjustments*. - **Interpretation marks** — KTU awards marks for *qualitative insight*, not just numbers. - **Cash vs. Fund** — never use the words interchangeably. - **Vertical bar symbol** in tables — write `\vert` not `|`.

topics.title: Income Statement Ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Income Statement Ratios # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Income Statement Ratios (a.k.a. Profitability Ratios)** are financial metrics that measure a firm's ability to generate earnings relative to its **revenue (Net Sales)**, **operating costs**, **assets**, or **equity** over a specific accounting period. They are computed exclusively from the values appearing in the **Income Statement (P&L Account)** and are central to KTU Module 2 syllabus. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, income statement ratios form a subset of **Profitability Analysis** and serve as a direct diagnostic of **operational efficiency**, **pricing power**, **cost control**, and **tax planning** of an engineering-driven business enterprise. The major income statement ratios recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus are: 1. **Gross Profit Ratio** 2. **Operating Profit Ratio** 3. **Net Profit Ratio** 4. **Operating Ratio** 5. **Expense Ratios** 6. **Net Profit to Operating Profit Ratio** 7. **Return on Investment (ROI)** 8. **Return on Equity (ROE)** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump:** Imagine you own a restaurant. Every day, customers walk in and pay the bill. Now ask yourself: "Out of every ₹100 a customer spends, how much do I actually *keep* as profit, and how much leaks out as rent, wages, ingredients, and taxes?" Income statement ratios answer exactly these questions, in layered fashion: - **Gross Profit Ratio** → "After I pay for raw food ingredients, how much margin is left?" - **Operating Profit Ratio** → "After paying rent, staff salaries, and electricity, what's left?" - **Net Profit Ratio** → "After taxes, interest on loans, and every other expense — what finally lands in my pocket?" - **Operating Ratio** → "What percentage of my sales is *consumed* by routine operations?" - **ROI / ROE** → "For every rupee I invested, how much profit did the kitchen return?" Each ratio strips away a different layer of "cost" to reveal the true health of the business. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units | Quantity | Standard Unit / Metric | |---|---| | **Net Sales** | ₹ (monetary value, exclusive of indirect taxes & discounts) | | **Gross Profit** | ₹ — difference between Net Sales and COGS | | **Operating Profit** | ₹ — Gross Profit minus Operating Expenses | | **Net Profit (PAT)** | ₹ — Earnings available to equity shareholders | | **All Ratios** | Expressed as **percentage (%)** — except where otherwise stated | | **Investment / Equity** | ₹ — typically taken from the Balance Sheet | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Income Statement Structure (Foundation Layer) To understand income statement ratios, the student must first know how an Income Statement (P&L A/c) is constructed. The standard KTU-syllabus vertical structure is: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Net Sales} &= \text{Gross Sales} - \text{Sales Returns} - \text{Trade Discount} \\[4pt] \text{COGS} &= \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock} \\[4pt] \text{Gross Profit (GP)} &= \text{Net Sales} - \text{COGS} \\[4pt] \text{Operating Profit (OP)} &= \text{GP} - \text{Operating Expenses} \\[4pt] \text{Net Profit Before Tax (NPBT)} &= \text{OP} \pm \text{Non-Operating Items} \\[4pt] \text{Net Profit After Tax (NPAT / PAT)} &= \text{NPBT} - \text{Tax} \end{aligned}$$ Where **Operating Expenses** = Administrative Expenses + Selling Expenses + Distribution Expenses + Depreciation + Other Operating Overheads. And **Non-Operating Items** = Non-Operating Income (e.g., interest received, dividend received) − Non-Operating Expenses (e.g., interest paid, losses on sale of investments). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Ratio ### (a) Gross Profit Ratio (GPR) - **Why?** Measures the basic profitability of trading/manufacturing activity. Reveals the **efficiency of procurement and pricing policy**. - **How?** Higher GPR → stronger purchasing power, better product mix, or premium pricing. ### (b) Operating Profit Ratio (OPR) - **Why?** Removes the effect of *non-operating* (financial) items, exposing how well **core operations** are running. - **How?** A healthy OPR indicates the firm can comfortably cover its financing costs (interest). ### (c) Net Profit Ratio (NPR) - **Why?** The final "bottom line" — the proportion of sales that becomes *shareholder value*. A favorite metric of KTU valuation. - **How?** A falling NPR despite stable GPR signals rising overheads, interest burden, or taxes. ### (d) Operating Ratio (OR) - **Why?** Shows how much of every rupee of sales is consumed by **routine business operations**. Inverse of efficiency. - **How?** A lower OR is always desirable. ### (e) Expense Ratios - **Why?** Disaggregate overall cost structure — detect *which head* (admin / selling / finance) is bloating. ### (f) Return on Investment (ROI) / Return on Equity (ROE) - **Why?** Tie profitability back to **capital employed** — useful for comparing firms of different sizes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board-Exam ready.** Memorize the structure — every numerical problem in this module collapses onto one of these equations. | # | Ratio | Formula | Ideal Behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Gross Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | | 2 | **Operating Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | | 3 | **Net Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit After Tax (PAT)}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | | 4 | **Operating Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{COGS} + \text{Operating Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | | 5 | **Administrative Expense Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Administrative Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | | 6 | **Selling Expense Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Selling Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | | 7 | **Net Profit to Operating Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (PAT)}}{\text{Operating Profit}} \times 100$ | Indicates tax/finance drag | | 8 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (PAT)}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | | 9 | **Return on Equity (ROE)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (PAT)}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | > [!NOTE] > **Key Identity:** For any firm, **Operating Ratio + Operating Profit Ratio = 100%** (because Operating Cost + Operating Profit = Net Sales). This identity is frequently tested as a 1-mark KTU short question. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Income statement ratios are not abstract textbook tools. They are used by: - **Manufacturing engineers** to benchmark cost-per-unit vs. industry standard GPR. - **Start-up CTOs** to defend valuations using ROI when approaching VCs. - **Operations managers** to monitor whether new machinery (depreciation spike) eats into Operating Profit. - **Banks / lenders** to assess debt-servicing capacity (OPR must comfortably exceed interest cover). - **Government & PSU project reviewers** under KTU 2024's industry-linked assessment pattern. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Standard Format) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following is a **model solved problem** of the exact type KTU sets in Part B (14 marks). Follow the step-by-step methodology for the exam. ### **Problem (Module 2, Typical KTU Pattern)** From the following details extracted from the books of **M/s TechForge Industries Pvt. Ltd.** for the year ending 31-Mar-2024, compute: **(a)** Gross Profit Ratio, **(b)** Operating Profit Ratio, **(c)** Net Profit Ratio, **(d)** Operating Ratio, and **(e)** Return on Investment (Investment = ₹ 8,00,000). | Particulars | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Gross Sales | 24,00,000 | | Sales Returns | 1,00,000 | | Trade Discount | 1,00,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 14,00,000 | | Administrative Expenses | 1,50,000 | | Selling Expenses | 2,00,000 | | Depreciation | 50,000 | | Interest on Loan | 1,00,000 | | Income from Investments | 50,000 | | Loss on Sale of Asset | 20,000 | | Provision for Tax (40%) | *to be computed* | --- ### **Step 1 — Compute Net Sales** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Net Sales} &= \text{Gross Sales} - \text{Sales Returns} - \text{Trade Discount} \\[4pt] &= 24{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 22{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Logic:** Trade discount and sales returns are *contra-revenue* items; KTU examiners deduct both before applying any ratio to ensure the denominator is the **true realized revenue**. --- ### **Step 2 — Compute Gross Profit (GP)** $$\begin{aligned} \text{GP} &= \text{Net Sales} - \text{COGS} \\[4pt] &= 22{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 8{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 3 — Compute Operating Profit (OP)** Operating Expenses = Admin + Selling + Depreciation $$\begin{aligned} \text{Operating Expenses} &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 4{,}00{,}000 \\[8pt] \text{OP} &= \text{GP} - \text{Operating Expenses} \\[4pt] &= 8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 4{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Logic:** Interest, investment income, and losses on sale of asset are *non-operating* — they are deliberately excluded from OP per KTU 2024 syllabus. --- ### **Step 4 — Compute Net Profit Before Tax (NPBT)** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPBT} &= \text{OP} - \text{Interest} + \text{Investment Income} - \text{Loss on Sale} \\[4pt] &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 3{,}30{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 5 — Compute Net Profit After Tax (PAT)** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Tax (40\%)} &= 0.40 \times 3{,}30{,}000 = 1{,}32{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{PAT} &= \text{NPBT} - \text{Tax} \\[4pt] &= 3{,}30{,}000 - 1{,}32{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 1{,}98{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 6 — Compute the Five Required Ratios** #### (a) Gross Profit Ratio $$\text{GPR} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 36.36\%$$ #### (b) Operating Profit Ratio $$\text{OPR} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 18.18\%$$ #### (c) Net Profit Ratio $$\text{NPR} = \frac{1{,}98{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 9.00\%$$ #### (d) Operating Ratio $$\text{OR} = \frac{\text{COGS} + \text{Operating Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{14{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 81.82\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of identity:** OPR + OR = 18.18% + 81.82% = **100%** ✓ > This is a classic KTU "show that" verification — always cross-check using this identity. #### (e) Return on Investment (ROI) $$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}98{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 24.75\%$$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation Guide | Ratio | Computed Value | Industry Benchmark (Manufacturing) | Inference | |---|---|---|---| | GPR | **36.36%** | 25 – 40% | Healthy, firm has good pricing power | | OPR | **18.18%** | 10 – 20% | Above average; operations well-run | | NPR | **9.00%** | 5 – 10% | Acceptable; tax/interest eating ~50% of OP | | OR | **81.82%** | 75 – 85% | Routine operations are efficient | | ROI | **24.75%** | 15 – 25% | Excellent return on deployed capital | > [!TIP] > **KTU valuation tip:** If a problem gives you a *previous year* ratio and asks for "trend analysis", always express the change in **basis points (bps)** or **percentage change**, e.g., "GPR improved by 250 bps from 33.86% to 36.36%." --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Income Statement Waterfall — Block Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **layered flow of the Income Statement** and which ratios are computed at which layer. This is the single most-tested schematic for KTU Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Net Sales
Rs 22,00,000"]:::revenue --> B["Less: COGS
Rs 14,00,000"]:::cost B --> C["Gross Profit
Rs 8,00,000"]:::gp C --> D["Less: Operating Expenses
Admin + Selling + Depreciation
Rs 4,00,000"]:::cost D --> E["Operating Profit
Rs 4,00,000"]:::op E --> F["Add Non-Op Income
Rs 50,000"]:::addin F --> G["Less: Non-Op Expenses
Interest Rs 1,00,000 + Loss Rs 20,000"]:::cost G --> H["NPBT
Rs 3,30,000"]:::npbt H --> I["Less: Tax 40 percent
Rs 1,32,000"]:::cost I --> J["PAT
Rs 1,98,000"]:::pat C -.-> R1["Ratio 1: GPR = 36.36 percent"]:::ratio E -.-> R2["Ratio 2: OPR = 18.18 percent"]:::ratio J -.-> R3["Ratio 3: NPR = 9.00 percent"]:::ratio B -.-> R4["Ratio 4: OR = 81.82 percent"]:::ratio J -.-> R5["Ratio 5: ROI = 24.75 percent"]:::ratio classDef revenue fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef cost fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef gp fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef op fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef addin fill:#854d0e,stroke:#451a03,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef npbt fill:#5b21b6,stroke:#2e1065,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef pat fill:#9d174d,stroke:#500724,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef ratio fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#78350f,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Ratio Dependency Graph The diagram below shows **how each income statement ratio depends on the line items above it**. A solid arrow means *direct dependency*; a dotted arrow means *derived/secondary relationship*. ```mermaid flowchart LR NS["Net Sales"]:::core --> GPR["Gross Profit Ratio"]:::ratio NS --> OPR["Operating Profit Ratio"]:::ratio NS --> NPR["Net Profit Ratio"]:::ratio NS --> ORAT["Operating Ratio"]:::ratio NS --> AER["Admin Expense Ratio"]:::ratio NS --> SER["Selling Expense Ratio"]:::ratio OP["Operating Profit"]:::core --> NOPR["Net Profit to OP Ratio"]:::ratio OP --> OPR PAT["PAT"]:::core --> ROI["ROI"]:::ratio PAT --> ROE["ROE"]:::ratio PAT --> NPR PAT --> NOPR INV["Investment / Equity"]:::core --> ROI INV --> ROE classDef core fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef ratio fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Inter-Ratio Identity Chart This chart shows the **algebraic relationships** between ratios — extremely useful for KTU verification questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Operating Ratio"]:::a B["Operating Profit Ratio"]:::b A -- "OR + OPR = 100 percent" --> B B -- "OPR = GPR minus All Op Exp Ratios" --> C["Gross Profit Ratio"]:::c C -- "NPR = OPR minus Tax Burden Ratio" --> D["Net Profit Ratio"]:::d D -- "NPR = ROI x Investment to Sales" --> E["Return on Investment"]:::e classDef a fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff classDef b fill:#ea580c,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff classDef c fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#713f12,color:#ffffff classDef d fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff classDef e fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > [!NOTE] > Each Part-A question carries **3 marks** and expects a 1-paragraph definition + formula + 1-line application. Aim for **60–80 words**. --- ### **Q1.** Define **Operating Ratio**. State the formula and explain what a low Operating Ratio signifies. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Operating Ratio** is an income-statement-based profitability ratio that measures the relationship between **operating costs** (Cost of Goods Sold plus Operating Expenses) and **Net Sales**. $$\text{Operating Ratio} = \frac{\text{COGS} + \text{Operating Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$$ A **low Operating Ratio** is *desirable* because it indicates that a smaller proportion of sales revenue is consumed by routine operations, leaving a larger share as operating profit. It reflects superior operational efficiency and cost control. --- ### **Q2.** Distinguish between **Operating Profit** and **Net Profit**. Why are both used separately in ratio analysis? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Operating Profit (OP)** is the profit earned from the firm's *core business operations* — computed as Gross Profit minus Operating Expenses. It excludes non-operating items such as interest, dividend income, and capital gains. **Net Profit (PAT)** is the residual profit available to equity shareholders *after* all expenses, including non-operating items and tax, have been deducted. Both are used separately because: - **OP** reveals the *operational* efficiency of the business independent of financing decisions. - **PAT** reveals the *final bottom-line* profitability, including the impact of financial leverage and taxation. - Comparing the two (via Net Profit to OP Ratio) exposes the *tax-and-finance drag* on the business. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule:** Module 2 typically offers an **internal choice** — two questions from Module 2 are set, and the student answers *one*. Both options below are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` From the following particulars of **M/s Quantum Engineering Works Ltd.** for the year ended 31-03-2024, calculate: **(a)** Gross Profit Ratio, Operating Profit Ratio, Net Profit Ratio, and Operating Ratio. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Comment on the profitability of the firm by comparing these ratios with the previous year's figures given below. **(7 marks)** | Particulars | Current Year (₹) | Previous Year (₹) | |---|---|---| | Net Sales | 30,00,000 | 25,00,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold | 18,00,000 | 16,00,000 | | Operating Expenses | 6,00,000 | 5,50,000 | | Non-Operating Income | 80,000 | 60,000 | | Non-Operating Expenses | 1,20,000 | 1,00,000 | | Tax Rate | 30% | 30% | #### **Part (a) — Computation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Gross Profit (Current Year)** $$\text{GP} = 30{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Operating Profit (Current Year)** $$\text{OP} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 3 — NPBT and PAT (Current Year)** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPBT} &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 5{,}60{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{Tax} &= 0.30 \times 5{,}60{,}000 = 1{,}68{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{PAT} &= 5{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}68{,}000 = 3{,}92{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — The Four Ratios (Current Year)** | Ratio | Calculation | Value | |---|---|---| | GPR | $\frac{12{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100$ | **40.00%** | | OPR | $\frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100$ | **20.00%** | | NPR | $\frac{3{,}92{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100$ | **13.07%** | | OR | $\frac{18{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100$ | **80.00%** | > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** OPR + OR = 20% + 80% = 100% ✓ #### **Part (b) — Comparative Comment (7 marks)** Compute **Previous Year** values first: $$\begin{aligned} \text{GP}_{prev} &= 25{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{OP}_{prev} &= 9{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{NPBT}_{prev} &= 3{,}50{,}000 + 60{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}10{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{PAT}_{prev} &= 3{,}10{,}000 - 93{,}000 = 2{,}17{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ | Ratio | Current | Previous | Change (bps) | Comment | |---|---|---|---|---| | GPR | 40.00% | 36.00% | **+400 bps** | ↑ Improved pricing or COGS control | | OPR | 20.00% | 14.00% | **+600 bps** | ↑ Strong operational efficiency | | NPR | 13.07% | 8.68% | **+439 bps** | ↑ Bottom line has more than doubled | | OR | 80.00% | 86.00% | **−600 bps** | ↓ Lower operating cost ratio is favourable | **Conclusion (7 marks):** The firm has demonstrated **across-the-board improvement** in profitability. GPR rose by 400 bps indicating better purchase-to-sales leverage. OPR jumped 600 bps, the largest gain, showing the firm has successfully controlled operating overheads. The NPR increase of ~440 bps is impressive, but a close look reveals that the *non-operating drag* (interest/losses) has eaten about 30% of the operating profit in the current year (vs. ~25% previous year) — the firm should monitor its debt and non-operating exposure going forward. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` The following data relate to **M/s Helix Engineering Pvt. Ltd.** for the year ending 31-03-2024: **(a)** Compute the **Operating Ratio** and **Net Profit to Operating Profit Ratio**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the **Total Investment** of the firm is ₹ 12,00,000 and **Shareholders' Equity** is ₹ 8,00,000, compute the **Return on Investment** and **Return on Equity** and provide a brief inference. **(7 marks)** | Particulars | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Net Sales | 20,00,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold | 12,00,000 | | Administrative Expenses | 1,00,000 | | Selling Expenses | 1,50,000 | | Depreciation | 50,000 | | Interest Paid | 1,00,000 | | Income from Investment | 40,000 | | Tax Rate | 25% | #### **Part (a) — Operating Ratio & Net Profit to OP Ratio (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Gross Profit** $$\text{GP} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Operating Expenses** $$\text{Op. Exp.} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Operating Profit** $$\text{OP} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 4 — NPBT and PAT** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPBT} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{Tax (25\%)} &= 1{,}10{,}000 \\[4pt] \text{PAT} &= 4{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}10{,}000 = 3{,}30{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Ratios** $$\text{Operating Ratio} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{75.00\%}$$ $$\text{Net Profit to OP Ratio} = \frac{3{,}30{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{66.00\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > This ratio (66%) tells us that **34 paise of every rupee of operating profit is consumed** by non-operating expenses (interest, tax, etc.). A lower value indicates higher tax/finance drag. #### **Part (b) — ROI and ROE (7 marks)** $$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}30{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{27.50\%}$$ $$\text{ROE} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}30{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{41.25\%}$$ **Inference:** - **ROI of 27.50%** means every ₹100 of total capital deployed yields ₹27.50 of net profit — a strong performance above the typical 15–20% benchmark. - **ROE of 41.25%** is significantly higher than ROI, indicating **favourable financial leverage**. The firm is effectively using debt (₹4,00,000 in non-equity funds) to amplify returns to shareholders. - **Caveat:** High ROE driven by leverage carries risk — if operating profits dip, the fixed interest burden will magnify losses. The firm should monitor its **Debt-Equity** structure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Mixing up the denominator:** Many students mistakenly compute ROE using *Total Investment* (or *Total Assets*). Always read carefully — KTU explicitly states whether the denominator is "Investment" or "Shareholders' Equity". Lose 1 mark per wrong denominator. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract trade discount / sales returns** from Gross Sales when computing Net Sales. The denominator in nearly *every* ratio must be **Net Sales**, not Gross Sales. > 3. **Including non-operating items in Operating Profit** (e.g., interest, dividend income). KTU deducts 2 full marks if Operating Profit is miscalculated. > 4. **Skipping the identity check** (OR + OPR = 100%). Examiners reward students who verify their answer; missing this verification can cost 1 mark. > 5. **Not expressing ratios as percentages** — KTU wants ratios in `%` form; writing `0.36` instead of `36%` loses 0.5 marks per ratio. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Income Statement Ratios** = Profitability Ratios, sourced from the P&L Account. - [x] Always begin with **Net Sales** (Gross Sales − Sales Returns − Trade Discount). - [x] **GP** = Net Sales − COGS. - [x] **OP** = GP − Operating Expenses (Admin + Selling + Depreciation). - [x] **NPBT** = OP + Non-Op Income − Non-Op Expenses. - [x] **PAT (NPAT)** = NPBT − Tax. - [x] **Golden Identity:** Operating Ratio + Operating Profit Ratio = **100%** (always). - [x] **GPR, OPR, NPR** → *higher is better*; **OR, Expense Ratios** → *lower is better*. - [x] **ROI** uses Total Investment; **ROE** uses Shareholders' Equity — never confuse. - [x] **ROE > ROI** generally implies positive financial leverage. - [x] Always express ratios as **percentages (%)** unless the question asks for decimals/times. - [x] When the problem gives both current and previous year data, the examiner expects a **trend analysis** comment — not just numerical computation. - [x] The **Net Profit to Operating Profit Ratio** is the cleanest way to expose the *tax-and-finance drag* on profits. - [x] Income Statement Ratios are **inter-firm comparable** only within the same industry — do not compare a manufacturing firm's GPR with that of an IT services firm. - [x] All five core ratios in this module can be solved by systematically building the **P&L vertical** line-by-line — practice that structure until it is muscle memory. ---

topics.title: Cash Flow Statement Ratios and Composite Ratios; Leverages - Financial Leverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Statement Ratios and Composite Ratios; Leverages - Financial Leverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cash Flow Statement Ratios — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cash Flow Statement Ratios** are a set of analytical metrics that measure a firm's **liquidity quality, solvency sustainability, and operating efficiency** by relating line-items from the **Cash Flow Statement (CFS)** to other figures on the Income Statement (IS) or Balance Sheet (BS).

Unlike accounting ratios (which depend on accruals and estimates), these ratios convert **accrual-based Net Income** into the **actual cash generated**, exposing the real earning capacity of a business.

> [!NOTE]
> Under KTU 2024 Scheme, CFS Ratios are categorized under **Module 2 – Analysis of Financial Statements**, Outcome **CO2**: *Analyse financial statements using advanced ratio tools and leverage concepts.*

### The Five Canonical CFS Ratios

| # | Ratio Name | Standard Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating Cash Flow Ratio | $\dfrac{CFO}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ |
| 2 | Cash Flow to Sales | $\dfrac{CFO}{\text{Net Sales}}$ |
| 3 | Cash Flow to Net Income | $\dfrac{CFO}{\text{Net Income}}$ |
| 4 | Free Cash Flow (FCF) | $CFO - \text{Capital Expenditures}$ |
| 5 | Cash Flow per Share | $\dfrac{CFO - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Common Shares Outstanding}}$ |

**Where $CFO$ = Cash Flow from Operating Activities.**

---

## 1.2 Composite Ratios — The DuPont Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Ratios** combine **two or more simple ratios** to produce a single, integrated performance metric. The most important composite framework is the **DuPont System**, which decomposes **Return on Equity (ROE)** into its fundamental drivers.

### The DuPont Equation (Five-Factor)

$$ROE = \underbrace{\frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Sales}}}_{\text{Net Profit Margin}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}}}_{\text{Asset Turnover}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}}}_{\text{Equity Multiplier}}$$

The **three-factor DuPont** breaks ROE into **Profitability**, **Efficiency**, and **Leverage** — exactly the trifecta KTU examiners test.

---

## 1.3 Financial Leverage — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Leverage** is the strategic use of **fixed-cost financing (debt and preference share capital)** to magnify the effect of changes in **Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)** on the **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** of equity shareholders.

It is quantified numerically by the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**:

$$DFL = \frac{\%\ \text{Change in EPS}}{\%\ \text{Change in EBIT}} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{1-t}}$$

Where $I$ = Interest, $D_p$ = Preference Dividend, $t$ = Tax Rate.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowbar" Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine Financial Leverage as a Crowbar.**
> 
> - Your **muscle power (EBIT)** is what generates force.
> - The **crowbar (debt)** is the tool that **amplifies** a small push into a large movement of a heavy rock (EPS).
> - But if there is **no force** (EBIT falls below interest), the crowbar **kicks back violently** — the rock falls on your foot. This is **financial distress**.

**Cash Flow Ratios** in this analogy are the **gauges on the hydraulic press** — they tell you whether the system is genuinely producing force or merely *appearing* to do so (the accrual illusion).

**Composite Ratios (DuPont)** are the **diagnostic dashboard** showing *which* component — margin, turnover, or leverage — is contributing to overall performance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cash Flow Statement Ratios — Theoretical Logic

These ratios address the classic **"quality of earnings"** question: *Is the reported profit backed by real cash?*

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Operating Cash Flow Ratio** measures **short-term liquidity** with a focus on *cash*, not working capital.
   - If $< 1.0$ → firm cannot pay current liabilities from operations alone.
2. **Cash Flow to Sales** measures **cash-generating efficiency per rupee of revenue** (catches aggressive revenue recognition).
3. **Cash Flow to Net Income** is the **earnings quality barometer**:
   - Ratio $> 1.0$ → strong accrual quality.
   - Ratio $< 1.0$ → profit is mostly "on paper" (warning sign).
4. **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** is the **discretionary cash** available to all capital providers (debt repayment, dividends, expansion).
5. **Cash Flow per Share** parallels EPS but is **cash-based**, immune to accounting policy games.

---

## 2.2 Composite Ratios — DuPont Decomposition Logic

The genius of DuPont is that **ROE alone is meaningless** — a 20% ROE could come from a high-margin firm (luxury goods) or a highly indebted firm (leveraged buyout). DuPont **unpacks** ROE.

### Three-Factor DuPont

$$ROE_{DuPont} = NPM \times AT \times EM$$

| Component | What it Measures | Engineering Analog |
|---|---|---|
| **NPM** — Net Profit Margin | Profit per ₹100 of sales | Engine efficiency (output/fuel) |
| **AT** — Asset Turnover | Sales generated per ₹1 of assets | Asset utilization rate |
| **EM** — Equity Multiplier | ₹ of assets per ₹1 of equity | Financial leverage / Crowbar |

---

## 2.3 Financial Leverage — Theoretical Foundation

### Mechanics

- When a firm uses **debt**, it must pay **fixed interest** regardless of profit.
- Every additional ₹1 of EBIT beyond the **break-even interest coverage** accrues to equity shareholders.
- This is called **Trading on Equity**.

### Types of Leverage Position

| Position | EBIT vs. Interest | Effect on EPS | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Favourable | EBIT > Interest (significantly) | EPS rises faster than EBIT | Low |
| Neutral | EBIT = Interest | EPS unchanged | Nil |
| Unfavourable | EBIT < Interest | EPS falls faster than EBIT | **High** |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula examinable under this topic. Master these for guaranteed marks.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating Cash Flow Ratio | $\dfrac{CFO}{CL}$ | Ideal $> 1$; measures short-term debt coverage |
| 2 | Cash Flow to Sales | $\dfrac{CFO}{\text{Sales}}$ | Higher = better cash conversion |
| 3 | Cash Flow to Net Income | $\dfrac{CFO}{NI}$ | Healthy: $\geq 1$; flag if $< 0.8$ |
| 4 | Free Cash Flow (FCFF) | $CFO - CapEx$ | Available for debt & dividend |
| 5 | FCFE | $CFO - CapEx + \text{Net Borrowing}$ | Available for equity only |
| 6 | Cash Flow per Share | $\dfrac{CFO - D_p}{N}$ | Cash analog of EPS |
| 7 | DuPont ROE | $NPM \times AT \times EM$ | $EM = \dfrac{TA}{TE}$ |
| 8 | Return on Assets (ROA) | $\dfrac{NI}{TA}$ | Or $NPM \times AT$ |
| 9 | Return on Equity (ROE) | $\dfrac{NI}{TE}$ | Or $NPM \times AT \times EM$ |
| 10 | DFL (with Pref. Div.) | $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{1-t}}$ | $> 1$ when debt exists |
| 11 | DFL (no Pref. Div.) | $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$ | Basic form |
| 12 | Financial Break-even EBIT | $EBIT = I + \dfrac{D_p}{1-t}$ | Below → EPS falls |
| 13 | Combined Leverage (DTL) | $DOL \times DFL$ | Total business risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Notes for KTU:**
> - $DFL = 1$ → No debt (all-equity firm).
> - $DFL \to \infty$ → EBIT exactly equals interest (financial distress).
> - $CFO/NI < 1$ sustained for 3 years → accrual fraud red flag.
> - $EM = 1$ → No leverage; $EM = 2$ → 50% debt in capital structure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Project Finance & EPC Contracts**: Engineers evaluating infrastructure projects use **FCF projections** to determine DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio) — a CFS ratio variant.
- **Startups & Capex Decisions**: **FCF** dictates whether an engineering firm can self-fund R&D for next-gen prototypes without diluting equity.
- **Leverage in Manufacturing**: A capital-intensive firm (e.g., semiconductor fab) inherently has high EM; engineers must understand this when designing cost-of-capital models.
- **DuPont in Operations**: Quality engineers use **AT** (Asset Turnover) to gauge how efficiently a plant converts fixed assets into revenue.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DuPont Identity

Starting from the **base ratio**:

$$ROE = \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Total Equity}}$$

**Step 1**: Multiply numerator and denominator by **Sales**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROE &= \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Sales}} \times \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Equity}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Multiply and divide by **Total Assets** (insert $\frac{TA}{TA}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROE &= \frac{NI}{Sales} \times \frac{Sales}{TA} \times \frac{TA}{TE} \\
&= NPM \times AT \times EM
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The algebraic identity is exact — no approximation. The decomposition is *additive in log-form*:
> $$\ln(ROE) = \ln(NPM) + \ln(AT) + \ln(EM)$$
> This allows isolation of each driver's contribution to ROE changes — a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of DFL (with Preference Dividends)

**Starting point**: Percentage change in EPS vs. percentage change in EBIT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \frac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{\Delta EPS}{EPS}}{\dfrac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1**: Express EPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS &= \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - D_p}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Take partial derivative of EPS with respect to EBIT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial EPS}{\partial EBIT} &= \frac{(1-t)}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Substitute into the DFL ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \frac{\dfrac{(1-t)}{N}}{\dfrac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - D_p}{N}} \\
&= \frac{(1-t)}{EBIT(1-t) - I(1-t) - \dfrac{D_p}{N} \cdot N} \cdot N \\
&= \frac{EBIT(1-t)}{EBIT(1-t) - I(1-t) - D_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Simplify (divide numerator & denominator by $(1-t)$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result**: The presence of **preference dividends** $D_p$ *increases* DFL because they must be paid **after tax** (unlike interest, which is tax-deductible). Dividing by $(1-t)$ grosses up the dividend to its pre-tax equivalent.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — DuPont Analysis

> **Question**: A firm reports:
> - Net Income = ₹ 6,00,000
> - Sales = ₹ 40,00,000
> - Total Assets = ₹ 50,00,000
> - Total Equity = ₹ 20,00,000
> 
> Calculate the three-factor DuPont ROE and identify the dominant driver.

### Step 1: Net Profit Margin (NPM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPM &= \frac{NI}{Sales} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.15 = 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Asset Turnover (AT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AT &= \frac{Sales}{TA} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.80\ \text{times}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Equity Multiplier (EM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EM &= \frac{TA}{TE} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 2.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Composite ROE

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROE &= NPM \times AT \times EM \\
&= 0.15 \times 0.80 \times 2.50 \\
&= 0.30 = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Driver Decomposition (Logarithmic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(ROE) &= \ln(0.15) + \ln(0.80) + \ln(2.50) \\
&= (-1.897) + (-0.223) + (0.916) \\
&= -1.204
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification**: $e^{-1.204} = 0.30$ ✓

| Driver | Log Contribution | % Share |
|---|---|---|
| NPM | $-1.897$ | (loss-making absolute, but margin-driven) |
| AT | $-0.223$ | Underperforming |
| EM | $+0.916$ | **Dominant positive driver** |

> **Conclusion**: The 30% ROE is sustained primarily by **leverage (EM = 2.5)**. If interest rates rise, ROE will collapse — a classic leveraged firm warning.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Financial Leverage (DFL)

> **Question**: A company has:
> - EBIT = ₹ 10,00,000
> - Interest = ₹ 3,00,000
> - Tax Rate = 30%
> - 1,00,000 equity shares outstanding
> - No preference shares.
> 
> Calculate DFL, EPS, and interpret.

### Step 1: Compute EBT and EAT

$$
\begin{aligned}
EBT &= EBIT - I = 10{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \\
EAT &= EBT \times (1-t) = 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = 4{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute EPS

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS &= \frac{EAT}{N} = \frac{4{,}90{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹\ 4.90
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute DFL

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 1.4286
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta EPS &= 1.4286 \times \% \Delta EBIT
\end{aligned}
$$

> A **1% rise in EBIT** → **1.43% rise in EPS**.
> A **1% fall in EBIT** → **1.43% fall in EPS**.
> 
> **Break-even EBIT** = ₹ 3,00,000. Below this, EPS becomes zero or negative.

### Step 5: Sensitivity Table

| % Change in EBIT | New EBIT (₹) | New EPS (₹) | % Change in EPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| $+20\%$ | 12,00,000 | 6.30 | $+28.57\%$ |
| $+10\%$ | 11,00,000 | 5.60 | $+14.29\%$ |
| $0\%$ | 10,00,000 | 4.90 | $0\%$ |
| $-10\%$ | 9,00,000 | 4.20 | $-14.29\%$ |
| $-20\%$ | 8,00,000 | 3.50 | $-28.57\%$ |

> **Notice**: The EPS changes are exactly **1.4286 × EBIT change** — confirming the DFL.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Cash Flow Statement Ratios

> **Question**: From the following data:
> - Cash Flow from Operations (CFO) = ₹ 18,00,000
> - Net Sales = ₹ 60,00,000
> - Net Income = ₹ 9,00,000
> - Current Liabilities = ₹ 12,00,000
> - Capital Expenditures = ₹ 5,00,000
> - 5,00,000 equity shares
> 
> Calculate and interpret the key CFS ratios.

### Solution Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Operating CF Ratio} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.50 \\
\text{(ii) CF to Sales} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} = 30\% \\
\text{(iii) CF to Net Income} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = 2.00 \\
\text{(iv) Free Cash Flow} &= 18{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 13{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{(v) CF per Share} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000 - 0}{5{,}00{,}000} = ₹\ 3.60
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interpretation

- **Operating CF Ratio = 1.50** → Firm generates ₹1.50 of operating cash per ₹1 of current liabilities. **Strong liquidity.**
- **CF to Sales = 30%** → For every ₹100 of sales, ₹30 converts into operating cash. **Healthy.**
- **CF to Net Income = 2.0** → Earnings are **fully backed by cash**; no aggressive accruals. **Excellent quality.**
- **FCF = ₹ 13 lakh** → Significant discretionary cash for dividends/debt repayment.
- **CF per Share = ₹ 3.60** vs. EPS = ₹ 1.80 (assuming NI = 9L) → Cash-strong firm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    node12 -.uses.-> node13
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Trace from `node3` (EBIT) downward. **DFL magnifies** every change at `node3` into a proportionally larger change at `node11` (EPS).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define **Financial Leverage**. Explain the concept of **Trading on Equity** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark)**: Financial Leverage is the use of fixed-cost financing (debt and preference capital) to increase the return to equity shareholders. It is measured by the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL), given by $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$.

> **Trading on Equity (1 Mark)**: When the firm earns more on the borrowed funds than the cost of borrowing, the surplus accrues to equity holders, magnifying their return. This is *Trading on Equity* (or "leveraging").

> **Example (1 Mark)**: A firm borrows ₹ 10 lakh at 10% interest. It earns 15% on these funds via operations. The excess 5% (₹ 50,000) increases EPS without any additional equity investment — favourable leverage.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
List any **four Cash Flow Statement Ratios** and state the significance of the **Operating Cash Flow Ratio**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Four CFS Ratios (2 Marks)**:
> 1. Operating Cash Flow Ratio = $\dfrac{CFO}{CL}$
> 2. Cash Flow to Sales = $\dfrac{CFO}{Sales}$
> 3. Cash Flow to Net Income = $\dfrac{CFO}{NI}$
> 4. Free Cash Flow = $CFO - CapEx$

> **Significance (1 Mark)**: The Operating Cash Flow Ratio measures the firm's ability to pay off **current liabilities from operating cash alone**, without resorting to external financing. A value $> 1$ indicates healthy liquidity; $< 1$ is a red flag for short-term solvency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module 2 requires **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024]

#### (a) Explain the **DuPont Analysis** in detail. Discuss its **three-factor and five-factor models** with the help of an illustrative example. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark)**:
DuPont Analysis, developed by Donaldson Brown of the DuPont Corporation (USA) in the 1920s, is a framework that **decomposes Return on Equity (ROE)** into its constituent drivers. This allows management to identify whether profitability, efficiency, or leverage is contributing most to shareholder returns.

**2. Three-Factor DuPont (3 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROE &= \frac{NI}{TE} = \frac{NI}{Sales} \times \frac{Sales}{TA} \times \frac{TA}{TE} \\
&= NPM \times AT \times EM
\end{aligned}
$$

- **NPM (Net Profit Margin)** = Profitability per rupee of sales.
- **AT (Asset Turnover)** = Sales per rupee of assets (efficiency).
- **EM (Equity Multiplier)** = Leverage factor.

**3. Five-Factor DuPont (Extended) (2 Marks)**:
The five-factor model further decomposes NPM and AT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPM &= \frac{EBIT}{Sales} \times \frac{EBIT - I}{EBIT} \times \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-t)}{EBIT - I} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> That is, $ROE = (\text{Tax Burden}) \times (\text{Interest Burden}) \times (\text{EBIT Margin}) \times (\text{AT}) \times (\text{EM})$

**4. Illustrative Example (1 Mark)**:
A firm: $NI = ₹6L$, $Sales = ₹40L$, $TA = ₹50L$, $TE = ₹20L$.
- $NPM = 15\%$, $AT = 0.80$, $EM = 2.50$
- $ROE = 15\% \times 0.80 \times 2.50 = 30\%$

> **Conclusion**: ROE is sustained primarily by leverage. If interest rates rise, ROE will erode rapidly — the firm is **highly geared**.

---

#### (b) The following data pertains to M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024. (7 Marks)

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Net Sales | 80,00,000 |
| Cash Flow from Operations (CFO) | 24,00,000 |
| Net Income | 10,00,000 |
| Current Liabilities | 16,00,000 |
| Capital Expenditure | 8,00,000 |
| Preference Dividend Paid | 2,00,000 |
| Equity Shares Outstanding | 5,00,000 |

**Calculate and interpret**:
(i) Operating Cash Flow Ratio
(ii) Cash Flow to Net Income Ratio
(iii) Free Cash Flow
(iv) Cash Flow per Share

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Operating Cash Flow Ratio**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{CFO}{CL} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (½ Mark)**: The firm generates ₹1.50 of operating cash for every ₹1 of current liabilities. **Strong short-term liquidity.**

**(ii) Cash Flow to Net Income Ratio**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{CFO}{NI} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 2.40
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (½ Mark)**: Operating cash flow is **2.4× the reported net income** — indicating **excellent accrual quality**. Profits are backed by real cash, not aggressive accounting.

**(iii) Free Cash Flow**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCF &= CFO - CapEx \\
&= 24{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹\ 16{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (½ Mark)**: ₹16 lakh is available for **debt repayment, dividend distribution, or reinvestment** after maintaining productive assets.

**(iv) Cash Flow per Share**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF\ per\ Share &= \frac{CFO - D_p}{N} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
&= ₹\ 4.40
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (½ Mark)**: Cash per share (₹4.40) is **higher than accounting EPS (₹2.00)** — strong cash-backed equity return.

**Final Comment (1 Mark)**: Kerala Engineering Ltd. demonstrates **robust financial health** — high liquidity, strong earnings quality, and substantial free cash for growth.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

#### (a) Discuss the concept and types of **Financial Leverage**. Explain the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** with formula and interpretation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 Marks)**:
Financial Leverage refers to the **proportion of fixed-cost financing** (debt and preference capital) in the firm's capital structure. It arises whenever a firm uses funds bearing **fixed financial charges** — interest on debt and dividend on preference shares — irrespective of the level of operating profit.

The presence of fixed charges creates a **multiplier effect**: a change in EBIT produces a magnified change in EPS.

**2. Types of Leverage Position (2 Marks)**:

| Position | Condition | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Favourable** | $ROA > Cost\ of\ Debt$ | EPS rises faster than EBIT |
| **Neutral** | $ROA = Cost\ of\ Debt$ | EPS unaffected |
| **Unfavourable** | $ROA < Cost\ of\ Debt$ | EPS falls faster than EBIT |

**3. Degree of Financial Leverage — Formula (2 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFL &= \frac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{1-t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> In the absence of preference shares: $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$

**4. Interpretation (1 Mark)**:
