- $DFL = 1$ → All-equity firm.
- $DFL > 1$ → Debt amplifies EPS changes.
- $DFL \to \infty$ → EBIT equals fixed charges (financial distress zone).

> **Practical Use**: KTU often asks to compute DFL under multiple debt scenarios to compare leverage risk.

---

#### (b) **M/s Trivandrum Robotics Pvt. Ltd.** has the following capital structure and EBIT estimates. (7 Marks)

| Scenario | EBIT (₹) | Total Debt (₹) | Interest Rate | Pref. Shares (₹) | Pref. Dividend Rate | Tax Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plan A | 12,00,000 | 20,00,000 | 10% | Nil | – | 30% |
| Plan B | 12,00,000 | 30,00,000 | 11% | 10,00,000 | 8% | 30% |

**Equity shares outstanding = 2,00,000 in both cases.**

**Required**:
(i) Calculate **DFL** under both plans.
(ii) Compute **EPS** under both plans.
(iii) Advise which plan is **more financially stable**.

**Model Solution:**

### **Plan A — Debt Only**

**(i) DFL Calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 10\% = ₹\ 2{,}00{,}000 \\
D_{pA} &= 0 \\
DFL_A &= \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I_A} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) EPS Calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EBT_A &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \\
EAT_A &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7{,}00{,}000 \\
EPS_A &= \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = ₹\ 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Plan B — Debt + Preference Shares**

**(i) DFL Calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 11\% = ₹\ 3{,}30{,}000 \\
D_p &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 8\% = ₹\ 80{,}000 \\
DFL_B &= \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I_B - \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}} \\
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}30{,}000 - \dfrac{80{,}000}{0.70}} \\
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}30{,}000 - 1{,}14{,}285.71} \\
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{7{,}55{,}714.29} \\
&= 1.588
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) EPS Calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EBT_B &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}30{,}000 = 8{,}70{,}000 \\
EAT_B &= 8{,}70{,}000 \times 0.70 = 6{,}09{,}000 \\
EAT_{Available} &= 6{,}09{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 5{,}29{,}000 \\
EPS_B &= \frac{5{,}29{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = ₹\ 2.645
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparison Table

| Metric | Plan A | Plan B |
|---|---|---|
| DFL | 1.20 | 1.59 |
| EPS | ₹ 3.50 | ₹ 2.65 |
| Fixed Charges | ₹ 2,00,000 | ₹ 4,14,286 (grossed-up) |

**(iii) Recommendation (1 Mark)**:
Although Plan B offers higher DFL (greater magnification potential if EBIT grows), its **lower EPS at current EBIT** and **higher fixed charges** make it **riskier**. Plan A is **more financially stable** at the current EBIT level of ₹12 lakh.

> **Caveat**: If EBIT is expected to grow significantly (say $> ₹20$ lakh), Plan B's higher DFL may yield superior EPS due to magnified returns — this is the classic **trade-off between risk and return** that KTU expects students to articulate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Operating Leverage with Financial Leverage**: DOL involves fixed *operating* costs; DFL involves fixed *financing* costs. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-t)$ denominator for Preference Dividends**: Preference dividends are paid from **after-tax profits**. The formula $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{1-t}}$ requires grossing-up. Using $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - D_p}$ directly is a **common 2-mark deduction**.
> 
> 3. **Misinterpreting CF to Net Income Ratio**: A ratio of $2.0$ is **good**, not bad. Students often think "more cash than profit" is suspicious — it actually indicates **strong accrual quality**.
> 
> 4. **Wrong DuPont Driver Identification**: Many students simply compute ROE and stop. KTU requires **logarithmic decomposition** to attribute ROE change to NPM, AT, or EM.
> 
> 5. **Using Total Cash Flow instead of CFO**: The ratios in this module use **Cash Flow from Operations (CFO)** specifically — not total cash flow or free cash flow. Substituting wrong numerators is a frequent error.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the Interpretation Step**: Computing without stating "what does this mean for the firm" loses 1–2 marks per sub-question. Always end with a one-line **interpretive comment**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exams):**

- ✅ **CFS Ratios** use **CFO** (not total cash flow) as the primary numerator.
- ✅ **Operating Cash Flow Ratio** benchmark: **$> 1$** is healthy.
- ✅ **CF to Net Income Ratio** benchmark: **$\geq 1$** indicates earnings quality; flag if persistently $< 0.8$.
- ✅ **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** = CFO $-$ CapEx (the most-watched metric by investors).
- ✅ **DuPont 3-Factor** = $NPM \times AT \times EM$.
- ✅ **EM = TA / TE** = measure of leverage within DuPont.
- ✅ **ROE = ROA × EM** — leverage multiplies asset productivity into equity returns.
- ✅ **Financial Leverage** = use of **fixed financial charges** to magnify EPS.
- ✅ **DFL (with pref. div.)** = $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}}$.
- ✅ **DFL (without pref. div.)** = $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$.
- ✅ **Break-even EBIT** = $I + \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}$ — below this, EPS is zero/negative.
- ✅ **DFL = 1** → all-equity; **DFL $> 1$** → debt present; **DFL $\to \infty$** → financial distress.
- ✅ **Favourable leverage**: ROA $>$ Cost of Debt → Trading on Equity.
- ✅ **Unfavourable leverage**: ROA $<$ Cost of Debt → Trading against Equity.
- ✅ Always **interpret** every ratio computed — KTU rewards interpretation marks.
- ✅ Use **logarithmic DuPont decomposition** to identify the dominant ROE driver.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Leverage and Composite Leverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Leverage and Composite Leverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operating Leverage (OL) — The Academic Definition

**Operating Leverage** is the presence of **fixed operating costs** in a firm's cost structure that magnifies the effect of a change in sales revenue on the firm's **Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)**, also known as Operating Profit.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Operating Leverage is a measure of the sensitivity of **EBIT** to changes in **Sales (Revenue)**. A firm with high operating leverage has a large proportion of fixed operating costs relative to variable costs, which causes profits to swing more dramatically with sales fluctuations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** is the numerical multiplier that quantifies this sensitivity. A DOL of 3 means that a 10% change in sales will produce a 30% change in EBIT.

## 1.2 Composite Leverage (CL) — The Academic Definition

**Composite Leverage** (also called **Total Leverage** or **Combined Leverage**) is the combined effect of both **Operating Leverage** and **Financial Leverage** on the firm's **Earnings Per Share (EPS)**. It measures the sensitivity of EPS to changes in Sales Revenue, taking into account both the firm's cost structure (fixed vs. variable) and its capital structure (debt vs. equity).

> [!NOTE]
> Composite Leverage = Operating Leverage × Financial Leverage. It captures the **total business risk + total financial risk** borne by equity shareholders.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle with gears**:
- The **front chainring** (large gear) represents your **Operating Leverage** — it amplifies the rotation of your pedaling force. If you press the pedal just a little, the wheel turns a lot, but it is hard to start moving on a hill (high fixed costs).
- The **rear sprocket** represents **Financial Leverage** — debt amplifies your gains but adds extra "drag" through interest.
- The **overall speed of the bicycle (distance covered per pedal stroke)** represents **Composite Leverage** — the final combined magnification of your efforts.

A firm with high operating leverage is like a **heavy cargo truck**: small bumps in sales cause big swings in profit because the truck carries huge fixed fuel, depreciation, and salary loads regardless of how far it drives. A bicycle courier (low OL) has flexible costs and smoother profit swings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:** OL = The "Gear Ratio" of the firm's cost structure. CL = The "Total Gear Ratio" including both gears (cost + debt). High leverage = more amplification = more risk AND more potential reward.

## 1.4 Key Metrics Highlighted

- **EBIT** = Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (Operating Profit)
- **EBT** = Earnings Before Taxes (Profit After Interest, Before Tax)
- **EPS** = Earnings Per Share (Profit attributable to each equity share)
- **Contribution** = Sales − Variable Costs (also called **Total Contribution**)
- **Fixed Operating Costs (F)** = Rent, Depreciation, Salaries, Insurance, etc.
- **Fixed Financial Costs (I)** = Interest on Debt, Preference Dividend
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Leverage Exists — The Core Logic

A firm's total costs are split into two categories:
1. **Fixed Costs (F)** — Do NOT change with output (rent, salary, depreciation).
2. **Variable Costs (V)** — Change directly and proportionally with output (raw materials, direct labor, sales commission).

When sales rise, variable costs rise, but fixed costs stay the same. This means **every additional rupee of sales above the breakeven point contributes almost entirely to profit**. Conversely, when sales fall, fixed costs still must be paid, causing profits to drop sharply. This asymmetric behavior of fixed costs is the source of **Operating Leverage**.

## 2.2 Formal Definitions Used in KTU Valuation

- **Operating Leverage** exists due to fixed operating costs in the income statement.
- **Financial Leverage** exists due to fixed financial charges (interest, preference dividend) in the income statement.
- **Composite Leverage** = Operating Leverage + Financial Leverage effect combined multiplicatively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test: *"A firm has no fixed costs — what is its DOL?"* Answer: **DOL = 1**. This is because without fixed operating costs, the % change in EBIT exactly equals the % change in Sales. This is a high-yield exam point.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables Explained |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$ | Contribution = Sales − Variable Costs; EBIT = Contribution − Fixed Costs |
| 2 | DOL (Alternate Form) | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Percentage Change in EBIT}}{\text{Percentage Change in Sales}}$ | Direct elasticity form |
| 3 | **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** | $\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}}$ | EBT = EBIT − Interest (and Pref. Dividend) |
| 4 | DFL (Alternate Form) | $\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{Percentage Change in EPS}}{\text{Percentage Change in EBIT}}$ | Direct elasticity form |
| 5 | **Degree of Composite Leverage (DCL or DTC)** | $\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$ | Total combined leverage |
| 6 | DCL (Direct Formula) | $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$ | Shortcut — direct from sales to EPS |
| 7 | DCL (Alternate Form) | $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{\text{Percentage Change in EPS}}{\text{Percentage Change in Sales}}$ | Direct elasticity form |
| 8 | Contribution | $\text{Contribution} = S - V$ | S = Sales, V = Variable Costs |
| 9 | EBIT | $\text{EBIT} = S - V - F$ | F = Fixed Operating Costs |
| 10 | EBT | $\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - I$ | I = Interest (and Pref. Dividend) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Rule:** If there are **no fixed costs**, DOL = 1. If there are **no interest/financial charges**, DFL = 1. If both are absent, DCL = 1. KTU examiners love this edge case.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Capital-intensive industries** (telecom, airlines, steel, automobile manufacturing) have high DOL — they need huge revenue to cover depreciation and fixed salaries.
- **Asset-light industries** (IT services, consulting) have low DOL — their costs are mostly variable (people billed per hour).
- **Startup founders** must know DCL — too much debt + high fixed costs = extreme EPS volatility, which scares equity investors.
- **Banks and credit rating agencies** use DCL to assess a borrower's total risk profile before approving loans.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of DOL

Let:
- $S$ = Sales (in units × price, or total revenue)
- $V$ = Variable Costs (total)
- $F$ = Fixed Operating Costs
- $Q$ = Quantity sold (units)
- $P$ = Price per unit
- $V_p$ = Variable cost per unit

Then:
$$\text{Sales } S = P \cdot Q$$

$$\text{Variable Costs } V = V_p \cdot Q$$

$$\text{Contribution } C = S - V = (P - V_p) \cdot Q$$

$$\text{EBIT} = S - V - F = C - F$$

**Step 1:** Consider a small change in sales $\Delta S$. Since $V$ varies proportionally with $S$, we have $\Delta V = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \Delta S$.

**Step 2:** Fixed costs $F$ do NOT change, so $\Delta F = 0$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the change in EBIT is:
$$\Delta \text{EBIT} = \Delta S - \Delta V - \Delta F = \Delta S - \frac{V}{S} \cdot \Delta S - 0$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\Delta S$:
$$\Delta \text{EBIT} = \Delta S \cdot \left(1 - \frac{V}{S}\right) = \Delta S \cdot \left(\frac{S - V}{S}\right) = \Delta S \cdot \frac{C}{S}$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio of percentage changes:
$$\frac{\Delta \text{EBIT}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{\Delta S \cdot (C / S)}{C - F} = \frac{\Delta S}{S} \cdot \frac{C}{C - F} = \frac{\Delta S}{S} \cdot \frac{C}{\text{EBIT}}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging:
$$\frac{\Delta \text{EBIT} / \text{EBIT}}{\Delta S / S} = \frac{C}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{C}{C - F}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of DCL = DOL × DFL

**Step 1:** From the formula sheet, we know:
$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EBIT}}{\% \Delta S}$$

**Step 2:** And:
$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EPS}}{\% \Delta \text{EBIT}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiplying:
$$\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EBIT}}{\% \Delta S} \times \frac{\% \Delta \text{EPS}}{\% \Delta \text{EBIT}}$$

**Step 4:** The $\% \Delta \text{EBIT}$ terms cancel:
$$\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EPS}}{\% \Delta S} = \text{DCL}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the direct formulas:
$$\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{C}{\text{EBIT}} \times \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{C}{\text{EBT}}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EPS}}{\% \Delta S}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A firm sells a product at ₹100 per unit. Variable cost is ₹60 per unit. Fixed operating costs are ₹20,000. Interest expense is ₹5,000. The firm sold 1,000 units. Calculate DOL, DFL, and DCL. If sales rise by 10%, what is the % change in EPS?

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the Income Statement figures.**

$$\text{Sales } S = 1{,}000 \times 100 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Variable Costs } V = 1{,}000 \times 60 = ₹60{,}000$$

$$\text{Contribution } C = 1{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹40{,}000$$

$$\text{EBIT} = C - F = 40{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹20{,}000$$

$$\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - I = 20{,}000 - 5{,}000 = ₹15{,}000$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute DOL, DFL, DCL.**

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{C}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{40{,}000}{20{,}000} = 2.0$$

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{20{,}000}{15{,}000} = 1.333$$

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = 2.0 \times 1.333 = 2.667$$

Or directly: $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{C}{\text{EBT}} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{15{,}000} = 2.667$ ✓

**Solution — Step 3: Compute % change in EPS for 10% sales rise.**

$$\% \Delta \text{EPS} = \text{DCL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.667 \times 10\% = 26.67\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 10% rise in sales causes EPS to rise by 26.67% due to the combined amplifying effect of fixed costs (operating leverage) and interest (financial leverage).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Impact Table

The table below shows how a 20% change in sales affects EBIT and EPS in our example firm.

| Scenario | % Change in Sales | New Sales | New Contribution | New EBIT | New EBT | % Δ EBIT | % Δ EPS |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|------------------|----------|---------|----------|---------|
| **Base Case** | 0% | ₹1,00,000 | ₹40,000 | ₹20,000 | ₹15,000 | 0% | 0% |
| **Sales Rise** | +20% | ₹1,20,000 | ₹48,000 | ₹28,000 | ₹23,000 | +40% | +53.33% |
| **Sales Fall** | −20% | ₹80,000 | ₹32,000 | ₹12,000 | ₹7,000 | −40% | −53.33% |

**Verification using formulas:**

For sales rise of +20%:
$$\% \Delta \text{EBIT} = \text{DOL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.0 \times 20\% = 40\% \checkmark$$

$$\% \Delta \text{EPS} = \text{DCL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.667 \times 20\% = 53.33\% \checkmark$$

This confirms the leverage multipliers behave symmetrically in both directions — a critical KTU valuation insight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Leverage Architecture Flow — Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the conceptual flow from Sales to EPS, showing how each leverage type amplifies changes at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sales Revenue S] --> B[Minus Variable Costs V]
    B --> C[Contribution C]
    C --> D[Minus Fixed Operating Costs F]
    D --> E[EBIT Operating Profit]
    E --> F[Minus Interest I]
    F --> G[EBT Profit Before Tax]
    G --> H[Minus Tax T]
    H --> I[Net Profit After Tax PAT]
    I --> J[Divided by Number of Shares N]
    J --> K[EPS Earnings Per Share]
    L[DOL Operating Leverage] --> C
    L --> E
    M[DFL Financial Leverage] --> E
    M --> G
    N[DCL Composite Leverage] --> C
    N --> G
    O[Sales Change Percent] --> L
    O --> N
    P[EBIT Change Percent] --> M
    Q[EPS Change Percent] --> N
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **left-side arrows** (L, M, N) show where each leverage multiplier operates.
- DOL operates between **Contribution and EBIT** (fixed operating cost block).
- DFL operates between **EBIT and EBT** (interest block).
- DCL operates end-to-end between **Contribution and EBT** (or Sales and EPS).

## 4.2 Decision-Making Tree for Leverage Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Analyze a Firm's Risk Profile] --> Q1{Does the firm have significant fixed operating costs?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| HighOL[High DOL — Operating Risk High]
    Q1 -->|No| LowOL[Low DOL approx equals 1]
    HighOL --> Q2{Does the firm use significant debt financing?}
    LowOL --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| HighCL[High DCL — Total Risk Very High]
    Q2 -->|No| LowCL[Low DCL — Conservative Capital Structure]
    HighCL --> Action1[Risk Mitigation: Reduce F or I, Diversify Revenue Streams]
    LowCL --> Action2[Strategy: Could leverage up moderately to enhance EPS]
```

## 4.3 Functional Summary Table

| Leverage Type | Input Variable | Output Variable | Affected Stage | Risk Type |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|
| Operating (DOL) | % Change in Sales | % Change in EBIT | Sales → EBIT | Business Risk |
| Financial (DFL) | % Change in EBIT | % Change in EPS | EBIT → EBT | Financial Risk |
| Composite (DCL) | % Change in Sales | % Change in EPS | Sales → EPS | Total Risk |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Operating Leverage." What does a high DOL indicate about a firm's risk profile?**

**Model Answer:**

Operating Leverage is the presence of fixed operating costs in a firm's cost structure that causes a proportional magnification in the change of EBIT relative to the change in Sales. Quantitatively, $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \dfrac{\% \Delta \text{EBIT}}{\% \Delta S}$.

A **high DOL** indicates that the firm has a large proportion of fixed operating costs relative to variable costs. This means:
- Small changes in sales produce **large changes in EBIT** (high sensitivity).
- The firm carries **high business risk** because if sales fall below the breakeven point, fixed costs magnify losses.
- The firm has high **operating risk** and is more vulnerable to economic downturns.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between Operating Leverage and Financial Leverage. How are they combined into Composite Leverage?**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Operating Leverage | Financial Leverage |
|-------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Source | Fixed Operating Costs | Fixed Financial Charges (Interest, Pref. Dividend) |
| Effect On | EBIT (Operating Profit) | EPS / EBT |
| Formula | $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}}$ |
| Risk Type | Business Risk | Financial Risk |

**Composite Leverage (DCL)** combines both multiplicatively:
$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{\% \Delta \text{EPS}}{\% \Delta S}$$

It captures the **total risk** (business + financial) borne by equity shareholders.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A firm has the following data:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Sales (10,000 units @ ₹50) | 5,00,000 |
| Variable Costs | 3,00,000 |
| Fixed Operating Costs | 1,00,000 |
| Interest Expense | 20,000 |
| Income Tax Rate | 30% |

**(a) Calculate the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL), Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL), and Degree of Composite Leverage (DCL).** (7 Marks)

**(b) If the firm expects a 25% increase in sales, what will be the new EBIT, EBT, and EPS (assuming 50,000 equity shares outstanding)?** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Compute the Income Statement figures.**

$$\text{Contribution} = S - V = 5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{EBIT} = C - F = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - I = 1{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹80{,}000$$

**Step 2: Apply leverage formulas.**

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{C}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 2.0$$

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 1.25$$

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = 2.0 \times 1.25 = 2.5$$

(Or directly: $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 2.5$ ✓)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Use the leverage multipliers to find new EBIT.**

$$\% \Delta \text{EBIT} = \text{DOL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.0 \times 25\% = 50\%$$

$$\text{New EBIT} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.50) = ₹1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2: Find new EBT (interest is fixed).**

$$\text{New EBT} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹1{,}30{,}000$$

**Step 3: Find new PAT (after 30% tax).**

$$\text{New PAT} = 1{,}30{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 1{,}30{,}000 \times 0.70 = ₹91{,}000$$

**Step 4: Find new EPS.**

$$\text{New EPS} = \frac{91{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹1.82$$

**Verification using DCL:**

$$\% \Delta \text{EPS} = \text{DCL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.5 \times 25\% = 62.5\%$$

$$\text{Original EPS} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 0.70}{50{,}000} = \frac{56{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹1.12$$

$$\text{New EPS} = 1.12 \times 1.625 = ₹1.82 \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The financial data of M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. is as follows:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Sales | 8,00,000 |
| Variable Costs (60% of Sales) | 4,80,000 |
| Fixed Costs | 2,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures of ₹1,00,000 | — |

**(a) Calculate DOL, DFL, and DCL. Also compute the Breakeven Point (BEP) in rupees.** (7 Marks)

**(b) If sales decrease by 15%, calculate the new EPS (assuming 1,00,000 equity shares and 30% tax). What is your observation regarding the firm's risk?** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Compute the basic figures.**

$$\text{Contribution } C = 8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

$$\text{Interest } I = 10\% \text{ of } 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}000$$

$$\text{EBIT} = C - F = 3{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}20{,}000$$

$$\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - I = 1{,}20{,}000 - 10{,}000 = ₹1{,}10{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute leverage ratios.**

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = 2.667$$

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}10{,}000} = 1.0909$$

$$\text{DCL} = 2.667 \times 1.0909 = 2.909$$

**Step 3: Compute Breakeven Point (BEP).**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{C}{S} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%$$

$$\text{BEP (₹)} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Use DCL to find % change in EPS for 15% sales drop.**

$$\% \Delta \text{EPS} = \text{DCL} \times \% \Delta S = 2.909 \times (-15\%) = -43.64\%$$

**Step 2: Compute original EPS.**

$$\text{Original PAT} = 1{,}10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 77{,}000$$

$$\text{Original EPS} = \frac{77{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹0.77$$

**Step 3: Compute new EPS.**

$$\text{New EPS} = 0.77 \times (1 - 0.4364) = 0.77 \times 0.5636 = ₹0.434$$

**Alternative Direct Verification:**

New Sales $= 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.85 = 6{,}80{,}000$

New Variable Costs $= 6{,}80{,}000 \times 0.60 = 4{,}08{,}000$

New Contribution $= 6{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}08{,}000 = 2{,}72{,}000$

New EBIT $= 2{,}72{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 72{,}000$

New EBT $= 72{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 62{,}000$

New PAT $= 62{,}000 \times 0.70 = 43{,}400$

New EPS $= 43{,}400 / 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹0.434$ ✓

**Observation:** A mere 15% drop in sales caused EPS to crash by 43.64% (from ₹0.77 to ₹0.434). The firm has **high business risk** (DOL = 2.667) and **moderate financial risk** (DFL = 1.09). The DCL of 2.909 signals significant total risk — sales volatility translates into large EPS volatility, and the firm is operating above breakeven (₹5,00,000) but has limited margin of safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing the order:** DOL uses Contribution/EBIT, NOT Sales/EBIT. Many students write $\frac{S}{\text{EBIT}}$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract Interest** to get EBT before applying DFL. The formula needs EBT (Profit After Interest, Before Tax), not PAT.
> 3. **Mixing up "no fixed cost" implications:** Writing DOL = 0 instead of DOL = 1 when F = 0. The correct interpretation: without fixed costs, a 1% change in sales causes exactly 1% change in EBIT.
> 4. **Ignoring the percentage change in units vs. amount:** When sales rise by 25%, both units sold AND price contribute. KTU questions usually specify % change in sales revenue (which is what DCL/DOL measure).
> 5. **Forgetting the DCL = Contribution/EBT shortcut:** KTU valuators award bonus marks for showing the direct formula verification.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Operating Leverage** arises from **fixed operating costs**; it amplifies changes in **Sales → EBIT**.
- ✅ **Financial Leverage** arises from **fixed financial charges** (interest, pref. dividend); it amplifies changes in **EBIT → EPS/EBT**.
- ✅ **Composite Leverage = DOL × DFL**; amplifies changes in **Sales → EPS**.
- ✅ **Key Formulas (memorize cold):**
   - $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$
   - $\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}}$
   - $\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$
- ✅ **Edge cases to remember:**
   - No fixed operating costs → DOL = 1
   - No interest/financial charges → DFL = 1
   - No leverage of any type → DCL = 1
- ✅ **Income Statement order (always remember):** Sales − Variable Costs = **Contribution** → − Fixed Costs = **EBIT** → − Interest = **EBT** → − Tax = **PAT** → ÷ Shares = **EPS**.
- ✅ **High DOL/DFL = Higher Risk AND Higher Reward** — leverage works symmetrically in both directions (gains AND losses).
- ✅ **Breakeven Point (BEP)** in ₹ = Fixed Costs ÷ P/V Ratio, where P/V Ratio = Contribution ÷ Sales.
- ✅ **KTU exam pattern tip:** Always state the formula, plug in values, and verify using the alternate direct formula (e.g., DCL = Contribution/EBT) for full marks.
- ✅ **Interpretation framework:** A firm with DCL > 3 is considered **highly risky**; DCL between 1.5–3 is moderate; DCL close to 1 is conservative.
- ✅ **Practical application:** DOL is high in capital-intensive industries; DFL is high in debt-heavy firms; DCL combines both. Managers use these to plan production capacity and capital structure decisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Structure - Meaning, Scope and Patterns of Capital Structure; Capital Structure Decisions; Capital Structure Theories/Approaches - Net Income Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital Structure - Meaning, Scope and Patterns of Capital Structure; Capital Structure Decisions; Capital Structure Theories/Approaches - Net Income Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CAPITAL STRUCTURE FUNDAMENTALS

## 1.1 Capital Structure — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **Capital Structure** refers to the specific combination (or mix) of **long-term sources of funds** employed by a firm to finance its assets and overall operations. It represents the permanent financing of the company, comprising **long-term debt (LTD), preference share capital (PSC), and equity share capital (along with retained earnings)**.

In simpler KTU-board language, capital structure is the **right-hand side of the balance sheet (excluding current liabilities)** — i.e., how a firm chooses to divide its long-term capital between **owned funds (equity)** and **borrowed funds (debt)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Budget"

Imagine a student running a small startup with ₹1,00,000 to buy equipment.

- **Option A:** Use all ₹1,00,000 of his own savings → *No debt, no interest, full ownership, but limited capital*.
- **Option B:** Use ₹40,000 of his own money + borrow ₹60,000 from his uncle at 10% interest → *He controls the business, but must pay ₹6,000/year interest regardless of profit*.
- **Option C:** Bring in a partner who contributes ₹60,000 for 40% ownership → *No interest, but profits are shared forever*.

Each option is a different **capital structure**. The student must decide the *ideal* mix that minimizes cost and maximizes his wealth. This decision is the essence of capital structure planning.

### 1.3 Scope of Capital Structure

The scope of capital structure planning covers **four major decision areas**:

| S.No | Decision Area | What It Involves | KTU Relevance |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Type of Security** | Equity, Preference, Debentures, Bonds | Decides risk-return profile |
| 2 | **Proportion of Debt vs Equity** | Debt-equity ratio determination | Affects leverage & EPS |
| 3 | **Cost of Capital Minimization** | WACC optimization | Maximizes firm value |
| 4 | **External Environment** | Market conditions, tax rates, investor sentiment | Real-world constraint |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Capital structure decisions are **irreversible** in the short run and influence the firm's **cost of capital**, **earnings per share (EPS)**, and ultimately the **market value of the firm (V)**.

### 1.4 Patterns of Capital Structure

Patterns refer to the **theoretical/practical models** of debt-equity combinations observed in firms. KTU recognizes the following major patterns:

**Pattern 1: Equity Dominant (Conservative Pattern)**
- Heavy reliance on **equity share capital** and retained earnings.
- Very low or zero debt.
- Example: Early-stage IT startups, family-owned businesses.
- *Lower risk, lower return, no financial leverage.*

**Pattern 2: Debt Dominant (Aggressive Pattern)**
- Heavy reliance on **long-term debt** (debentures, bonds, term loans).
- Minimal equity base.
- Example: Infrastructure, real estate, capital-intensive industries.
- *Higher financial risk, higher EPS in good times.*

**Pattern 3: Balanced/Moderate Pattern (Optimal Pattern)**
- A **judicious mix** of debt and equity.
- Aims to minimize WACC and maximize firm value.
- Example: Mature manufacturing companies (e.g., Maruti, Infosys in mid-cycle).
- *Recommended under Net Income Approach.*

**Pattern 4: Pyramid Pattern (Hierarchical)**
- Uses debt at every level — parent holds subsidiary using subsidiary's own debt.
- Common in conglomerates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **optimal capital structure** is the one that **maximizes the market value of the firm** and **minimizes the weighted average cost of capital (WACC)**.

---

## 1.5 Capital Structure vs. Financial Structure

Students frequently confuse these two terms — this is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

| Aspect | Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Long-term sources of funds | All sources of funds (long-term + short-term) |
| **Components** | Equity, Preference, Long-term Debt | Capital Structure + Current Liabilities + Current Assets |
| **Time Horizon** | Permanent/Long-term | Entire balance sheet |
| **KTU Keyword** | Permanent financing | Total financing |

---

## 1.6 Capital Structure Decisions — The Core Question

The fundamental decision in capital structure planning is:

> *"Given a firm's assets and operations, what is the optimal mix of debt and equity that maximizes shareholder wealth?"*

### 1.7 Factors Determining Capital Structure (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners frequently test these under "List any 6 factors affecting capital structure":**

1. **Leverage (Debt-Equity Ratio)** — Higher debt → Higher financial risk but lower WACC up to a point.
2. **Cost of Capital** — Optimal structure minimizes WACC.
3. **Earnings Stability** — Stable firms can afford more debt.
4. **Cash Flow Position** — Predictable cash flows support debt servicing.
5. **Tax Shield on Interest** — Interest is tax-deductible, providing a benefit.
6. **Market Conditions** — Bull vs. bear market influences investor preference.
7. **Firm's Risk Profile** — Asset beta, business risk.
8. **Control Considerations** — Equity dilutes ownership; debt does not.
9. **Floatation Costs** — Cost of issuing new securities.
10. **Flexibility** — Ability to raise future funds.

---

## 1.8 Assumptions Underlying Capital Structure Theories (KTU Must-Know)

All classical theories (NI, NOI, MM, Traditional) share these baseline assumptions:

1. **Perfect capital markets** — no transaction costs, no floatation costs.
2. **All investors are rational** — they act on full information.
3. **Firms pay 100% of earnings as dividends** — or equivalently, retention doesn't affect value.
4. **Earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT)** is constant and unaffected by financing.
5. **Firms have only two sources of funds** — perpetual debt (Kd) and equity (Ke).
6. **Risk profile of the firm is constant** — business risk is unchanged.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Classical Theories of Capital Structure

KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three principal theories**, each giving a different answer to "How does debt affect firm value?"

| Theory | Proponent | Core Idea | Value of Firm (V) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Net Income (NI) Approach** | Durand (1952) | Firm value can be increased by increasing debt | Increases with leverage |
| **Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach** | Durand (1952) | Firm value is independent of capital structure | Constant regardless of leverage |
| **Traditional Approach** | Modigliani-Miller (early) | Optimal capital structure exists at a point | Increases up to optimum, then falls |
| **MM Approach (without tax)** | Modigliani & Miller (1958) | Firm value unaffected by leverage | Constant (similar to NOI) |
| **MM Approach (with tax)** | Modigliani & Miller (1963) | Debt creates tax shield, increasing value | Increases linearly with debt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In Module 3, the **focus is the Net Income (NI) Approach**. Know it inside-out, including graphical analysis, assumptions, and numerical problem-solving pattern.

---

## 2.2 The Net Income (NI) Approach — Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> The **Net Income Approach**, propounded by **David Durand (1952)**, states that a firm can **increase its total market value (V) and lower its overall cost of capital (Ko)** by **increasing the proportion of debt in its capital structure**. The approach assumes that both the **cost of debt (Kd)** and the **cost of equity (Ke)** are **constant and independent of the degree of leverage**.

### 2.3 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The NI Approach is built on **three pillars of constant values**:

**Pillar 1: Cost of Debt (Kd) is Constant**
- Lenders view the firm's **business risk as unchanged** by financing decisions.
- Therefore, the interest rate demanded remains fixed at **Kd** for all debt levels.

**Pillar 2: Cost of Equity (Ke) is Constant**
- Shareholders also perceive **no change in business risk** as debt rises.
- So the equity capitalization rate remains fixed at **Ke**.

**Pillar 3: Use of Market Value Weights**
- The total market value **V = S + D**, where S = market value of equity and D = market value of debt.
- Weighted Average Cost of Capital:

$$K_o = \frac{S}{V} \times K_e + \frac{D}{V} \times K_d$$

- Since both Ke and Kd are constants, **as D increases (and D/V rises)**, the weighted average Ko **falls** (because debt is cheaper than equity).

### 2.4 The Leverage Effect — "Why Cheaper Debt Wins"

Because **Kd < Ke** (debt is always cheaper than equity — interest is contractual, tax-deductible, and lower risk than residual equity claims), every time a firm substitutes debt for equity:

- The **weight on the cheaper component (D/V)** rises.
- The **weight on the costlier component (S/V)** falls.
- **Ko decreases** mathematically.
- **V = EBIT / Ko increases** because EBIT is constant but denominator is smaller.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **NI Approach implies 100% debt is optimal** (which is unrealistic in practice). This is the **biggest criticism** of the theory. Examiners love testing this!

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU Numerical is built from these equations.**

| S.No | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Notes |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Total Market Value | V | $V = S + D$ | S = equity, D = debt |
| 2 | Net Operating Income | EBIT | Given/Constant | Independent of financing |
| 3 | Interest | I | $I = D \times K_d$ | Constant Kd assumed |
| 4 | Earnings to Equity | NI | $NI = (EBIT - I)(1 - t)$ | t = tax rate |
| 5 | Market Value of Equity | S | $S = \dfrac{NI}{K_e}$ | Constant Ke |
| 6 | Market Value of Debt | D | $D = \dfrac{I}{K_d}$ | Constant Kd |
| 7 | Overall Cost of Capital | Ko | $K_o = \dfrac{S}{V} K_e + \dfrac{D}{V} K_d$ | Weighted average |
| 8 | Value under NI | V_NI | $V_{NI} = \dfrac{EBIT}{K_o}$ | Same as EBIT/Ko |
| 9 | Debt-Equity Ratio | D/E | $\dfrac{D}{E}$ (book or market) | Use **market value** in NI |
| 10 | EPS | EPS | $EPS = \dfrac{NI}{N}$ | N = number of equity shares |

### 2.6 Critical Boundary Conditions (KTU Board)

| Condition | Under NI Approach |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Behavior of Kd | **Constant** for all debt levels |
| Behavior of Ke | **Constant** for all debt levels |
| Behavior of Ko | **Decreases** as debt increases |
| Behavior of V (Firm Value) | **Increases** as debt increases |
| Optimal Capital Structure | **Maximum debt (theoretically 100%)** |
| Realism | **Low** — ignores bankruptcy risk |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

Even though the NI Approach is simplistic, its logic underpins **real capital budgeting decisions**:

- **Infrastructure Projects** (Roads, Power Plants): Heavy debt financing is used because cash flows are predictable and tax shields are valuable — exactly what NI prescribes.
- **Tech Startups**: Avoid debt because EBIT is volatile — they use equity, accepting higher Ko.
- **Acquisitions**: LBO (Leveraged Buyouts) use maximum debt — but with a clear repayment plan, mirroring NI's logic within controllable limits.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Critically evaluate NI Approach", always mention that **MM (without tax) refuted NI** by showing that Ke rises as D/V rises, exactly offsetting the benefit of cheaper debt. This is a **favourite 14-mark question**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Master Derivation — NI Approach Value Equation

We now derive the **canonical valuation formula** under the Net Income Approach. Every KTU numerical begins here.

### Given:
- EBIT (constant, independent of capital structure)
- Kd (constant cost of debt)
- Ke (constant cost of equity)
- D (market value of debt)
- t (tax rate)

### Step 1: Compute Total Interest

The interest paid to debt-holders is contractual:

$$I = D \times K_d$$

### Step 2: Compute Earnings Before Tax (EBT)

EBT is EBIT minus interest:

$$EBT = EBIT - I = EBIT - (D \times K_d)$$

### Step 3: Compute Net Income (NI) After Tax

Tax is levied on EBT:

$$NI = (EBT)(1 - t) = (EBIT - D \times K_d)(1 - t)$$

### Step 4: Compute Market Value of Equity (S)

Equity holders receive NI in perpetuity. They capitalize at Ke:

$$S = \dfrac{NI}{K_e} = \dfrac{(EBIT - D \times K_d)(1 - t)}{K_e}$$

### Step 5: Compute Market Value of Debt (D)

Debt value is the present value of perpetual interest, capitalized at Kd:

$$D = \dfrac{I}{K_d} = \dfrac{D \times K_d}{K_d} = D$$

*(This is an identity — it just confirms consistency.)*

### Step 6: Total Firm Value V

$$V = S + D = \dfrac{(EBIT - D \times K_d)(1 - t)}{K_e} + D$$

### Step 7: The Simplified V Formula (After Algebraic Manipulation)

Bringing to common denominator:

$$V = \dfrac{EBIT(1 - t)}{K_e} + D \left[1 - \dfrac{K_d(1 - t)}{K_e}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU board-favorite formula.** When $K_e > K_d(1 - t)$, the term in brackets is **positive**, so **V rises with D**. This is the heart of the NI Approach.

### Step 8: Compute Ko

$$K_o = \dfrac{S}{V} K_e + \dfrac{D}{V} K_d$$

Substituting values:

$$K_o = \dfrac{K_e \cdot S}{V} + \dfrac{K_d \cdot D}{V} = \dfrac{K_e \cdot S + K_d \cdot D}{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: $K_e \cdot S = NI$ (by definition of S). So $K_o = \dfrac{NI + K_d \cdot D}{V}$. Also $NI + K_d \cdot D = EBIT(1-t)$, which gives $K_o = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{V}$, equivalently $V = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_o}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the exact KTU 14-mark format. Memorize the steps.**

### Problem Statement
A firm has **EBIT = ₹5,00,000**. The firm wants to choose between two financing plans:

| Plan | Debt (D) | Kd | Ke | Tax Rate (t) |
|------|----------|-----|-----|--------------|
| Plan I (All Equity) | ₹0 | — | 12% | 30% |
| Plan II (Mixed) | ₹10,00,000 | 8% | 12% | 30% |

**Required:** Compute V, Ko, and EPS for both plans using the NI Approach. Comment on which is better.

### Solution

**Step 1: Plan I — All Equity (D = 0)**

$$I_1 = D \times K_d = 0$$

$$EBT_1 = EBIT - I_1 = 5,00,000 - 0 = 5,00,000$$

$$NI_1 = EBT_1 \times (1 - t) = 5,00,000 \times 0.70 = 3,50,000$$

$$S_1 = \dfrac{NI_1}{K_e} = \dfrac{3,50,000}{0.12} = 29,16,666.67$$

$$V_1 = S_1 + D_1 = 29,16,666.67 + 0 = 29,16,666.67$$

$$K_{o1} = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{V_1} = \dfrac{5,00,000 \times 0.70}{29,16,666.67} = \dfrac{3,50,000}{29,16,666.67} = 12\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> With **zero debt**, Ko equals Ke. This makes sense because there is no debt to weight.

**Step 2: Plan II — Mixed Capital Structure (D = ₹10,00,000)**

$$I_2 = D \times K_d = 10,00,000 \times 0.08 = 80,000$$

$$EBT_2 = EBIT - I_2 = 5,00,000 - 80,000 = 4,20,000$$

$$NI_2 = EBT_2 \times (1-t) = 4,20,000 \times 0.70 = 2,94,000$$

$$S_2 = \dfrac{NI_2}{K_e} = \dfrac{2,94,000}{0.12} = 24,50,000$$

$$V_2 = S_2 + D_2 = 24,50,000 + 10,00,000 = 34,50,000$$

$$K_{o2} = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{V_2} = \dfrac{3,50,000}{34,50,000} = 10.14\%$$

### Step 3: Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Plan I (No Debt) | Plan II (Mixed) | Change |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|--------|
| **V (Firm Value)** | ₹29,16,667 | ₹34,50,000 | **↑ ₹5,33,333** |
| **Ko (Cost of Capital)** | 12.00% | 10.14% | **↓ 1.86%** |
| **EPS (per ₹100 share)*** | ₹35.00 | ₹24.50 | ↓ |

*Assuming total equity = ₹10,00,000 for Plan I and ₹10,00,000 for Plan II (residual equity). EPS calculation: ₹3,50,000/10,000 shares = ₹35; ₹2,94,000/12,000 shares = ₹24.50. Actual numbers may vary based on share data.*

### Step 4: Conclusion

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Model Answer:** Under the NI Approach, **Plan II is superior** because it yields a **higher firm value (V)** and a **lower cost of capital (Ko)**. This demonstrates the theory's central claim — **substituting cheaper debt for costlier equity increases firm value**.

---

## 3.3 Algebraic Sensitivity — How Much Does V Change with D?

Starting from:

$$V = \dfrac{EBIT(1 - t)}{K_e} + D\left[1 - \dfrac{K_d(1-t)}{K_e}\right]$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to D:

$$\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial D} = 1 - \dfrac{K_d(1-t)}{K_e}$$

- If $\dfrac{K_d(1-t)}{K_e} < 1$ → $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial D} > 0$ → V rises with D.
- If $\dfrac{K_d(1-t)}{K_e} = 1$ → V is constant (NOI case).
- If $\dfrac{K_d(1-t)}{K_e} > 1$ → V falls with D (Traditional case, after optimum).

Under NI, since Kd < Ke, the partial derivative is positive — confirming V rises monotonically with D.

---

## 3.4 EPS Sensitivity Under NI

$$EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT - D \cdot K_d)(1-t)}{N}$$

where N = number of equity shares.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** NI Approach assumes **Ke is constant**. In reality, EPS often *appears* to rise with debt because share count shrinks. But the true measure is **share price (P)**, not EPS. NI blurs this distinction — a frequent **valuation trap**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flow Architecture — Capital Structure Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Firm Needs Long-Term Funds] --> nodeB[Identify Financing Sources]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Source Selection}
    nodeC -->|Debt| nodeD[Bonds, Debentures, Term Loans]
    nodeC -->|Equity| nodeE[Common Shares, Retained Earnings]
    nodeC -->|Hybrid| nodeF[Preference Shares, Convertibles]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Compute Kd]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Compute Ke]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Compute Kp]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Calculate Weighted Average Ko]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Ko minimized?}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[Optimal Capital Structure Achieved]
    nodeK -->|No| nodeM[Adjust Debt-Equity Mix]
    nodeM --> nodeC
    nodeL --> nodeN[End: Maximize Firm Value V]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Classical Theories Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Capital Structure Theories]
    nodeX --> nodeP[NI Approach]
    nodeX --> nodeQ[NOI Approach]
    nodeX --> nodeR[Traditional Approach]
    nodeX --> nodeS[MM Without Tax]
    nodeX --> nodeT[MM With Tax]
    nodeP --> nodeP1[V rises with D]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2[Ko falls with D]
    nodeQ --> nodeQ1[V constant]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2[Ko rises as Ke rises offsetting Kd fall]
    nodeR --> nodeR1[V rises then falls]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2[Optimal at intermediate D/E]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[V constant same as NOI]
    nodeT --> nodeT1[V rises linearly with D]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2[Tax shield benefit]
```

## 4.3 NI Approach — Behavioural Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[EBIT Constant] --> nodeB[Increase D]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Interest I rises]
    nodeC --> nodeD[NI falls]
    nodeD --> nodeE[S = NI/Ke falls but proportionally less]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Net Effect V rises]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Ko falls]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Recommendation: Maximum Debt]
```

## 4.4 Debt-Equity Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Optimal_Debt_Range
    nodeA1[Low Debt] --> nodeA2[Moderate Debt]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Optimal Debt]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[High Debt Risk Zone]
    end
    nodeA1 -.Low leverage.-> nodeA1
    nodeA2 -.Balanced.-> nodeA2
    nodeA3 -.Maximum V.-> nodeA3
    nodeA4 -.Bankruptcy risk.-> nodeA4
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — NI Approach Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Inputs: EBIT, Kd, Ke, t] --> nodeB[Choose D]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute I = D*Kd]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute NI = EBIT-I times 1-t]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute S = NI/Ke]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute V = S+D]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Ko]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compare V across plans]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Select max V as optimal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Capital Structure. Differentiate it from Financial Structure. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Capital Structure refers to the mix of long-term sources of funds — equity share capital, preference share capital, and long-term debt — used by a firm to finance its assets.

**Difference (1.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Scope | Long-term funds only | Entire liabilities side (LT + ST) |
| Components | Equity, Preference, LTD | Capital Structure + Current Liabilities |

---

### Question 2: State any three assumptions of the Net Income Approach. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Constant Cost of Debt (Kd):** The cost of debt remains unchanged regardless of the degree of leverage.
2. **Constant Cost of Equity (Ke):** Shareholders perceive no change in business risk, so Ke is fixed.
3. **Perfect Capital Markets:** No transaction costs, taxes (or fixed tax), or floatation costs.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the meaning and scope of capital structure. Discuss any **four patterns** of capital structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm expects to earn **₹6,00,000** per annum before interest and taxes. The firm is considering two alternative financial plans:

| Plan | Debt (₹) | Kd | Ke |
|------|----------|-----|-----|
| Plan A | 0 | — | 14% |
| Plan B | 15,00,000 | 10% | 14% |

**Tax rate = 30%.** Compute the **value of the firm (V)** and the **overall cost of capital (Ko)** under both plans using the **Net Income Approach**. Which plan is preferable and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution A:**

**(a) Meaning, Scope & Patterns of Capital Structure (7 Marks)**

**Meaning (2 Marks):** Capital structure is the permanent financing arrangement of a firm, comprising long-term debt, preference capital, and equity capital (including retained earnings). It excludes short-term liabilities.

**Scope (2 Marks):** Capital structure decisions cover:
- Choice of security type (debt vs equity)
- Determination of debt-equity proportion
- Minimization of WACC
- Maximization of firm value
- Consideration of external market conditions

**Patterns (3 Marks — list and explain four):**

1. **Equity Pattern (Conservative):** Heavy equity, minimal debt. Lower risk, lower return.
2. **Debt Pattern (Aggressive):** Heavy debt, minimal equity. Higher financial risk, higher leverage.
3. **Balanced Pattern (Moderate):** Optimal mix of debt and equity. Most firms aim for this.
4. **Pyramid Pattern:** Hierarchical debt usage across parent and subsidiaries.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Plan A — All Equity:**

$$I_A = 0$$

$$EBT_A = 6,00,000 - 0 = 6,00,000$$

$$NI_A = 6,00,000 \times 0.70 = 4,20,000$$

$$S_A = \dfrac{4,20,000}{0.14} = 30,00,000$$

$$V_A = S_A + D_A = 30,00,000 + 0 = \mathbf{₹30,00,000}$$

$$K_{oA} = \dfrac{6,00,000 \times 0.70}{30,00,000} = \dfrac{4,20,000}{30,00,000} = \mathbf{14\%}$$

**Plan B — Mixed Capital Structure:**

$$I_B = 15,00,000 \times 0.10 = 1,50,000$$

$$EBT_B = 6,00,000 - 1,50,000 = 4,50,000$$

$$NI_B = 4,50,000 \times 0.70 = 3,15,000$$

$$S_B = \dfrac{3,15,000}{0.14} = 22,50,000$$

$$V_B = S_B + D_B = 22,50,000 + 15,00,000 = \mathbf{₹37,50,000}$$

$$K_{oB} = \dfrac{4,20,000}{37,50,000} = \mathbf{11.2\%}$$

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Plan A | Plan B | Decision |
|-----------|--------|--------|----------|
| V (₹) | 30,00,000 | 37,50,000 | **Plan B Higher** |
| Ko | 14% | 11.2% | **Plan B Lower** |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Plan B is preferable because it yields a **higher firm value** and a **lower cost of capital**, consistent with the NI Approach's claim that increasing debt enhances firm value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is meant by Capital Structure Decisions? Discuss any **six factors** that determine capital structure choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** XYZ Ltd. has an EBIT of **₹4,00,000**. It is evaluating three financing alternatives:

| Alternative | Debt (₹) | Kd | Ke |
|-------------|----------|-----|-----|
| I | 0 | — | 15% |
| II | 5,00,000 | 9% | 15% |
| III | 10,00,000 | 9% | 15% |

**Tax rate = 25%.** Ascertain the **best capital structure** using the Net Income Approach. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution B:**

**(a) Capital Structure Decisions & Determining Factors (7 Marks)**

**Meaning (2 Marks):** Capital structure decisions involve selecting the optimal mix of long-term debt and equity to **maximize shareholder wealth** while **minimizing the weighted average cost of capital (WACC)**.

**Six Determining Factors (5 Marks — explain any 6):**

1. **Leverage/Debt-Equity Ratio:** Higher leverage → higher financial risk.
2. **Cost of Capital:** Optimal structure minimizes WACC.
3. **Earnings Stability:** Stable earnings support higher debt.
4. **Cash Flow Position:** Predictable cash flows ease debt servicing.
5. **Tax Considerations:** Interest is tax-deductible, favoring debt.
6. **Market Conditions:** Investor appetite for debt vs equity varies.
7. **Control:** Equity issuance dilutes ownership.
8. **Floatation Costs:** Affect cost of raising fresh capital.
9. **Firm's Risk Profile:** Business risk constrains financial risk.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Three Plans (7 Marks)**

**Alternative I: All Equity (D = ₹0)**

$$I_1 = 0, \quad NI_1 = 4,00,000 \times 0.75 = 3,00,000$$

$$S_1 = \dfrac{3,00,000}{0.15} = 20,00,000$$

$$V_1 = 20,00,000 + 0 = \mathbf{₹20,00,000}$$

$$K_{o1} = \dfrac{3,00,000}{20,00,000} = \mathbf{15.00\%}$$

**Alternative II: D = ₹5,00,000**

$$I_2 = 5,00,000 \times 0.09 = 45,000$$

$$EBT_2 = 4,00,000 - 45,000 = 3,55,000$$

$$NI_2 = 3,55,000 \times 0.75 = 2,66,250$$

$$S_2 = \dfrac{2,66,250}{0.15} = 17,75,000$$

$$V_2 = 17,75,000 + 5,00,000 = \mathbf{₹22,75,000}$$

$$K_{o2} = \dfrac{3,00,000}{22,75,000} = \mathbf{13.19\%}$$

**Alternative III: D = ₹10,00,000**

$$I_3 = 10,00,000 \times 0.09 = 90,000$$

$$EBT_3 = 4,00,000 - 90,000 = 3,10,000$$

$$NI_3 = 3,10,000 \times 0.75 = 2,32,500$$

$$S_3 = \dfrac{2,32,500}{0.15} = 15,50,000$$

$$V_3 = 15,50,000 + 10,00,000 = \mathbf{₹25,50,000}$$

$$K_{o3} = \dfrac{3,00,000}{25,50,000} = \mathbf{11.76\%}$$

**Summary Table (1 Mark):**

| Alt | V (₹) | Ko | Rank |
|-----|--------|-----|------|
| I | 20,00,000 | 15.00% | 3rd |
| II | 22,75,000 | 13.19% | 2nd |
| **III** | **25,50,000** | **11.76%** | **Best** |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Under the NI Approach, **Alternative III** (highest debt) is optimal because it yields the **maximum firm value (₹25,50,000)** and **minimum WACC (11.76%)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting tax (1-t) factor** in NI computation — costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 2. **Using book value of debt instead of market value** — KTU strictly expects market value weights.
> 3. **Mixing up V = EBIT/Ko vs V = EBIT(1-t)/Ko** — the (1-t) term appears because NI is after-tax.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** before solving — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Comparing EPS instead of V/Ko** — under NI, the right metric is **firm value (V)** and **WACC (Ko)**, not EPS.
> 6. **Skipping units (₹)** in the answer — small penalty but adds up.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Final day revision — read this section 3 times before the exam.**

- ✅ **Capital Structure** = permanent long-term financing (Debt + Preference + Equity).
- ✅ **Financial Structure** = Capital Structure + Current Liabilities.
- ✅ **Scope** covers security choice, debt-equity mix, WACC minimization, and value maximization.
- ✅ **Four Patterns:** Equity-dominant, Debt-dominant, Balanced, Pyramid.
- ✅ **NI Approach** (Durand 1952) — **V rises and Ko falls** as debt rises.
- ✅ **Core Assumptions:** Constant Kd, Constant Ke, EBIT independent of financing.
- ✅ **Core Formulas:**
  - $V = S + D$
  - $S = \dfrac{(EBIT - D \cdot K_d)(1-t)}{K_e}$
  - $K_o = \dfrac{S}{V} K_e + \dfrac{D}{V} K_d$
  - $V = \dfrac{EBIT(1-t)}{K_o}$
- ✅ **Optimal under NI** = **Maximum debt** (theoretical, often criticized).
- ✅ **Criticism:** Ignores bankruptcy risk, MM's offsetting rise in Ke, and financial distress costs.
- ✅ **Comparison:** NI ↔ NOI (V constant) ↔ Traditional (optimal at intermediate D) ↔ MM with tax (linear rise in V).
- ✅ **KTU Board 14-mark pattern:** Numerical problem with 2–3 financing alternatives, requiring V and Ko computation and recommendation.
- ✅ **Always state** that the plan with **highest V** and **lowest Ko** is best under NI.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Infrastructure firms use NI logic; tech startups reject it due to volatility.
- ✅ **Key takeaway:** NI provides the *entry-point framework* — but reality demands MM and Traditional refinements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Operating Income Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Net Operating Income Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach** is a capital structure theory propounded by **David Durand** in **1952**. It asserts that the **market value of the firm ($V$)** and the **overall cost of capital ($K_o$)** are **completely independent of the firm's capital structure** (i.e., the debt-equity mix). Under this approach, the equilibrium market value of the firm is determined by capitalizing the expected net operating income at the overall cost of capital, irrespective of how the firm finances its assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The NOI Approach is one of the three foundational theories of capital structure, alongside the **Net Income (NI) Approach** and the **Traditional Approach**. It is frequently tested for **2-mark, 3-mark, and 14-mark** descriptive and numerical questions in KTU ESE.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake (the firm). The total cake size (firm value) is fixed by the recipe (business risk and NOI), **not** by how much sugar vs flour (debt vs equity) you use. You can change the proportions, but the oven's heat (overall cost of capital) stays the same. If you add cheaper ingredient (debt), the taste (cost of equity) automatically becomes bitter to compensate — keeping the overall flavor identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Adding cheaper debt increases financial risk for equity holders, who demand a higher return ($K_e$ rises). This rise in $K_e$ exactly offsets the benefit of cheaper debt, leaving $K_o$ unchanged.

### Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Net Operating Income (NOI):** Earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) — measured in **currency units (₹)**.
- **Overall Cost of Capital ($K_o$):** Weighted average cost of capital, expressed in **percentage (%)**.
- **Financial Leverage:** Measured as the **Debt-to-Equity ratio (D/E)**, a **dimensionless ratio**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Pillars of the NOI Approach

- **Pillar 1 — Capital Structure Irrelevance:**
  The market value of the firm ($V$) does **not** change with the degree of financial leverage. Whether a firm uses 0% debt or 90% debt, its total value stays the same.

- **Pillar 2 — Constant Overall Cost of Capital ($K_o$):**
  Since $V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$ and $V$ is fixed, $K_o$ must also be fixed. The firm cannot reduce its weighted average cost by substituting debt for equity.

- **Pillar 3 — Variable Cost of Equity ($K_e$):**
  As the proportion of debt increases, the financial risk borne by equity shareholders increases. To compensate, $K_e$ rises automatically to **perfectly offset** any reduction in average cost from cheaper debt.

- **Pillar 4 — Pie Model Logic:**
  Durand illustrated this using the "pie model" — the size of the pie (total firm value) is fixed. Cutting a larger slice for debt leaves a smaller slice for equity, but the **total pie size never changes**.

- **Pillar 5 — Constant Cost of Debt ($K_d$):**
  In its purest form, NOI Approach assumes $K_d$ is independent of leverage (some extended versions allow $K_d$ to vary, but the change is fully neutralized by $K_e$).

### Core Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underlying Assumptions of NOI Approach:**
> 1. The firm's **business risk** remains constant.
> 2. There are **no corporate taxes** (MM-no-tax version influence).
> 3. The cost of debt ($K_d$) is **less than** the cost of equity ($K_e$).
> 4. Investors and firms can borrow at the **same rate** (homogeneous expectations).
> 5. The **overall capitalization rate ($K_o$) is independent** of capital structure.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$ | Total market value of firm | $V$ in ₹, $NOI$ in ₹, $K_o$ in % |
| $E = V - D$ | Market value of equity | $E, V, D$ all in ₹ |
| $K_e = \dfrac{NOI - I}{E}$ | Cost of equity | $I$ = Interest in ₹, $K_e$ in % |
| $K_o = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right) K_d$ | Weighted average cost | All ratios dimensionless, rates in % |
| $I = D \times K_d$ | Total interest expense | $I, D$ in ₹, $K_d$ in % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Always calculate $K_o$ as the **weighted average** of $K_e$ and $K_d$. In NOI Approach problems, $K_o$ remains **fixed across all leverage levels** — this is the verification check students must perform.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

The NOI Approach is the intellectual **precursor to the Modigliani-Miller (MM) Proposition I (without taxes)**. It is used in:
- **Capital budgeting decisions** where firms wish to separate investment (operating) decisions from financing decisions.
- **Leverage policy formulation** by financial managers to evaluate the true impact of debt.
- **Valuation of engineering firms** in M&A (Mergers & Acquisitions) when assessing the irrelevance of capital structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Total Value of the Firm ($V$)

Starting from the fundamental valuation principle that the value of any income-generating asset equals its expected earnings divided by the capitalization rate:

$$
V = \frac{NOI}{K_o}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Define NOI} \rightarrow \text{NOI} = \text{EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Define } K_o \rightarrow K_o = \text{Overall (weighted average) cost of capital of the firm}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Apply perpetuity-style valuation} \rightarrow V = \frac{NOI}{K_o}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: State NOI Assumption} \rightarrow V \text{ is independent of debt-equity mix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the NOI Approach, since $NOI$ and $K_o$ are both assumed constant, **$V$ is constant** regardless of leverage. This is the foundation of the entire theory.

### Derivation 2 — Cost of Equity ($K_e$)

$$
K_e = \frac{NOI - I}{E}
$$

**Exhaustive algebraic expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Net income to equity holders} &= NOI - \text{Interest} \\
&= NOI - (D \times K_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Equity capital base} &= E = V - D
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Required return for equity} &= K_e = \frac{NOI - (D \times K_d)}{V - D}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation 3 — Verification of Constant $K_o$ (The Offsetting Mechanism)

To prove $K_o$ remains unchanged as leverage rises:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_o &= \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d \\
&= \frac{E}{V} \cdot \left(\frac{NOI - D \cdot K_d}{E}\right) + \frac{D}{V} \cdot K_d \\
&= \frac{NOI - D \cdot K_d}{V} + \frac{D \cdot K_d}{V} \\
&= \frac{NOI}{V} \\
&= K_o \;\;\text{(constant)} \;\;\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Behavior Across Capital Structures

| **Leverage Level** | **Debt ($D$)** | **Interest ($I$)** | **Equity ($E$)** | **$K_d$** | **$K_e$** | **$V$** | **$K_o$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero Leverage | 0 | 0 | High | $\vert$ | $K_o$ | $V$ | $K_o$ |
| Low Leverage | Low | Low | High | $K_d$ | $K_e \uparrow$ | $V$ | $K_o$ |
| Moderate Leverage | Medium | Medium | Medium | $K_d$ | $K_e \uparrow\uparrow$ | $V$ | $K_o$ |
| High Leverage | High | High | Low | $K_d$ | $K_e \uparrow\uparrow\uparrow$ | $V$ | $K_o$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition:** As $D \uparrow$, $E \downarrow$, and $K_e \uparrow$ such that the weighted average $K_o$ **always remains at the same constant value**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Conceptual Flow of NOI Approach Valuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Net Operating Income NOI"] --> B["Capitalize at Overall Cost of Capital Ko"]
    B --> C["Total Market Value of Firm V"]
    C --> D["Debt Component D"]
    C --> E["Equity Component E"]
    D --> F["Cost of Debt Kd"]
    E --> G["Cost of Equity Ke"]
    F --> H["Weighted Average Ko Constant"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Verification of NOI Theory"]
```

### Diagram 2: Durand's Pie Model Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pieA["Total Pie Size V Fixed"] --> slice1["Debt Slice D"]
    pieA --> slice2["Equity Slice E"]
    slice1 --> note1["Larger Slice means Higher Kd Benefit"]
    slice2 --> note2["Smaller Slice means Higher Ke Risk Premium"]
    note1 --> outcome["Net Effect Ko Unchanged"]
    note2 --> outcome
```

### Diagram 3: Offsetting Mechanism Between $K_d$ and $K_e$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Leverage Increases D Rises"] --> effect1["Interest I Increases"]
    effect1 --> effect2["Financial Risk Rises for Equity"]
    effect2 --> effect3["Required Ke Increases"]
    effect3 --> effect4["Lower Weighted Equity Component"]
    effect4 --> effect5["Ko Remains Constant"]
    start -.-> parallel["Parallel Effect: Cheaper Kd"]
    parallel --> effect4
```

### Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inputs → Process → Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Input: Expected NOI"] --> proc1
    inputB["Input: Overall Ko"] --> proc1
    inputC["Input: Debt Level D"] --> proc1
    inputD["Input: Cost of Debt Kd"] --> proc1
    proc1["Process: Capital Structure Analysis"] --> outA["Output: Total Firm Value V"]
    proc1 --> outB["Output: Equity Value E"]
    proc1 --> outC["Output: Equity Cost Ke"]
    proc1 --> outD["Output: Weighted Ko Verification"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define the Net Operating Income Approach. Who propounded it?

**Model Answer:**

The **Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach** is a theory of capital structure proposed by **David Durand in 1952**. It states that the market value of a firm and its overall cost of capital are **independent of its debt-equity mix**. The firm's value is determined by capitalizing its net operating income at the overall cost of capital, and any increase in leverage raises the cost of equity to perfectly offset the benefit of cheaper debt.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State any **three** core assumptions of the NOI Approach.

**Model Answer:**

1. The **business risk** of the firm remains constant and is unaffected by capital structure changes.
2. The **cost of debt ($K_d$)** is lower than the cost of equity ($K_e$).
3. The **overall cost of capital ($K_o$)** is independent of the degree of financial leverage.
4. (Bonus) There are no corporate taxes, and firms and investors can borrow at the same rate.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

### (a) Explain the Net Operating Income Approach in detail. Discuss its assumptions, propositions, and implications for financial decision-making. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Net Operating Income Approach was formulated by **David Durand (1952)** as one of the pioneering theories of capital structure. The central proposition is that the **market value of the firm ($V$)** and the **overall cost of capital ($K_o$)** are **completely unaffected** by the financing mix between debt and equity.

**Core Propositions:**

$$
V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \text{constant}
$$

$$
K_o = \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d = \text{constant}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Business risk is constant.
2. No corporate taxes exist.
3. Investors and firms have homogeneous expectations and access to the same borrowing rates.
4. $K_d$ is less than $K_e$.

**Implications:**
- Capital structure decisions are **irrelevant** for value maximization.
- Leverage decisions cannot reduce the firm's weighted average cost of capital.
- The increase in $K_e$ exactly neutralizes the cheaper cost of debt.

**Conclusion:** Managers should focus on **operational efficiency (NOI)** rather than tinkering with capital structure to create value.

---

### (b) A firm has a Net Operating Income of **₹5,00,000** per annum. It currently employs debt of **₹10,00,000** at a cost of **8%**. The overall cost of capital is **10%**. Calculate the total value of the firm and the cost of equity using the NOI Approach. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $NOI = ₹5,00,000$
- $D = ₹10,00,000$
- $K_d = 8\% = 0.08$
- $K_o = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 1: Total Value of the Firm**

$$
V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \frac{5,00,000}{0.10} = ₹50,00,000
$$

**Step 2: Market Value of Equity**

$$
E = V - D = 50,00,000 - 10,00,000 = ₹40,00,000
$$

**Step 3: Interest Expense**

$$
I = D \times K_d = 10,00,000 \times 0.08 = ₹80,000
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Equity**

$$
K_e = \frac{NOI - I}{E} = \frac{5,00,000 - 80,000}{40,00,000} = \frac{4,20,000}{40,00,000} = 0.105 = 10.5\%
$$

**Step 5: Verification of $K_o$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_o &= \left(\frac{E}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d \\
&= \left(\frac{40,00,000}{50,00,000}\right)(0.105) + \left(\frac{10,00,000}{50,00,000}\right)(0.08) \\
&= (0.80)(0.105) + (0.20)(0.08) \\
&= 0.084 + 0.016 \\
&= 0.10 = 10\% \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- Total firm value $V = ₹50,00,000$
- Cost of equity $K_e = 10.5\%$
- $K_o$ verified as $10\%$ — **constant**, confirming NOI Approach.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)**

### (a) Compare and contrast the NOI Approach with the **Net Income (NI) Approach** and the **Traditional Approach**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **NOI Approach** | **NI Approach** | **Traditional Approach** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proponent** | David Durand (1952) | David Durand (1952) | Ezra Solomon / Traditional view |
| **Capital Structure** | Irrelevant to value | Highly relevant — increases $V$ | Optimal structure exists |
| **$V$ Behavior** | Constant at all leverage levels | Rises continuously with leverage | First rises, then falls |
| **$K_o$ Behavior** | Constant | Decreases continuously | First falls, then rises |
| **$K_e$ Behavior** | Rises to offset cheaper $K_d$ | Constant | Rises after optimal point |
| **Optimal Capital Structure** | Does not exist | Infinite leverage is optimal | Exists at a specific D/E ratio |
| **Realism** | Less realistic (extremist) | Less realistic (opposite extreme) | Most realistic — supports practical finance |

**Conclusion:** The NOI Approach and NI Approach represent the **two extreme ends** of capital structure theory. The Traditional Approach occupies the **middle ground**, acknowledging that an optimal capital structure exists where $K_o$ is minimized and $V$ is maximized.

---

### (b) XYZ Ltd. expects a constant NOI of **₹6,00,000** per year. The overall cost of capital is **12%**. Compute the firm's value and cost of equity under the following three capital structures. Comment on the results. **[7 Marks]**

**Capital Structure Options:**

| **Plan** | **Debt ($D$)** | **$K_d$** |
|---|---|---|
| Plan I | ₹0 | $\vert$ |
| Plan II | ₹5,00,000 | 9% |
| Plan III | ₹10,00,000 | 10% |

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $NOI = ₹6,00,000$, $K_o = 12\%$

**Step 1: Total Value of the Firm (Same under all plans — NOI Assumption)**

$$
V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \frac{6,00,000}{0.12} = ₹50,00,000
$$

**Step 2: Plan-wise Calculations**

**Plan I (Zero Leverage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0, \quad I = 0 \\
E &= V - D = 50,00,000 - 0 = ₹50,00,000 \\
K_e &= \frac{NOI - I}{E} = \frac{6,00,000 - 0}{50,00,000} = 12.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plan II (Moderate Leverage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 5,00,000 \times 0.09 = ₹45,000 \\
E &= 50,00,000 - 5,00,000 = ₹45,00,000 \\
K_e &= \frac{6,00,000 - 45,000}{45,00,000} = \frac{5,55,000}{45,00,000} = 12.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plan III (High Leverage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 10,00,000 \times 0.10 = ₹1,00,000 \\
E &= 50,00,000 - 10,00,000 = ₹40,00,000 \\
K_e &= \frac{6,00,000 - 1,00,000}{40,00,000} = \frac{5,00,000}{40,00,000} = 12.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| **Plan** | **$D$ (₹)** | **$I$ (₹)** | **$E$ (₹)** | **$K_d$ (%)** | **$K_e$ (%)** | **$V$ (₹)** | **$K_o$ (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | 0 | 0 | 50,00,000 | $\vert$ | 12.00 | 50,00,000 | 12.00 |
| II | 5,00,000 | 45,000 | 45,00,000 | 9.00 | 12.33 | 50,00,000 | 12.00 |
| III | 10,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 40,00,000 | 10.00 | 12.50 | 50,00,000 | 12.00 |

**Comments:**

- Total firm value ($V$) remains **constant at ₹50,00,000** across all plans.
- Cost of equity ($K_e$) rises as debt increases — from **12% → 12.33% → 12.50%**.
- Overall cost of capital ($K_o$) stays at **12%** throughout, validating the NOI Approach.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the question type:** If asked for "cost of capital" give $K_o$, if asked for "cost of equity" give $K_e$. Mixing them up costs **2-3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Verification step is mandatory:** Always recompute $K_o$ using the weighted average formula at the end. KTU examiners award **1 grace mark** for this verification.
> 3. **Don't assume $K_d$ is constant across plans:** If the question provides different $K_d$ for different plans, use the **plan-specific rate** — students often mistakenly use a single rate.
> 4. **Units consistency:** Convert percentages to decimals (e.g., $10\% = 0.10$) before substitution in formulas.
> 5. **Naming convention:** Remember the theory is called **"Net Operating Income Approach"**, not **"Net Income Approach"** — they are two distinct theories (NOI vs NI).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Proponent:** **David Durand (1952)** — must be remembered for 2-mark questions.
- **Core Formula:** $V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$ — the entire theory hinges on this equation.
- **Three Constants under NOI:** $V$ (firm value), $K_o$ (overall cost), and $NOI$ (operating income) — **all remain unchanged** as leverage varies.
- **One Variable:** $K_e$ (cost of equity) — **increases monotonically** with leverage to offset cheaper debt.
- **Key Terminology:** "Pie Model" — visualize total firm value as a fixed pie being re-sliced between debt and equity claimants.
- **Relationship with MM:** NOI Approach is the **conceptual precursor** to **Modigliani-Miller Proposition I (no-tax case)**.
- **Contrast Traps:** KTU loves to ask "Compare NOI vs NI" or "NOI vs Traditional" — keep the comparison table memorized.
- **Numerical Strategy:** Always solve for $V$ first, then $E$, then $K_e$, then **verify $K_o$**.
- **Default Assumption:** If no taxes are mentioned, NOI Approach applies as originally formulated (without tax shield).
- **Practical Relevance:** In real corporate finance, the **Traditional Approach** is closer to truth — but NOI is still essential for theoretical foundations and academic examinations.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** After computing $K_e$, it must always be **greater than** $K_d$ under NOI Approach — otherwise the offsetting logic is broken.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional Approach and Modigliani-Miller (MM) Approach; Cost of Capital; Planning for Sources of Finance (Domestic and International)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Traditional Approach and Modigliani-Miller (MM) Approach; Cost of Capital; Planning for Sources of Finance (Domestic and International)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capital Structure — The Foundation

**Capital Structure** is the specific mix of long-term debt and equity financing that a firm uses to fund its operations, expansions, and assets. It is the permanent financing of the company, balancing debt and equity to maximize firm value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *Capital structure refers to the proportion of long-term sources of funds (debt, preference capital, equity share capital, and retained earnings) used by a firm to finance its long-term investments.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The See-Saw of Finance"

Imagine a **see-saw** in a playground:
- One end = **Debt** (cheaper but riskier)
- Other end = **Equity** (safer but costlier)

The finance manager's job is to find the *perfect balance point* so the firm neither falls into *debt-trap* nor *dilutes ownership excessively*. The theories we study are different "balancing recipes" proposed by great economists.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Module 3

### Pillar 1: Capital Structure Theories

> [!NOTE]
> **Traditional Approach (Intermediate View):**
