## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Inventory" in the context of financial management. List the four main types of inventories held by a manufacturing firm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Inventory refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization at a given point in time. It is treated as a current asset on the balance sheet and represents idle resources that have economic value but are awaiting sale, processing, or use in production.

**Four Types (1.5 marks):**
1. **Raw Materials** — Basic inputs purchased for production (e.g., steel, electronic components).
2. **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** — Semi-finished goods mid-production.
3. **Finished Goods** — Completed products ready for sale.
4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating Supplies)** — Consumables supporting production.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is EOQ? State the formula for determining Economic Order Quantity with its underlying assumptions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is the optimal order quantity that minimizes the total inventory cost (sum of ordering and holding costs) for a given annual demand.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2AO}{C}}$$
where $A$ = annual demand, $O$ = ordering cost per order, $C$ = holding cost per unit per year.

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Constant and known annual demand.
2. Constant and known lead time.
3. Instantaneous replenishment (no in-transit inventory).
4. No stock-outs permitted.
5. No quantity discounts available.
6. Ordering and holding costs are the only relevant costs.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
*An engineering components manufacturing firm uses 24,000 units of a particular bolt annually. The cost of placing one order is ₹150. The carrying cost per unit per year is ₹6. The firm operates 300 days a year, and the lead time for procurement is 10 days.*

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), the number of orders per year, the optimal time between orders, and the total annual inventory cost at EOQ.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Determine the Reorder Point. If the firm wants to maintain a 95% service level and the standard deviation of daily demand is 4 units, calculate the safety stock and revised ROP. Also compute the total cost if safety stock is maintained (assuming carrying cost applies to safety stock too).

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Given Data:**
- Annual demand $A = 24{,}000$ units
- Ordering cost $O = ₹150$/order
- Holding cost $C = ₹6$/unit/year
- Working days $= 300$
- Lead time $L = 10$ days
- Standard deviation of daily demand $\sigma_d = 4$ units
- Service level $= 95\%$, so $Z = 1.65$

---

**Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Calculate EOQ (2 marks)**

$$Q^* = EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot A \cdot O}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{6}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000}$$

$$Q^* \approx 1095.45 \approx 1095 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Number of orders per year (2 marks)**

$$N = \frac{A}{Q^*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1095.45} \approx 21.91 \approx 22 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 3: Optimal time between orders (1 mark)**

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{A} = \frac{1095.45}{24{,}000} \approx 0.0456 \text{ years}$$

$$T^* = 0.0456 \times 12 \approx 0.548 \text{ months} \approx 16.66 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Total annual inventory cost at EOQ (2 marks)**

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot A \cdot O \cdot C} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 6}$$

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} = ₹6{,}571.03$$

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Daily demand (1 mark)**

$$d = \frac{A}{\text{working days}} = \frac{24{,}000}{300} = 80 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 2: Reorder Point without safety stock (1 mark)**

$$ROP = d \times L = 80 \times 10 = 800 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Safety Stock calculation (2 marks)**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 4 \times \sqrt{10}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 4 \times 3.162 = 20.87 \approx 21 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Revised ROP (1 mark)**

$$ROP_{revised} = d \cdot L + SS = 800 + 21 = 821 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5: Additional holding cost due to safety stock (1 mark)**

$$TC_{SS} = SS \cdot C = 21 \times 6 = ₹126 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: New total annual cost (1 mark)**

$$TC_{new} = TC_{min} + TC_{SS} = 6{,}571.03 + 126 = ₹6{,}697.03$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**
*XYZ Engineering Pvt. Ltd. manufactures industrial pumps. The firm classifies its inventory using ABC analysis. The following data pertains to 5 items in the inventory:*

| **Item** | **Annual Usage (units)** | **Unit Cost (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| P-101 | 1,000 | 500 |
| P-102 | 5,000 | 100 |
| P-103 | 200 | 1,000 |
| P-104 | 8,000 | 50 |
| P-105 | 500 | 800 |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Perform ABC analysis by computing the annual consumption value for each item, ranking them, and classifying them into A, B, and C categories. State the percentage of items and value in each category.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the characteristics and control implications of each category. Also, discuss any TWO other inventory classification techniques (VED and FSN) and their practical utility in an engineering firm.

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Consumption Value (ACV) for each item (2 marks)**

$$ACV = \text{Annual Usage} \times \text{Unit Cost}$$

| **Item** | **Usage (units)** | **Unit Cost (₹)** | **ACV (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-101 | 1,000 | 500 | 5,00,000 |
| P-102 | 5,000 | 100 | 5,00,000 |
| P-103 | 200 | 1,000 | 2,00,000 |
| P-104 | 8,000 | 50 | 4,00,000 |
| P-105 | 500 | 800 | 4,00,000 |
| **Total** | — | — | **20,00,000** |

**Step 2: Rank items in descending order of ACV (1 mark)**

| **Rank** | **Item** | **ACV (₹)** | **% of Total Value** | **Cumulative %** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P-101 | 5,00,000 | 25.0% | 25.0% |
| 2 | P-102 | 5,00,000 | 25.0% | 50.0% |
| 3 | P-104 | 4,00,000 | 20.0% | 70.0% |
| 4 | P-105 | 4,00,000 | 20.0% | 90.0% |
| 5 | P-103 | 2,00,000 | 10.0% | 100.0% |

**Step 3: Classify into A, B, C categories (3 marks)**

- **Category A (Top 70% value, ~40% items):** Items P-101, P-102, P-104 — 3 items (60% of items) covering 70% of value.
- **Category B (Next 20% value, ~20% items):** Item P-105 — 1 item (20% of items) covering 20% of value.
- **Category C (Remaining 10% value, ~20% items):** Item P-103 — 1 item (20% of items) covering 10% of value.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| **Category** | **Items** | **% of Items** | **% of Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | P-101, P-102, P-104 | 60% | 70% |
| B | P-105 | 20% | 20% |
| C | P-103 | 20% | 10% |

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Characteristics and Control Implications (4 marks):**

**Category A — Tight Control:**
- **Characteristics:** High unit value, low quantity, critical to operations.
- **Control:** Rigorous record-keeping, perpetual inventory system, frequent physical counts (weekly/monthly), close supplier relationships, accurate forecasts, low safety stock tolerance, frequent review.

**Category B — Moderate Control:**
- **Characteristics:** Moderate value and quantity, somewhat important.
- **Control:** Periodic review (monthly/quarterly), standard EOQ ordering, moderate safety stock, simple records.

**Category C — Loose Control:**
- **Characteristics:** Low unit value, high quantity, less critical.
- **Control:** Simple controls like two-bin system, bulk ordering annually, large safety stock acceptable, minimal records, no frequent review needed.

**Other Classification Techniques (3 marks):**

**1. VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable):**
- Classifies items based on **criticality of shortage** rather than value.
- **Vital (V):** Production stops if unavailable (e.g., a unique circuit board in a medical device).
- **Essential (E):** Production is impaired, but doesn't halt completely.
- **Desirable (D):** Shortage causes minor inconvenience.
- **Utility:** Used heavily in spare parts management for critical engineering equipment.

**2. FSN Analysis (Fast, Slow, Non-moving):**
- Classifies items based on **rate of consumption or issue**.
- **Fast (F):** Frequently issued items — keep high availability.
- **Slow (S):** Infrequently used — review periodically.
- **Non-moving (N):** Not issued for long periods — candidates for disposal, scrapping, or write-off.
- **Utility:** Helps in identifying obsolete inventory and reducing carrying costs; critical in B.Tech project labs and industrial warehouses.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU ESE for Inventory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always mention units in final answers (units, ₹/year, days, etc.) — losing 0.5 mark per omission.
>
> 2. **EOQ Rounding Errors:** Round EOQ only at the final step; carry full precision in intermediate calculations to avoid compounding errors.
>
> 3. **Z-Value Confusion:** Service level 95% means $Z = 1.65$, NOT 0.95 or 95. Use standard normal tables or remember key values: 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Safety Stock Cost:** When safety stock is added, the **total cost increases** by $SS \times C$. Many students forget this and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **ROP Formula Variants:** Don't use $ROP = L \times \sigma_d$ — that's wrong. Correct: $ROP = d \times L + SS$.
>
> 6. **ABC Classification Mistakes:** Always compute **cumulative percentage of value** for accurate categorization, not just individual percentages.
>
> 7. **Lead Time in Days vs. Annual:** Be consistent — if lead time is in days, daily demand must be calculated using working days per year.
>
> 8. **Assumption Statement:** Always list the assumptions of EOQ model when asked — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this in Part A and Part B.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Inventories (Module 4)**

- **Inventory** = RM + WIP + FG + MRO; classified as a **current asset**.
- **Three Costs:** Ordering ($C_o$), Carrying ($C_c$), Shortage ($C_s$) — total cost is U-shaped.
- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2AO/C}$ — derived by minimizing $TC = AO/Q + QC/2$.
- **Minimum Total Cost:** $TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot A \cdot O \cdot C}$.
- **Number of Orders:** $N = A/Q^* = \sqrt{AC/(2O)}$.
- **Cycle Time:** $T^* = Q^*/A$ years.
- **Reorder Point (ROP):** $ROP = d \times L + SS$ — triggers new order.
- **Safety Stock:** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ for variable demand.
- **Z-values (must memorize):** 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33.
- **EOQ with Stockouts:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2AO/C \cdot (C + C_s)/C_s}$.
- **ABC Analysis:** A = 10-20% items, 70-80% value; B = 20-30% items, 15-25% value; C = 50-70% items, 5-10% value.
- **VED:** Criticality-based (Vital, Essential, Desirable).
- **FSN:** Movement-based (Fast, Slow, Non-moving).
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR):** $COGS / Average\ Inventory$ — higher is better.
- **Days Sales of Inventory (DSI):** $365 / ITR$ — average days to sell.
- **Risk Types:** Stock-out, Obsolescence, Price, Shrinkage, Liquidity.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Safety stock, multiple suppliers, hedging, JIT, FSN analysis.
- **Always state EOQ assumptions** in exam answers.
- **Always carry full precision** in intermediate calculations; round only the final answer.
- **Engineering Context:** Inventory management applies to raw materials in factories, MRO in labs, project materials on construction sites.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade Receivables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Trade Receivables ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Trade Receivables** (also known as **Accounts Receivable** or **Sundry Debtors**) represent the amounts owed to a business enterprise by its customers for goods sold or services rendered on credit during the ordinary course of business. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework of Financial Management for Engineers, trade receivables are classified as a **current asset** on the balance sheet and constitute a significant component of working capital. In strict accounting terminology aligned with the **Indian Accounting Standard (Ind AS) framework** and as expected in KTU university valuations: $$\text{Trade Receivables} = \text{Bills Receivable} + \text{Sundry Debtors (Open Account Receivables)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Trade Receivables are the monetary claims of a firm against its customers arising out of credit sales, which are expected to be realized within one operating cycle or one financial year, whichever is longer. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are an **engineering contractor** in Kerala who has just finished installing a solar power system for a local rubber plantation. The plantation owner cannot pay the full ₹15 lakh invoice immediately — they promise to pay in **45 days**. Until that money arrives in your bank account, you essentially have an **IOU** from the customer. This IOU is your **Trade Receivable**. During those 45 days: - You still need to pay your workers - You need to buy materials for your next project - You cannot use that ₹15 lakh for any other purpose - There is a small risk the customer may delay or default This is exactly the dilemma every business faces — **Trade Receivables are the price of credit sales**, and managing them efficiently is the art of balancing **liquidity** (having cash) with **profitability** (making sales). ### 1.3 Components of Trade Receivables A trade receivable typically consists of **two key elements**: | Component | Description | KTU Treatment | |-----------|-------------|---------------| | **Gross Receivable** | Total amount billed to customer including profit margin | Pre-allowance figure | | **Bad Debt Provision** | Estimated amount that may never be collected | Shown as contra-asset | | **Net Receivable** | Realizable value of receivables | Shown in Balance Sheet | $$\text{Net Trade Receivables} = \text{Gross Trade Receivables} - \text{Provision for Doubtful Debts}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU:** *Notes Receivable* (post-dated cheques, promissory notes) are sometimes shown separately, while *Accounts Receivable* (open credit) form the bulk of trade receivables in most engineering and manufacturing firms. ### 1.4 Why Trade Receivables Exist — The Concept of Credit Sales Credit sales are extended due to **competitive market pressure** and **customer convenience**. The volume of trade receivables is influenced by: 1. **Volume of credit sales** — higher sales → higher receivables 2. **Credit period offered** — longer period → higher receivables 3. **Credit standards** — lenient standards → higher (and riskier) receivables 4. **Collection efforts** — inefficient collection → higher overdue receivables --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Trade-Off: Costs vs. Benefits of Trade Receivables From a financial management perspective, every credit policy decision involves a **trade-off between profitability and liquidity**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to explicitly evaluate both sides. #### 2.1.1 Benefits of Extending Credit (Costs Saved) - **Increased Sales Volume** → higher revenue and contribution margin - **Market Share Expansion** → competitive advantage - **Customer Loyalty & Retention** → long-term relationship value - **Reduced Inventory Holding Costs** → faster inventory turnover - **Possible Price Premiums** → customers pay for credit convenience #### 2.1.2 Costs of Maintaining Trade Receivables The KTU module emphasizes **four explicit cost categories**: | Cost Type | Description | Formula Symbol | |-----------|-------------|----------------| | **Opportunity Cost (Capital Cost)** | Interest foregone on funds locked up in receivables | $C_{op}$ | | **Administrative Cost** | Costs of credit analysis, billing, collection, record-keeping | $C_{ad}$ | | **Bad Debt Cost (Default Cost)** | Amounts written off as uncollectible | $C_{bd}$ | | **Discount Cost** | Cash discount offered for early payment | $C_{disc}$ | Total cost of trade receivables: $$C_{TR} = C_{op} + C_{ad} + C_{bd} + C_{disc}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** In numerical problems, the *opportunity cost* is the most frequently tested cost. It equals the **investment in receivables** multiplied by the **required rate of return (opportunity cost of capital)**. ### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet Below is the consolidated formula reference for solving **any** KTU-level problem on Trade Receivables. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | 1 | Average Collection Period (ACP) | $ACP = \dfrac{Accounts\ Receivable}{Net\ Credit\ Sales} \times 365$ | Days | | 2 | Accounts Receivable Turnover Ratio | $ART = \dfrac{Net\ Credit\ Sales}{Average\ Accounts\ Receivable}$ | Times | | 3 | Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) | $DSO = \dfrac{365}{ART}$ | Days | | 4 | Investment in Receivables | $I_{AR} = \dfrac{Credit\ Sales}{365} \times ACP$ | ₹ (currency) | | 5 | Opportunity Cost of Investment | $C_{op} = I_{AR} \times \dfrac{r}{100}$ | ₹ per year | | 6 | Marginal Cost of New Credit Policy | $\Delta C = \Delta C_{op} + \Delta C_{bd} + \Delta C_{ad} - \Delta C_{disc}$ | ₹ | | 7 | Marginal Benefit | $\Delta B = \Delta Contribution\ Margin$ | ₹ | | 8 | Factoring Cost (Resource Cost) | $FC = \dfrac{Discount\ Rate}{1 - Discount\ Rate} \times \dfrac{365}{Period}$ | % effective | | 9 | Bad Debt Loss Rate | $BDLR = \dfrac{Bad\ Debts}{Credit\ Sales} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 10 | Ageing Analysis Default Probability | Empirical weighting by age bracket | % per bracket | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Pipes:** Per the KTU formatting protocol, the **absolute value bars** in formulas like $\vert 1 - r \vert$ are rendered using $\vert$ and $\mid$ instead of plain pipes to preserve markdown table integrity. ### 2.3 The Receivables Management Decision Framework Engineering managers and KTU students must follow a structured **three-step decision process**: #### Step 1 — Credit Policy Decisions - **Credit Standards**: Who qualifies for credit? (5 C's of Credit — Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions) - **Credit Period**: How long to extend credit? (30, 60, 90 days) - **Credit Terms**: Cash discounts for early payment (e.g., "2/10, net 30") - **Collection Policy**: How aggressively to pursue overdue accounts? #### Step 2 — Credit Monitoring Decisions - Compute **DSO** and compare against industry benchmarks - Prepare **Ageing Schedule** (0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ days) - Monitor **Bad Debt Loss Rate** trend #### Step 3 — Receivables Financing Decisions - **Pledging** (using receivables as collateral for a loan) - **Factoring** (outright sale of receivables to a Factor) - **Bill Discounting** (discounting trade bills with a bank) ### 2.4 Factoring — A Special Form of Receivables Management > [!IMPORTANT] > **Factoring** is the outright sale of trade receivables to a specialized financial intermediary called a **Factor** at a discount. The KTU 2024 syllabus tests this concept heavily because it is widely used by Indian MSMEs and engineering firms. **Types of Factoring:** 1. **Resource Factoring** — With recourse (seller bears default risk) 2. **Non-Resource Factoring** — Without recourse (factor bears default risk) 3. **Advance Factoring** — Factor provides advance against receivables 4. **Maturity Factoring** — Factor pays on the due date only **Effective Cost of Factoring (KTU Standard Formula):** $$C_{factor} = \dfrac{d \times 365}{1 - d} \times \dfrac{1}{CP}$$ Where: - $d$ = factoring discount/commission rate (decimal) - $CP$ = average collection period in days ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In engineering firms (especially in Kerala's MSME sector), trade receivables management is critical for: - **Construction contractors** waiting for milestone payments - **Electrical & electronics manufacturers** supplying to institutional buyers - **Solar EPC firms** receiving payment after grid synchronization - **Industrial equipment suppliers** with 60–90 day credit cycles A delay in receivables collection directly impacts a firm's ability to execute new projects, pay workers, and meet statutory dues (GST, TDS, PF). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Investment in Trade Receivables Let us derive the **Investment in Trade Receivables** formula step by step, as KTU valuation keys expect explicit algebraic transition steps. **Given:** - Annual Credit Sales = $S$ - Average Collection Period = $N$ days - Cost of Goods Sold to Sales ratio = $c$ (variable cost ratio) - Markup applied on cost = $m$ (profit margin) **Step 1 — Daily Credit Sales** $$\text{Daily Credit Sales} = \dfrac{S}{365}$$ **Step 2 — Average Receivable Balance** The average receivable balance equals the sales generated over the average collection period: $$\text{Avg. Receivables} = \dfrac{S}{365} \times N$$ **Step 3 — Investment at Cost (not at Sales Price)** Since the firm's actual capital tied up is based on the **cost** of the goods (not the selling price), we multiply by the cost ratio: $$I_{AR} = \dfrac{S \times N}{365} \times c$$ **Step 4 — Opportunity Cost (Capital Cost)** If the required rate of return on investment is $r$ (per annum), then: $$\boxed{C_{op} = \dfrac{S \times N}{365} \times c \times \dfrac{r}{100}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Annual Cost of Factoring Consider a factoring arrangement with the following terms: - Discount (commission) charged by factor = $d = 2\%$ of invoice value - Average collection period = $CP = 60$ days - Advance paid by factor = $90\%$ of receivables **Step 1 — Identify the upfront deduction** The factor deducts its commission from the advance itself (in resource factoring), so the seller effectively **forgoes $d$ of every ₹1 of receivables**. **Step 2 — Calculate the per-period cost** $$\text{Cost per period} = \dfrac{d}{1 - d}$$ **Step 3 — Annualize using the number of periods in a year** Number of collection cycles per year = $365 / 60$ **Step 4 — Combine** $$C_{factor}^{eff} = \left[\dfrac{d}{1 - d}\right] \times \dfrac{365}{CP}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation (with $d = 0.02$, $CP = 60$)** $$\begin{aligned} C_{factor}^{eff} &= \left[\dfrac{0.02}{1 - 0.02}\right] \times \dfrac{365}{60} \\ &= \left[\dfrac{0.02}{0.98}\right] \times 6.0833 \\ &= 0.020408 \times 6.0833 \\ &= 0.12413 \end{aligned}$$ Converting to percentage: $$C_{factor}^{eff} = 12.41\% \text{ per annum}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** A nominal 2% factoring charge translates to an effective **12.41% annualized cost** — this is a frequent KTU question type and tests whether students understand the time-value-of-money impact of discounting. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimal Credit Policy Using Marginal Cost–Benefit Analysis A KTU standard 14-mark question evaluates whether to **tighten or relax** credit policy. **Given Change:** - Incremental Credit Sales = $\Delta S = ₹5,00,000$ - Variable cost ratio = $c = 0.80$ - Incremental ACP change = $\Delta N = +10$ days - Required return = $r = 12\%$ - Expected bad debt on incremental sales = ₹25,000 **Step 1 — Incremental Contribution Margin** $$\Delta CM = \Delta S \times (1 - c) = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Incremental Investment in Receivables** $$\Delta I_{AR} = \dfrac{\Delta S \times c \times \Delta N}{365} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 \times 10}{365}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \Delta I_{AR} &= \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000}{365} \\ &= ₹10{,}958.90 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Incremental Opportunity Cost** $$\Delta C_{op} = \Delta I_{AR} \times \dfrac{r}{100} = 10{,}958.90 \times 0.12 = ₹1{,}315.07$$ **Step 4 — Incremental Bad Debt Cost** $$\Delta C_{bd} = ₹25{,}000$$ **Step 5 — Total Incremental Cost** $$\Delta C_{total} = \Delta C_{op} + \Delta C_{bd} = 1{,}315.07 + 25{,}000 = ₹26{,}315.07$$ **Step 6 — Net Benefit Decision** $$\Delta B - \Delta C = 1{,}00{,}000 - 26{,}315.07 = ₹73{,}684.93$$ $$\boxed{\text{Since } \Delta B > \Delta C, \text{ the new credit policy should be ADOPTED.}}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix | Scenario | Condition | KTU-Board Recommendation | |----------|-----------|---------------------------| | Relax credit policy | $\Delta B > \Delta C$ | **Adopt the policy change** | | Tighten credit policy | $\Delta C_{saved} > \Delta B_{lost}$ | **Adopt tightening** | | Status quo | $\Delta B = \Delta C$ | **Indifferent point (Optimal)** | | Factoring vs Bank Loan | Effective cost of factoring < Loan rate | **Choose factoring** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Trade Receivables Management Cycle The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the operational cycle of managing trade receivables in an engineering firm — from credit sales initiation through final realization. ```mermaid flowchart TD N1([Credit Sale Initiated]) --> N2[Generate Invoice with Credit Terms] N2 --> N3{5 Cs of Credit
Verification} N3 -- Approved --> N4[Record as Account Receivable] N3 -- Rejected --> N5[Demand Cash or Advance Payment] N4 --> N6[Monitor DSO and Aging Schedule] N6 --> N7{Due Date
Reached?} N7 -- Yes --> N8{Payment
Received?} N7 -- No --> N6 N8 -- Yes --> N9[Close Receivable Entry] N8 -- No --> N10[Trigger Collection Process] N10 --> N11{Collection
Successful?} N11 -- Yes --> N9 N11 -- No --> N12[Consider Factoring or
Bad Debt Write-Off] N12 --> N13[Periodic Credit Policy Review] N13 --> N2 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following schematic maps the **functional architecture** of trade receivables decision-making, covering credit standards, monitoring, and financing decisions. This block-level diagram is the KTU-preferred fallback when a complex physical drawing would be infeasible. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Layer] IN1[Customer Order] IN2[Market Conditions] IN3[Industry Credit Norms] end subgraph POLICY[Credit Policy Engine] P1[Credit Standards Module] P2[Credit Period Module] P3[Discount Terms Module] P4[Collection Effort Module] end subgraph MONITOR[Monitoring Engine] M1[DSO Tracker] M2[Aging Schedule] M3[Bad Debt Ratio] M4[ART Ratio] end subgraph FINANCE[Financing Engine] F1[Self-Financing
via Working Capital] F2[Pledging
Receivables as Collateral] F3[Factoring
Outright Sale to Factor] F4[Bill Discounting
with Commercial Bank] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer] O1[Cash Inflow Realized] O2[Optimal Receivables Level] O3[Revised Credit Policy] end IN1 --> P1 IN2 --> P1 IN3 --> P2 P1 --> M1 P2 --> M2 P3 --> M3 P4 --> M4 M1 --> O2 M2 --> O2 M3 --> O3 M4 --> O3 O2 --> F1 O2 --> F2 O2 --> F3 O2 --> F4 F1 --> O1 F2 --> O1 F3 --> O1 F4 --> O1 ``` ### 4.3 Interpretation Notes for Diagrams > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the first flowchart, the left-side decision diamonds (`{}`) represent go/no-go control points typical in receivables workflow. In the second diagram, the **subgraph blocks** represent distinct functional layers within the firm's receivables management system — a structure commonly tested in KTU case-study questions. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Trade Receivables. Distinguish between Trade Receivables and Trade Payables.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Trade Receivables** are the amounts due to a business from its customers for goods sold or services rendered on credit in the ordinary course of business. They represent a **current asset** and are expected to be realized within one operating cycle. **Distinction Table:** | Basis | Trade Receivables | Trade Payables | |-------|-------------------|----------------| | Nature | **Asset** (money owed TO us) | **Liability** (money owed BY us) | | Position in Balance Sheet | Current Asset side | Current Liability side | | Cash Flow Impact | Conversion to **inflow** | Conversion to **outflow** | | Party | Customers / Debtors | Suppliers / Creditors | *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for distinction)* --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any three costs associated with the maintenance of trade receivables.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three main costs of maintaining trade receivables are: 1. **Opportunity Cost of Capital** — The interest income foregone on the funds locked up in receivables. *(1 mark)* 2. **Administrative Cost** — Expenses incurred for credit appraisal, billing, record-keeping, and collection efforts such as salaries of credit department staff. *(1 mark)* 3. **Bad Debt Cost** — The loss arising from customers who default on payment; these must be written off as uncollectible. *(1 mark)* *(Alternative accepted: Delinquency Cost, Discount Cost)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided within the **same module**. --- #### **Question A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain the **factors determining the size of trade receivables** in an engineering firm. Briefly describe the role of **Ageing Schedule** in receivables monitoring. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** ABC Engineering Pvt. Ltd. has annual credit sales of **₹1,80,00,000** and an **Average Collection Period (ACP) of 73 days**. The variable cost ratio is **0.75** and the required rate of return is **15% p.a.** Calculate: 1. Investment in trade receivables 2. Opportunity cost of capital locked up in receivables 3. If the firm reduces ACP to **45 days**, compute the savings in opportunity cost. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A** **Part (a) — Factors Determining the Size of Trade Receivables (7 Marks):** The size of trade receivables is influenced by the following factors: 1. **Volume of Credit Sales** — Higher credit sales automatically generate larger receivables balances. Engineering firms supplying to large institutional buyers (PSUs, government departments) typically carry high receivables. 2. **Credit Period / Terms** — Longer credit periods (e.g., 90 days vs 30 days) directly increase the average receivables balance. 3. **Credit Standards** — Lenient credit standards allow more customers (including risky ones), increasing receivables volume and bad debt risk. 4. **Collection Policy** — Weak collection efforts delay realizations, inflating the receivables balance. 5. **Customer Profile** — Sales to government / institutional clients involve procedural delays (audit, sanction), increasing receivable duration. 6. **Industry Norms** — Some industries (heavy machinery, infrastructure) inherently have longer credit cycles. 7. **Seasonal Factors** — Peak-season sales generate temporary receivable spikes. **Ageing Schedule** (1 mark for this part): An **Ageing Schedule** classifies outstanding receivables by age brackets (e.g., 0–30 days, 31–60 days, 61–90 days, 90+ days). It helps identify: - Overdue and potentially bad accounts - Effectiveness of collection efforts - The proportion of "slow-paying" customers - The need to revise credit policy *(7 marks: 5 factors × 1 mark + 2 marks for ageing schedule)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Credit Sales ($S$) = ₹1,80,00,000 - ACP ($N_1$) = 73 days - Variable cost ratio ($c$) = 0.75 - Required return ($r$) = 15% **Step 1 — Initial Investment in Receivables** $$I_{AR1} = \dfrac{S \times c \times N_1}{365} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 \times 73}{365}$$ $$I_{AR1} = \dfrac{98{,}55{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹27{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Initial Opportunity Cost** $$C_{op1} = I_{AR1} \times \dfrac{r}{100} = 27{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = ₹4{,}05{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Revised Investment (ACP = 45 days)** $$I_{AR2} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 \times 45}{365} = \dfrac{60{,}75{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹16{,}64{,}383.56$$ **Step 4 — Revised Opportunity Cost** $$C_{op2} = 16{,}64{,}383.56 \times 0.15 = ₹2{,}49{,}657.53$$ **Step 5 — Savings in Opportunity Cost** $$\boxed{\text{Savings} = C_{op1} - C_{op2} = 4{,}05{,}000 - 2{,}49{,}657.53 = ₹1{,}55{,}342.47}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key:** Full 7 marks require showing **every substitution** with intermediate values. Skipping the cost-ratio multiplication is a common 1-mark deduction. --- #### **Question B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** What is **Factoring**? Explain its types and discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of factoring from the perspective of an Indian engineering firm. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** M/s TechMech Industries is considering factoring its receivables. The factor offers: - Advance = **80%** of invoice value - Service charge (commission) = **2%** of invoice value - Interest on advance = **15% p.a.** - Average collection period = **60 days** Compute the **effective annual cost of factoring** and advise whether the firm should accept the offer if its current **bank loan rate is 13% p.a.** **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B** **Part (a) — Factoring Concept (7 Marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Factoring is a financial arrangement in which a business **sells its trade receivables** to a specialized financial institution (called a **Factor**) at a discount, in exchange for immediate cash, credit administration, and bad debt protection services. **Types of Factoring (3 marks):** 1. **Resource Factoring (with recourse)** — The seller bears the risk of bad debts; if the customer defaults, the seller must compensate the factor. 2. **Non-Resource Factoring (without recourse)** — The factor bears the bad debt risk; this is more expensive but provides complete protection. 3. **Advance Factoring** — The factor provides an immediate advance (typically 70–90%) of the receivables value; balance is paid on collection. 4. **Maturity Factoring** — The factor pays only on the maturity date of the receivables; mainly provides credit administration. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Immediate cash flow without waiting for customer payment - Professional debt collection reduces bad debts - Frees management from credit administration **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - Higher effective cost than bank loans (often 12–18% annualized) - Customer relationship is transferred to the factor - Not suitable for firms with strong bargaining power on credit terms --- **Part (b) — Effective Cost Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Advance fraction = 80% of invoice - Service charge (commission) = $d = 2\% = 0.02$ - Collection period = 60 days - Factor's interest on advance = 15% p.a. - Bank loan alternative rate = 13% p.a. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Formula** — when the factor deducts commission from the advance, the effective cost is computed on the **net amount received** by the client. **Step 1 — Net Amount Received per ₹100 of Invoice** $$\text{Advance received} = ₹80$$ $$\text{Less: Commission @ 2\%} = ₹2$$ $$\text{Net amount in hand} = ₹80 - ₹2 = ₹78$$ **Step 2 — Cost per ₹78 for 60 Days** The firm effectively borrows ₹78 but repays ₹80 after 60 days. $$\text{Cost per period} = \dfrac{80 - 78}{78} = \dfrac{2}{78} = 0.025641$$ **Step 3 — Annualize Using 365/60 Periods** $$C_{eff} = 0.025641 \times \dfrac{365}{60} = 0.025641 \times 6.0833$$ $$C_{eff} = 0.15598 \approx 15.60\% \text{ p.a.}$$ **Step 4 — Decision** $$\boxed{Effective\ Cost\ (15.60\%) > Bank\ Loan\ Rate\ (13.00\%)}$$ > **Recommendation:** The firm should **reject** the factoring offer and continue with the **bank loan at 13%**, unless factoring provides additional non-monetary benefits (credit insurance, professional collection) that offset the 2.60% cost differential. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Trade Receivable Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Cost Ratio ($c$):** Many students compute investment in receivables using **sales price** instead of **cost price**. The capital locked up is at cost, not at sales. (Deduct 1–2 marks) > > 2. **Confusing Days with Months:** Use **365 days** (annual basis) consistently; mixing 360 or 30-day months causes arithmetical errors. (Deduct 1 mark) > > 3. **Factoring Cost Formula Errors:** Using the simple interest rate instead of the **effective annualized cost** formula is a frequent 2-mark deduction. > > 4. **Ignoring Bad Debt Cost:** In marginal analysis, students often compute only opportunity cost and forget to add bad debt loss and discount cost. > > 5. **Not Defining Variables:** KTU 2024 valuation keys explicitly require listing **Given Data** at the start of the numerical solution. Skipping this loses 0.5 mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Trade Receivables** = Bills Receivable + Sundry Debtors (current asset, realized within one operating cycle). - **Four Costs of Receivables**: Opportunity Cost + Administrative Cost + Bad Debt Cost + Discount Cost. - **Key Formulas (must memorize):** - $ACP = \dfrac{Receivables}{Credit\ Sales} \times 365$ - $DSO = \dfrac{365}{ART\ Ratio}$ - $I_{AR} = \dfrac{S \times c \times N}{365}$ - $C_{op} = I_{AR} \times r$ - **Factoring effective cost** is computed on the **net amount received** (advance minus commission). - **Decision Rule:** Adopt a new credit policy **only if** $\Delta Benefit > \Delta Cost$. - **Ageing Schedule** is the primary monitoring tool — always classify by **0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+ days**. - **5 C's of Credit:** Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions — used for customer appraisal. - **Use 365 days** in all KTU calculations (not 360). - Always present numerical solutions with **Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer** structure for full marks. ---

topics.title: Trade Payables and Short-Term Financing; Operating Cycles; Capital Budgeting - Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods; Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade Payables and Short-Term Financing; Operating Cycles; Capital Budgeting - Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods; Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Trade Payables and Short-Term Financing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Trade Payables** represent the credit extended by suppliers (sellers) to a firm for the purchase of raw materials, components, or merchandise on credit. They are a spontaneous, interest-free source of short-term financing that arises automatically from the firm's ordinary course of business operations. The period for which credit is availed is governed by the **credit terms** negotiated with suppliers.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Grocery Store Credit:**
Imagine you are a small restaurant owner who buys vegetables daily from a wholesaler. The wholesaler says, *"Pay me within 30 days."* This 30-day credit is a **trade payable** — free money for 30 days. If you keep selling food and collecting cash from customers, then by the time you must pay the wholesaler, you have already used *their* money to run *your* business. The longer the supplier gives you credit (without penalty), the more working capital you save.

**Key Credit Terms Notation:**
- Terms like **"2/10, net 30"** mean: a **2% discount** is offered if payment is made within **10 days**; otherwise, the **full invoice amount** is due in **30 days**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Trade payables are classified as **spontaneous sources of short-term finance** because they arise automatically with purchases — the firm does not have to formally negotiate a loan. Other short-term financing sources include **commercial paper, bank overdraft, short-term loans, accrued expenses, and deferred income**.

---

### 1.2 Operating Cycle (Operating Cycle Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Operating Cycle** is the average time period between the acquisition of raw materials (or inventory) and the final collection of cash from sales (debtors). It measures the total length of time a firm's cash is tied up in its operating activities — from paying for inputs to receiving cash from customers.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Money's Round Trip:**
Think of operating cycle as the *time a ₹100 bill takes to come back to your wallet*. You pay ₹100 to buy raw materials → wait while it is converted into a finished product → store it as inventory → sell it on credit → wait for the customer to pay → finally cash returns to your hand. The shorter this round trip, the faster your business *recycles* cash and the less you need to borrow.

**Two Key Components:**
1. **Inventory Holding Period (IHP)** — Days raw materials/work-in-progress/finished goods sit before sale.
2. **Receivables Collection Period (RCP)** — Days taken to collect cash from credit customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Identity:**
> $$\text{Operating Cycle (OC)} = \text{Inventory Holding Period} + \text{Receivables Collection Period}$$
> $$\text{OC} = \text{IHP} + \text{RCP}$$
> When **trade payables deferral period** is considered, we get the **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)**.

---

### 1.3 Capital Budgeting — Conventional & DCF Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Capital Budgeting** is the process of evaluating and selecting long-term investment proposals that are expected to generate cash flows over multiple years. It involves comparing the expected future benefits of an investment against its initial outlay to determine whether the project is financially worthwhile.

**Intuitive Analogy — The House Purchase Decision:**
Capital budgeting is like deciding whether to buy a ₹50 lakh flat today that can be rented out for ₹25,000/month, or a ₹30 lakh flat giving ₹15,000/month. You compare the *future cash inflows* (rent) with the *present cost* (price), considering the **time value of money** — because ₹1 today is worth more than ₹1 received 5 years later.

**Two Broad Families of Methods:**

| Family | Core Idea | Time Value of Money? |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (Non-Discounted)** | Uses accounting profit/returns, ignores time value | **No** |
| **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** | Discounts future cash flows to present value | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional Methods covered in KTU Module 4:**
> 1. **Payback Period (PBP) Method**
> 2. **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) Method**
>
> **DCF Methods covered in KTU Module 4:**
> 1. **Net Present Value (NPV) Method**
> 2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method**
> 3. **Profitability Index (PI) Method**

---

### 1.4 Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Decision Criteria** are the standardized acceptance/rejection rules applied to capital budgeting evaluation results. They convert numerical outputs (years, %, ₹) into a clear *accept* or *reject* verdict, and extend to **mutually exclusive** vs. **independent** project selection scenarios.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Exam Passing Mark:**
Just as a student needs ≥ 40% to pass an exam, each capital budgeting method has a **benchmark (hurdle rate / cutoff)** — projects clearing the benchmark are accepted, others rejected. When two projects are competing for the same funds, additional tie-breaking rules (incremental analysis) come into play.

**Three Universal Decision Contexts:**
1. **Accept–Reject Decision** — Single project go/no-go.
2. **Mutually Exclusive Choice** — Pick *one* from many alternatives.
3. **Capital Rationing** — Limited budget, select best combination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Trade Payables — Cost & Credit Terms Analysis

#### A. Cost of Trade Credit (Foregone Discount)

When a supplier offers terms like *"2/10, net 30"*, paying late means **forgoing the cash discount**. The implicit annualized cost of this foregone discount is a critical KTU concept.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Discount offered = **d%** (e.g., 2%)
2. Credit period without discount = **n days** (e.g., 30)
3. Discount period = **p days** (e.g., 10)
4. If you pay on day 30 instead of day 10, you have *kept* the supplier's money for **(n − p)** extra days = 20 days
5. The cost is the **discount percentage** you gave up, annualized over the extra days kept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Annual Cost of Trade Credit Formula:**
> $$\text{Cost of Credit} = \frac{\text{Discount \%}}{100 - \text{Discount \%}} \times \frac{365}{\text{Net Period} - \text{Discount Period}}$$
>
> **Symbolically:**
> $$K_p = \frac{d}{1 - d} \times \frac{365}{n - p}$$
> where:
> - $K_p$ = effective annual cost of trade credit (as a decimal)
> - $d$ = discount rate (as decimal)
> - $n$ = net credit period (days)
> - $p$ = discount period (days)

#### B. When to Take the Discount — Decision Rule

- If the firm's **cost of borrowing** (e.g., bank loan interest rate) is **less than** $K_p$ → **Take the discount** and borrow from the bank to pay early.
- If the firm's **cost of borrowing** is **greater than** $K_p$ → **Forgo the discount** and use supplier's credit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Procurement managers in manufacturing firms use this exact logic to negotiate supplier terms, deciding whether to avail early-payment discounts or stretch payables to optimize working capital cost.

---

### 2.2 Operating Cycle — Theoretical Framework

#### A. Detailed Period Decomposition

The operating cycle breaks down into sub-periods, each of which can be computed separately:

1. **Raw Material Storage Period (RMP)** — Days raw materials sit before being used.
2. **Work-in-Progress Holding Period (WIP)** — Days semi-finished goods remain in production.
3. **Finished Goods Storage Period (FGP)** — Days finished goods sit before being sold.
4. **Receivables Collection Period (RCP)** — Days credit sales remain unpaid.

#### B. Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)

When we subtract the **payables deferral period** (time taken to pay suppliers), we get the **net cash cycle**:

$$\text{CCC} = \text{OC} - \text{Payables Deferral Period}$$

> The CCC tells the firm *how many days it must finance operations from its own funds (or borrowed funds)* before cash flows back in.

#### C. KTU Formula Sheet — Operating Cycle

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory Holding Period (IHP) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365$ | Days inventory is held |
| Receivables Collection Period (RCP) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Debtors}}{\text{Credit Sales}} \times 365$ | Days to collect receivables |
| Payables Deferral Period (PDP) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases}} \times 365$ | Days to pay suppliers |
| Operating Cycle (OC) | $\text{IHP} + \text{RCP}$ | Total cash tied up |
| Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) | $\text{OC} - \text{PDP}$ | Net financing need |

---

### 2.3 Capital Budgeting — Conventional (Non-Discounted) Methods

#### A. Payback Period (PBP) Method

**Definition:** The **Payback Period** is the time required for a project's cumulative cash inflows to recover the original investment outlay.

**Steps:**
1. List annual after-tax cash inflows.
2. Compute cumulative cash inflows year by year.
3. Identify the year in which cumulative cash flow ≥ initial investment.
4. Interpolate if recovery occurs mid-year.

**Decision Rule (Simple PBP):**
- If PBP ≤ **Target Payback Period** → **Accept**
- If PBP > Target → **Reject**

**Decision Rule (Discounted PBP):**
- Use **discounted cash flows** instead of raw cash flows. Same procedure, but with PVs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitations of PBP (frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):**
> 1. Ignores cash flows **after** the payback period.
> 2. **Ignores time value of money** (in simple PBP).
> 3. **No objective benchmark** — target period is subjective.

#### B. Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) Method

**Definition:** ARR expresses a project's average accounting profit as a percentage of the **average investment**.

**Steps:**
1. Compute **Average Annual Profit** = (Total profit over life) / (Project life)
2. Compute **Average Investment** = (Initial cost − Salvage value) / 2  *(for straight-line depreciation)*, OR Initial cost / 2.
3. ARR = (Average Annual Profit / Average Investment) × 100

**Decision Rule:**
- If ARR ≥ **Target Rate (Cost of Capital or hurdle rate)** → **Accept**
- Else → **Reject**

---

### 2.4 Capital Budgeting — DCF Methods

#### A. Net Present Value (NPV) Method

**Definition:** NPV is the **sum of the present values of all expected future cash flows** (inflows minus outflows) over the project's life, discounted at the firm's **cost of capital**, minus the initial investment.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t} - CF_0$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital)
- $CF_0$ = Initial investment (year 0)
- $n$ = Project life in years

**Decision Rules:**
- NPV > 0 → **Accept** (project adds value)
- NPV = 0 → **Indifferent** (project breaks even in PV terms)
- NPV < 0 → **Reject** (project destroys value)

**Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Select the project with the **highest positive NPV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPV is considered the *theoretically superior* method** in KTU examinations because:
> 1. It considers **all** cash flows over the project's life.
> 2. It accounts for the **time value of money**.
> 3. It uses the **objective cost of capital** as the discount rate.
> 4. It **maximizes shareholder wealth** (the firm's primary objective).

#### B. Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method

**Definition:** IRR is the discount rate that makes the **NPV of a project equal to zero**. It is the project's *break-even rate of return*.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + \text{IRR})^t} - CF_0$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If IRR > Cost of Capital ($r$) → **Accept**
- If IRR < Cost of Capital → **Reject**
- If IRR = Cost of Capital → Indifferent

**Computing IRR (Trial & Error / Interpolation):**

$$\text{IRR} = r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$$

Where:
- $r_L$ = lower rate (gives positive NPV)
- $r_H$ = higher rate (gives negative NPV)

> [!WARNING]
> **Multiple IRR Problem:** When cash flows change sign more than once (e.g., initial outflow, then inflows, then a large terminal outflow), the IRR equation has **multiple roots**. KTU examinations often test this with an explicit warning to prefer NPV in such cases.

#### C. Profitability Index (PI) Method

**Definition:** PI is the ratio of the **present value of future cash inflows** to the **initial investment**.

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{CF_0} = \frac{\text{PV of Inflows}}{\text{Initial Outlay}}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- PI > 1 → **Accept**
- PI < 1 → **Reject**
- PI = 1 → Indifferent

**Relationship to NPV:**

$$\text{NPV} = (\text{PI} - 1) \times CF_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PI is preferred over NPV under capital rationing** (limited budget), because PI measures *value created per rupee invested*, allowing ranking when funds are constrained.

---

### 2.5 Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet — Capital Budgeting

| Method | Formula | Decision Rule (Accept if...) |
|---|---|---|
| Payback Period (Simple) | Cumulative CF year = Initial Cost | PBP ≤ Target Period |
| Discounted Payback | Cumulative Discounted CF = Initial Cost | DPBP ≤ Target |
| ARR | $\dfrac{\text{Avg Annual Profit}}{\text{Avg Investment}} \times 100$ | ARR ≥ Target Rate |
| NPV | $\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - CF_0$ | NPV > 0 |
| IRR | Rate where NPV = 0 | IRR > Cost of Capital |
| PI | $\dfrac{\text{PV of Inflows}}{\text{Initial Outlay}}$ | PI > 1 |

---

### 2.6 Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting — Selection Rules

**A. Accept–Reject (Single Independent Project):**
- Use the cut-off/hurdle directly: NPV > 0, IRR > $r$, PI > 1, PBP ≤ target → Accept.

**B. Mutually Exclusive Projects (Pick One):**
1. **NPV Method:** Choose highest positive NPV.
2. **IRR / PI Method:** Use **Incremental Analysis** — compute $\Delta$NPV, $\Delta$IRR, or $\Delta$PI between projects ranked by investment size.
3. **Conflict Resolution:** NPV and IRR may give different rankings due to **scale** (size) or **timing** of cash flows. **Always prefer NPV.**

**C. Capital Rationing (Budget Constraint):**
- Rank projects by **Profitability Index (PI)** in descending order.
- Allocate funds to highest-PI projects until budget exhausted.
- Combine projects to **maximize total NPV** within the budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Engineering project managers (e.g., in plant expansion, R&D investment, machinery purchase) use these exact rules to evaluate proposals and present defendable recommendations to boards, ensuring capital is deployed to maximize firm value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Cost of Trade Credit — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A supplier offers credit terms **"3/15, net 45"**. Compute the effective annual cost of forgoing the discount. The firm's cost of bank borrowing is **12%**. Should the firm take the discount?

**Given:**
- Discount rate $d = 3\% = 0.03$
- Discount period $p = 15$ days
- Net credit period $n = 45$ days
- Cost of borrowing = $12\% = 0.12$

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters and substitute into the formula.**

The general formula is:

$$K_p = \frac{d}{1 - d} \times \frac{365}{n - p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$K_p = \frac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} \times \frac{365}{45 - 15}$$

$$K_p = \frac{0.03}{0.97} \times \frac{365}{30}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each fraction separately.**

$$\frac{0.03}{0.97} = 0.030928$$

$$\frac{365}{30} = 12.1667$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the two factors.**

$$K_p = 0.030928 \times 12.1667$$

$$K_p = 0.3763 \quad \text{(or } 37.63\% \text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the firm's cost of borrowing.**

Cost of forgoing discount ($37.63\%$) $\gg$ Cost of bank borrowing ($12\%$).

**Step 6 — Decision.**

Since the firm can borrow at 12% and **save** the 3% discount, paying early and borrowing is much cheaper. Therefore → **TAKE THE DISCOUNT** and pay on day 15 using borrowed funds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Whenever $K_p$ > cost of capital, the discount is essentially a *reward* of more than 37% annualized — never refuse such an offer.

---

### 3.2 Operating Cycle — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
From the following data of a manufacturing firm, compute the **Operating Cycle** and the **Cash Conversion Cycle**:
- Raw Material Consumption = ₹ 24,00,000
- Average Raw Material Inventory = ₹ 2,00,000
- Work-in-Progress (average) = ₹ 3,00,000
- Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) = ₹ 60,00,000
- Average Finished Goods Inventory = ₹ 5,00,000
- Credit Sales = ₹ 80,00,000
- Average Debtors = ₹ 8,00,000
- Credit Purchases = ₹ 36,00,000
- Average Creditors (Trade Payables) = ₹ 3,00,000
- (All data for the year, 365 days)

**Step 1 — Compute Raw Material Storage Period (RMP).**

$$\text{RMP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Raw Material Inventory}}{\text{Raw Material Consumed}} \times 365$$

$$\text{RMP} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.0833 \times 365 = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Work-in-Progress Holding Period.**

In the KTU standard simplification, WIP is converted using its share in total cost:

$$\text{WIP Period} = \frac{\text{Avg. WIP}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365 = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 18.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Finished Goods Holding Period (FGHP).**

$$\text{FGHP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Finished Goods}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365 = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Total Inventory Holding Period.**

$$\text{IHP} = \text{RMP} + \text{WIP Period} + \text{FGHP}$$

$$\text{IHP} = 30.42 + 18.25 + 30.42 = 79.09 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Receivables Collection Period (RCP).**

$$\text{RCP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Debtors}}{\text{Credit Sales}} \times 365 = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Operating Cycle.**

$$\text{OC} = \text{IHP} + \text{RCP} = 79.09 + 36.5 = 115.59 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7 — Payables Deferral Period (PDP).**

$$\text{PDP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Creditors}}{\text{Credit Purchases}} \times 365 = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{36{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 8 — Cash Conversion Cycle.**

$$\text{CCC} = \text{OC} - \text{PDP} = 115.59 - 30.42 = 85.17 \text{ days}$$

**Interpretation:** The firm's cash is tied up for **about 85 days** before it returns through operations; finance teams aim to reduce this through faster inventory turnover and quicker receivables collection.

---

### 3.3 Payback Period — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A project requires an initial investment of **₹ 5,00,000** and generates the following after-tax cash inflows:

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1,50,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 |
| 4 | 1,20,000 |
| 5 | 90,000 |

Compute the **Payback Period**. Target payback = 3 years.

**Step 1 — Compute cumulative cash flows year by year.**

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 3,30,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 5,30,000 |
| 4 | 1,20,000 | 6,50,000 |
| 5 | 90,000 | 7,40,000 |

**Step 2 — Identify the recovery year.**

Initial investment ₹ 5,00,000 is recovered during **Year 3** (cumulative jumps from ₹ 3,30,000 to ₹ 5,30,000).

**Step 3 — Interpolate the fractional year.**

$$\text{PBP} = 2 + \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}30{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\text{PBP} = 2 + \frac{1{,}70{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2 + 0.85 = 2.85 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since 2.85 years < 3 years target → **ACCEPT the project.**

---

### 3.4 NPV — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A firm is evaluating a project with the following data:
- Initial Investment = ₹ 10,00,000
- Project life = 5 years
- Annual Cash Inflow (after tax) = ₹ 3,00,000
- Salvage Value = ₹ 50,000 (received at end of Year 5)
- Cost of Capital = 10%

Compute NPV and decide.

**Step 1 — Identify cash flows.**

$$CF_0 = -10{,}00{,}000$$
$$CF_1 = CF_2 = CF_3 = CF_4 = 3{,}00{,}000$$
$$CF_5 = 3{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Use the annuity present value factor for years 1 to 4 and the single PV factor for year 5 (with salvage).**

$$\text{PVIFA}(10\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-4}}{0.10} = 3.1699$$

$$\text{PVIF}(10\%, 5) = (1.10)^{-5} = 0.6209$$

**Step 3 — Compute PV of years 1–4 cash flows.**

$$\text{PV}_{1-4} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1699 = 9{,}50{,}970$$

**Step 4 — Compute PV of year 5 cash flow (operating + salvage).**

$$\text{PV}_5 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 21{,}73{,}150$$

> **Note:** Correction — let me recompute: $3,50,000 \times 0.6209 = 2,17,315$.

$$\text{PV}_5 = 2{,}17{,}315$$

**Step 5 — Total PV of inflows.**

$$\text{Total PV} = 9{,}50{,}970 + 2{,}17{,}315 = 11{,}68{,}285$$

**Step 6 — NPV.**

$$\text{NPV} = 11{,}68{,}285 - 10{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}68{,}285$$

**Step 7 — Decision.**

NPV > 0 → **ACCEPT the project.** The project adds ₹ 1,68,285 in present-value terms.

---

### 3.5 IRR — Worked Numerical (Interpolation Method)

**Problem:**
A project requires ₹ 1,00,000 initial investment and yields ₹ 30,000 per year for 5 years. Compute IRR.

**Step 1 — Set up the equation.**

$$0 = -1{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{30{,}000}{(1+r)^t}$$

$$1{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}000 \times \text{PVIFA}(r, 5)$$

$$\text{PVIFA}(r, 5) = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = 3.3333$$

**Step 2 — Locate the factor in the annuity table.**

| Rate $r$ | PVIFA$(r, 5)$ |
|---|---|
| 15% | 3.3522 |
| 16% | 3.2743 |

Our factor 3.3333 lies between 15% and 16% → $r_L = 15\%$, $r_H = 16\%$.

**Step 3 — Compute NPV at each rate.**

At $r_L = 15\%$:

$$\text{NPV}_L = -1{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 \times 3.3522 = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}566 = +566$$

At $r_H = 16\%$:

$$\text{NPV}_H = -1{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 \times 3.2743 = -1{,}00{,}000 + 98{,}229 = -1{,}771$$

**Step 4 — Apply the interpolation formula.**

$$\text{IRR} = r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + \frac{566}{566 - (-1{,}771)} \times (16 - 15)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + \frac{566}{2{,}337} \times 1$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + 0.2422 = 15.24\%$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

If cost of capital is 10%, then IRR (15.24%) > 10% → **ACCEPT**.

---

### 3.6 Profitability Index — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A project requires ₹ 5,00,000 and generates cash inflows with present value of ₹ 6,50,000. Compute PI and decide.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{PV of Inflows}}{\text{Initial Outlay}} = \frac{6{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.30$$

**Step 2 — Decision.**

PI (1.30) > 1 → **ACCEPT.** Each ₹1 invested generates ₹1.30 in PV terms, i.e., ₹0.30 of NPV per ₹1 invested.

---

### 3.7 Mutually Exclusive Projects — Incremental NPV

**Problem:**
Two projects, A and B, are mutually exclusive. Both have 3-year life and cost of capital 10%.

| Project | Initial Investment | Annual CF | NPV @ 10% |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | ₹ 5,00,000 | ₹ 2,00,000 | ? |
| B | ₹ 8,00,000 | ₹ 3,10,000 | ? |

**Step 1 — Compute NPV of A.**

$$\text{PVIFA}(10\%, 3) = 2.4869$$

$$\text{NPV}_A = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.4869 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}97{,}380 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}620$$

Project A is **rejected** (NPV < 0).

**Step 2 — Compute NPV of B.**

$$\text{NPV}_B = 3{,}10{,}000 \times 2.4869 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}70{,}939 - 8{,}00{,}000 = -29{,}061$$

Project B is also **rejected** (NPV < 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Neither project is acceptable. The proper KTU answer is *reject both*, not "choose the better one." Always apply the absolute NPV > 0 rule first.

**Counter-Example — Both Positive:**

Suppose Project A: NPV = ₹ 1,00,000; PI = 1.20.
Project B: NPV = ₹ 1,50,000; PI = 1.10.

Even though PI(A) > PI(B), since they are **mutually exclusive**, choose **B** because NPV(B) > NPV(A) → B creates more absolute wealth.

---

### 3.8 ARR — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A machine costing ₹ 6,00,000 has a useful life of 4 years with no salvage value. Expected annual profit before depreciation is ₹ 1,80,000.

**Step 1 — Compute depreciation.**

$$\text{Depreciation} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 0}{4} = ₹ 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ p.a.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute average annual profit (after depreciation).**

$$\text{Avg. Profit} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹ 30{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute average investment.**

$$\text{Avg. Investment} = \frac{\text{Initial Cost} + \text{Salvage}}{2} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 0}{2} = ₹ 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — ARR.**

$$\text{ARR} = \frac{30{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

If target ARR (cost of capital) = 12%, then ARR (10%) < 12% → **REJECT.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Trade Payables. State two merits and two limitations of trade credit as a source of short-term finance.**

**Model Answer:**
Trade payables represent the amount owed by a firm to its suppliers for goods or services purchased on credit. They are a *spontaneous* source of short-term finance that arises automatically with the firm's purchases and does not require formal negotiation or interest payment.

**Merits:**
1. **Spontaneous and flexible** — increases automatically with purchases.
2. **No explicit interest cost** — unlike bank loans, no interest is paid if paid within the credit period.

**Limitations:**
1. Over-reliance may damage the firm's credit reputation with suppliers.
2. Loss of cash discounts — delaying payment beyond the discount period forfeits discount benefits.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Distinguish between Operating Cycle and Cash Conversion Cycle.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Operating Cycle (OC) | Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Time from acquisition of inventory to collection of cash from sales | Time the firm must finance its operations from external sources |
| Components | Inventory Period + Receivables Period | Operating Cycle − Payables Deferral Period |
| Measures | Gross cash tie-up | Net cash tie-up |
| Formula | $\text{OC} = \text{IHP} + \text{RCP}$ | $\text{CCC} = \text{OC} - \text{PDP}$ |

---

> **Q3.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **What is meant by "Time Value of Money"? Why is it important in Capital Budgeting?**

**Model Answer:**
Time Value of Money (TVM) refers to the economic principle that **a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received in the future** because of the earning potential of money (interest/inflation).

**Importance in Capital Budgeting:**
1. It enables **comparison of cash flows occurring at different times** on a common (present-value) basis.
2. It ensures the discount rate reflects the **opportunity cost of capital**.
3. Without TVM, projects with delayed returns would unfairly appear as attractive as those yielding immediate returns.

---

> **Q4.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
> **List any three limitations of the Payback Period method.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Ignores time value of money** (in the simple version).
2. **Ignores cash flows after the payback period** — a project with large later benefits may be wrongly rejected.
3. **Subjective cutoff** — the target payback period is set arbitrarily, not derived from market data.
4. **Bias towards short-term projects** — discourages long-gestation R&D or infrastructure projects.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A firm offers credit terms **"2/10, net 45"**. The firm's bank offers a loan at **14%** interest per annum.
(i) Calculate the effective annual cost of forgoing the cash discount. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Should the firm take the discount or not? Justify. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** From the following data of a manufacturing firm, compute:
(i) Raw Material Storage Period, **(1 mark)**
(ii) Work-in-Progress Period, **(1 mark)**
(iii) Finished Goods Holding Period, **(1 mark)**
(iv) Receivables Collection Period, **(1 mark)**
(v) Operating Cycle, **(1 mark)**
(vi) Cash Conversion Cycle. **(2 marks)**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Raw Material Consumed | 30,00,000 |
| Average Raw Material Inventory | 3,00,000 |
| Average Work-in-Progress | 4,00,000 |
| Total Production Cost | 75,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 75,00,000 |
| Average Finished Goods Inventory | 6,00,000 |
| Credit Sales | 90,00,000 |
| Average Debtors | 10,00,000 |
| Credit Purchases | 40,00,000 |
| Average Creditors | 4,00,000 |

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (a):**

**Given:**
$d = 2\% = 0.02$, $p = 10$ days, $n = 45$ days.

**Effective Annual Cost of Trade Credit:**

$$K_p = \frac{d}{1 - d} \times \frac{365}{n - p}$$

$$K_p = \frac{0.02}{0.98} \times \frac{365}{35}$$

$$K_p = 0.020408 \times 10.4286 = 0.2128 \text{ or } 21.28\%$$

**Decision:**
Bank borrowing cost (14%) **<** $K_p$ (21.28%) → The firm should **TAKE the discount** by paying on day 10 and borrowing from the bank at 14%. **Net benefit ≈ 21.28% − 14% = 7.28% per annum** on the funds used for early payment.

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (b):**

**Step (i) — Raw Material Storage Period (RMP):**

$$\text{RMP} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.10 \times 365 = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step (ii) — Work-in-Progress Period (WIP):**

$$\text{WIP Period} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.05333 \times 365 = 19.47 \text{ days}$$

**Step (iii) — Finished Goods Holding Period (FGHP):**

$$\text{FGHP} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.08 \times 365 = 29.2 \text{ days}$$

**Step (iv) — Receivables Collection Period (RCP):**

$$\text{RCP} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{90{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.1111 \times 365 = 40.56 \text{ days}$$

**Step (v) — Operating Cycle (OC):**

$$\text{OC} = \text{IHP} + \text{RCP}$$

$$\text{IHP} = 36.5 + 19.47 + 29.2 = 85.17 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{OC} = 85.17 + 40.56 = 125.73 \text{ days}$$

**Step (vi) — Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC):**

$$\text{PDP} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{CCC} = 125.73 - 36.5 = 89.23 \text{ days}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects with the following data (cost of capital = 12%):

| Project | Initial Investment | Annual Cash Inflow | Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| X | ₹ 6,00,000 | ₹ 2,40,000 | 4 years |
| Y | ₹ 8,00,000 | ₹ 3,00,000 | 4 years |

Compute NPV, IRR (using interpolation), and PI for each project. Recommend the project the firm should accept. **(10 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why NPV is considered the theoretically superior method over IRR and PI for mutually exclusive projects. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (a):**

**Step 1 — Discount factors at 12%:**

$$\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-4}}{0.12} = 3.0373$$

**Step 2 — NPV of Project X:**

$$\text{NPV}_X = 2{,}40{,}000 \times 3.0373 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}28{,}952 - 6{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}28{,}952$$

**Step 3 — NPV of Project Y:**

$$\text{NPV}_Y = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.0373 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}11{,}190 - 8{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}11{,}190$$

**Step 4 — IRR of Project X (interpolation):**

Set $\text{PVIFA}(r, 4) = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 2.5000$

Try $r = 21\%$: $\text{PVIFA}(21\%, 4) = 2.5404$ → NPV positive.
Try $r = 22\%$: $\text{PVIFA}(22\%, 4) = 2.4936$ → NPV negative.

$$\text{IRR}_X = 21 + \frac{2.5404 - 2.5000}{2.5404 - 2.4936} \times 1 = 21 + \frac{0.0404}{0.0468} = 21.86\%$$

**Step 5 — IRR of Project Y (interpolation):**

Set $\text{PVIFA}(r, 4) = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 2.6667$

Try $r = 17\%$: $\text{PVIFA}(17\%, 4) = 2.7432$ → NPV positive.
Try $r = 18\%$: $\text{PVIFA}(18\%, 4) = 2.6901$ → NPV positive.
Try $r = 19\%$: $\text{PVIFA}(19\%, 4) = 2.6386$ → NPV negative.

$$\text{IRR}_Y = 18 + \frac{2.6901 - 2.6667}{2.6901 - 2.6386} \times 1 = 18 + \frac{0.0234}{0.0515} = 18.45\%$$

**Step 6 — Profitability Index:**

$$\text{PI}_X = \frac{7{,}28{,}952}{6{,}00{,}000} = 1.215$$

$$\text{PI}_Y = \frac{9{,}11{,}190}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.139$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

| Metric | Project X | Project Y | Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|
| NPV | 1,28,952 | 1,11,190 | **X (Higher NPV)** |
| IRR | 21.86% | 18.45% | X |
| PI | 1.215 | 1.139 | X |

Both NPV > 0 → both acceptable individually; but since they are mutually exclusive, **select Project X** because its NPV (₹ 1,28,952) > NPV of Y (₹ 1,11,190). All three methods agree here.

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (b):**

**Why NPV is theoretically superior:**

1. **Direct wealth-maximization link:** NPV measures the absolute rupee increase in firm value. Accepting all positive-NPV projects is mathematically guaranteed to maximize shareholder wealth.

2. **Reinvestment assumption realism:** NPV implicitly assumes reinvestment at the **cost of capital** (which is realistic). IRR implicitly assumes reinvestment at the **IRR itself**, which is often unrealistic and can overstate returns.

3. **Scale and timing conflicts resolved:** When projects differ in size or cash-flow timing, NPV and IRR may give **conflicting rankings**. NPV (being the absolute-value metric) consistently selects the project that truly adds more wealth — IRR may favor smaller but high-percentage-return projects that destroy absolute value.

4. **No multiple-roots problem:** IRR may yield multiple solutions for non-conventional cash flows; NPV always yields a single value.

5. **Additivity property:** NPVs of independent projects can be summed directly to compute total firm value impact, which IRR cannot do.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Failing to interpolate** the fractional year in Payback Period — examiners award 1 mark separately for the interpolation formula.
> 2. **Using 360 instead of 365 days** in trade-credit cost calculation — KTU 2024 scheme strictly follows the **365-day year**.
> 3. **Choosing IRR over NPV** in mutually exclusive conflicts — this is a *guaranteed* loss of 2–3 marks because KTU boards explicitly test the "NPV supremacy" concept.
> 4. **Forgetting the salvage value** when computing year-5 NPV — many students omit terminal cash flows.
> 5. **Confusing Operating Cycle with Cash Conversion Cycle** — OC includes payables, CCC excludes them. Examiners mark this strictly.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Trade Payables** = spontaneous, interest-free credit from suppliers.
- **Cost of trade credit** formula uses **365 days** (KTU 2024 standard), not 360.
- **Take the discount** when $K_p$ > firm's cost of borrowing.
- **Operating Cycle (OC)** = IHP + RCP.
- **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** = OC − PDP.
- IHP includes Raw Material Period + WIP Period + Finished Goods Period.
- **Payback Period** ignores time value of money (simple PBP) and post-recovery cash flows.
- **ARR** uses **accounting profit** (after depreciation), not cash flow.
- **NPV = Σ PV of inflows − Initial investment.** Accept if NPV > 0.
- **IRR** is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0. Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital.
- **PI = PV of inflows / Initial investment.** Accept if PI > 1.
- For **mutually exclusive projects**, **always use NPV** (NPV > IRR > PI for selection).
- For **capital rationing**, use **PI ranking** to maximize NPV within budget.
- **Discounted Payback Period** uses discounted cash flows and is preferred over simple PBP.
- **Multiple IRR problem** occurs with non-conventional cash flows — prefer NPV.
- **Salvage value** must be added to the **final-year cash flow**, not ignored.
- **Depreciation** is a non-cash expense — it does **not** enter NPV/IRR/PBP calculations (only matters for ARR).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Present Value (NPV) - Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Comparisons
