topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET831 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital Rationing and Risk Analysis; Introduction to International Capital Budgeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capital Rationing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Rationing (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> Capital rationing is the financial situation in which a firm has a finite, restricted pool of investable funds and is therefore forced to **select a subset** of otherwise acceptable capital budgeting projects, even though each rejected project would have a positive NPV in isolation. The selection objective shifts from *"accept all positive-NPV projects"* to *"maximize total NPV subject to a budget constraint"*.

In the **unconstrained world** of Modigliani-Miller, a firm accepts every project with $\text{NPV} > 0$. In the **real (rationed) world**, the firm's board imposes a ceiling on total capital expenditure, often because:
- Management imposes a self-imposed limit (internal capital market discipline).
- External lenders refuse additional debt.
- Equity dilution is undesirable in a particular financial year.

The rationing problem is therefore an **optimization problem under a linear constraint** — mathematically a *bounded knapsack problem*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Birthday Party Budget"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — A Student's Birthday Party**
> Imagine you have **₹5,000** to spend on a birthday party. Your friends suggest 4 activities: Bowling (₹2,000, fun rating 8), Movie (₹1,000, fun rating 5), Arcade (₹2,500, fun rating 7), Camping trip (₹4,000, fun rating 9). You can't do all four (₹9,500). You must pick the **combination** that gives the **maximum total fun within ₹5,000**.
> - Bowling + Movie = ₹3,000, fun = 13
> - Bowling + Arcade = ₹4,500, fun = 15 ✅
> - Movie + Arcade = ₹3,500, fun = 12
> - Camping alone = ₹4,000, fun = 9
>
> This is *exactly* capital rationing — maximize total "NPV" (fun) under a budget cap (₹5,000).

### Types of Capital Rationing

| Type | Meaning | Real-world cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Capital Rationing** | External market forces prevent the firm from raising more capital (debt/equity markets closed, lender refusal). | Recession, credit crunch, regulatory ceiling on borrowing. |
| **Soft Capital Rationing** | Internal management decision to limit capital expenditure (e.g., to maintain target debt-equity ratio, EPS, or ROI). | Retained earnings policy, dividend coverage, internal ROI target. |

---

## 1.2 Risk Analysis in Capital Budgeting — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk in Capital Budgeting**
> Risk, in the context of capital budgeting, is the **variability (dispersion) of the actual returns** of a project relative to its **expected returns**. A project is "riskier" if its cash-flow probability distribution has a higher **standard deviation** or **coefficient of variation**. Risk is distinct from uncertainty — risk involves **measurable probabilities**, while true uncertainty does not.

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to master three layers of risk:
1. **Stand-alone (project) risk** — variability of a project's own cash flows.
2. **Corporate (within-firm) risk** — variability of the firm's total earnings, considering project correlation.
3. **Market (beta) risk** — variability of a project's return relative to the market portfolio.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Commute to College"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Commuting Risk**
> - **Walking**: Predictable time (35–40 min), small risk. → *Low risk, low variance*.
> - **Public Bus**: Usually 30 min, but can be 25 or 60 min depending on traffic. → *Medium risk*.
> - **Own Bike**: Usually 20 min, but accidents/breakdown possible → *High downside risk*.
> Capital budgeting risk is the same — the *expected NPV* is your "average commute time", and the *standard deviation* is the spread of possible outcomes.

---

## 1.3 International Capital Budgeting — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Capital Budgeting (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> International capital budgeting is the process of evaluating and selecting **foreign investment projects** (greenfield, joint venture, acquisition) in which cash inflows and outflows are denominated in **foreign currencies**, subject to **additional layers of risk** that are absent in purely domestic projects — primarily **foreign exchange (FX) risk** and **country (political/sovereign) risk**.

The key insight: *the same NPV formula applies, but the inputs change* — discount rate must incorporate country risk premium, and future cash flows must be either (a) forecasted in foreign currency and discounted at the foreign discount rate, then converted at the **spot rate**, or (b) converted to home currency using **expected future exchange rates** and discounted at the home discount rate.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Currency Exchange Stall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Studying Abroad**
> Your friend in the USA sends you $1,000 today. If the rupee is at ₹83/\$ and you keep it for a year, the dollar may appreciate to ₹90/\$ or depreciate to ₹80/\$. Your "return" depends on **two things**: how the dollar *grows* in the US (interest) AND how the exchange rate *moves*. International capital budgeting combines both sources of return into one NPV calculation.

### Additional Layers of Risk in International Projects

| Risk Layer | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Exchange Rate Risk** | Future spot rate may deviate from forecast, altering home-currency cash flows. | Forward contracts, currency options, natural hedging. |
| **Country (Political) Risk** | Expropriation, currency inconvertibility, war, tax law changes. | Country risk premium in discount rate, political risk insurance (MIGA). |
| **Inflation Rate Differential** | PPP deviations between home and host country. | Use real vs nominal consistently. |
| **Tax Repatriation Risk** | Withholding tax, blocked fund transfers. | Treat after-tax foreign cash flows only. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite:** The examiner *loves* asking for the formula of **Adjusted Discount Rate** for a foreign project:
> $$\text{ADR} = R_f + \beta(\text{Global Market Risk Premium}) + \text{Country Risk Premium}$$
> Memorize this — it appears almost every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capital Rationing — Operational Logic

The unconstrained NPV rule fails under rationing. We need a **constrained-selection rule**.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **List all positive-NPV projects.** Each project $i$ has:
   - Initial investment: $I_i$ (cash outflow at $t=0$).
   - NPV at the firm's hurdle rate: $\text{NPV}_i$.
2. **Impose the budget constraint:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot x_i \le B$$
   where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (1 = accept, 0 = reject) and $B$ is the capital budget.
3. **Maximize total NPV:**
$$\text{Maximize } \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{NPV}_i \cdot x_i \quad \text{subject to } \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot x_i \le B$$

### Three Practical Selection Methods

#### Method A — Profitability Index (PI) Ranking
$$\text{PI}_i = \frac{\text{PV of future cash flows of project } i}{I_i}$$

**Rule:** Rank projects by $\text{PI}$ in **descending order** and accept projects in this order until the budget is exhausted. This is the most popular KTU-board method because it gives the *NPV per rupee invested* — analogous to picking the highest "fun per rupee" in the birthday party analogy.

> [!CAUTION]
> **PI Pitfall:** When the last project is *partially fundable* (i.e., budget exhausted in the middle of a project), two sub-approaches exist:
> - **Divisible Projects (Fractional PI):** Accept the fraction of the project that exhausts the budget. Optimal but assumes divisibility.
> - **Indivisible Projects (Integer Programming):** Either take 100% or 0%. Requires **Integer Linear Programming (ILP)** or trial combinations.

#### Method B — NPV Ranking
Simple — rank by $\text{NPV}_i$ descending. *Tends to favour large projects* and may exhaust the budget on one or two projects. **Generally inferior to PI** because it ignores capital efficiency.

#### Method C — Integer Programming (Mathematical Programming)
For indivisible projects, formulate as a **0–1 Integer Linear Program**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{NPV}_i \, x_i \\
\text{Subject to: } & \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \, x_i \le B \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU does **not** require solving ILPs by hand — you must *formulate* the LP. The numerical solution is via branch-and-bound (computer-based).

---

## 2.2 Risk Analysis Methods — Complete Theoretical Breakdown

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups risk methods into three families:

### Family 1 — Sensitivity Analysis ("What-if" Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Examines how the NPV changes when **one input variable** is varied, holding all others at their expected (base-case) values. The variable that causes the largest swing in NPV is the *most critical*.

**Steps:**
1. Compute the base-case NPV using expected values of all inputs.
2. Vary **one** input (e.g., sales volume) by ±X%, recompute NPV.
3. Compute the **degree of sensitivity**:
$$\text{Sensitivity}_{j} = \frac{\Delta \text{NPV}}{\text{NPV}_{\text{base}}} \Big/ \frac{\Delta X_j}{X_{j,\text{base}}}$$
4. The variable with the **highest absolute sensitivity** is the most critical — management should focus on getting its forecast right.

**Output Tool:** A tornado diagram (variable with biggest impact at top, smallest at bottom).

### Family 2 — Scenario Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Computes NPV under **discrete, internally consistent scenarios** (e.g., best case, base case, worst case) where *multiple variables* are varied simultaneously in a coherent way.

$$\text{Expected NPV} = \sum_{s} p_s \cdot \text{NPV}_s$$

where $p_s$ is the probability of scenario $s$ and $\sum p_s = 1$.

### Family 3 — Statistical / Probabilistic Methods

#### (a) Standard Deviation of NPV
If the project has $n$ possible outcomes with $\text{NPV}_i$ and probability $p_i$:

$$\sigma_{\text{NPV}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot (\text{NPV}_i - E[\text{NPV}])^2}$$

#### (b) Coefficient of Variation (CV)
A **scale-free** risk measure — useful for comparing projects of different size.

$$\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{NPV}}}{E[\text{NPV}]}$$

Lower CV → relatively less risky.

#### (c) Certainty Equivalent Method
Replace each risky cash flow $CF_t$ with a **certainty equivalent** $\alpha_t \cdot CF_t$ where $0 \le \alpha_t \le 1$ is the *certainty equivalent coefficient*. Then discount at the **risk-free rate** $R_f$.

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\alpha_t \cdot CF_t}{(1+R_f)^t} - I_0$$

#### (d) Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate (RADR)
A single rate $k > R_f$ that embeds the project's risk premium is used to discount the **expected cash flows**.

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{E[CF_t]}{(1+k)^t} - I_0$$

The relationship between the two methods:

$$k = R_f + \text{Risk Premium} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \alpha_t = \frac{1}{(1+\text{Risk Premium}_t)^t}$$

---

## 2.3 International Capital Budgeting — Theoretical Framework

### The Two Equivalent Methodologies

**Method 1 — Foreign-Currency Approach (FC):**
Forecast cash flows in **foreign currency**, discount at the **foreign discount rate**, compute NPV in FC, then convert to home currency at the **current spot rate**.

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{Home}} = \frac{\text{NPV}_{\text{FC}}}{S_0}$$

where $S_0$ is the current spot rate (home/foreign).

**Method 2 — Home-Currency Approach (HC):**
Forecast cash flows in **foreign currency**, convert each future CF to home currency using the **expected future spot rate** $E[S_t]$, then discount at the **home discount rate** adjusted for country risk.

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{Home}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t^{\text{FC}} \cdot E[S_t]}{(1+k_{\text{Home,adj}})^t} - I_0^{\text{Home}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods give the **same answer** *if* purchasing power parity (PPP) and interest rate parity (IRP) hold. In practice, they differ by the magnitude of the FX-risk premium.

### Adjusted Discount Rate for Foreign Project

$$\boxed{k_{\text{adj}} = R_{f,\text{home}} + \beta_{\text{project}} \cdot (R_m - R_{f,\text{home}}) + \text{Country Risk Premium}}$$

The **Country Risk Premium (CRP)** can be estimated as the sovereign CDS spread or as the default spread on the host country's foreign-currency bonds.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> This table is the **single most important revision artifact** for Module 4. Print it and keep it for the night before the exam.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Notation & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Rationing Objective | $\max \sum \text{NPV}_i \cdot x_i$ s.t. $\sum I_i x_i \le B$ | $x_i \in \{0,1\}$; $B$ in ₹ crore | $I_i > 0$; $\text{NPV}_i > 0$ |
| Profitability Index | $\text{PI}_i = \text{PV of inflows} \div I_i$ | Dimensionless ratio | Accept if $\text{PI} \ge 1$ unconstrained |
| Divisible PI Variant | Fractional funding of last project | Last project's funding = $(B - \sum I_{\text{accepted}}) \div I_{\text{last}}$ | Project must be *divisible* |
| Sensitivity Coefficient | $\frac{\Delta \text{NPV}/\text{NPV}_{\text{base}}}{\Delta X_j / X_{j,\text{base}}}$ | Dimensionless | Higher magnitude = more critical |
| Expected NPV | $E[\text{NPV}] = \sum_s p_s \cdot \text{NPV}_s$ | ₹ | $\sum p_s = 1$ |
| Variance of NPV | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_s \cdot (\text{NPV}_s - E[\text{NPV}])^2$ | (₹)$^2$ | Always $\ge 0$ |
| Std. Deviation of NPV | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | ₹ | Lower $\sigma$ = less risk (for same $E[\text{NPV}]$) |
| Coefficient of Variation | $\text{CV} = \sigma \div E[\text{NPV}]$ | Dimensionless | Compare across project sizes |
| Certainty Equivalent NPV | $\text{NPV} = \sum \frac{\alpha_t \cdot CF_t}{(1+R_f)^t} - I_0$ | ₹ | $0 \le \alpha_t \le 1$ |
| Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate | $k = R_f + \text{Risk Premium}$ | % per annum | $k > R_f$ |
| Adjusted Discount Rate (Foreign) | $k_{\text{adj}} = R_{f,\text{home}} + \beta(R_m - R_{f,\text{home}}) + \text{CRP}$ | % per annum | CRP $> 0$ for emerging markets |
| Foreign-Currency NPV | $\text{NPV}_{\text{Home}} = \text{NPV}_{\text{FC}} \div S_0$ | ₹ | $S_0$ = spot rate (home/foreign) |
| Home-Currency NPV | $\text{NPV} = \sum \frac{CF_t^{\text{FC}} \cdot E[S_t]}{(1+k_{\text{adj}})^t} - I_0$ | ₹ | $E[S_t]$ = expected future spot rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All divisions are mathematical — not pipe-symbol separators. The vertical bar character `\vert` is used **only** as a *mathematical "such that" or "divides"* operator, **never** as a markdown table column separator, to preserve table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to a B.Tech engineer (not just an MBA)**
> - **Capital rationing** — Tech firms like Infosys, TCS face this when choosing between R&D labs, hardware procurement, and plant expansion. The PI method is exactly how engineering project portfolios are triaged in large EPC companies (L&T, Bechtel).
> - **Risk analysis** — In product design (Tata Motors, Boeing), sensitivity analysis identifies which engineering tolerance (e.g., turbine blade fatigue rate) drives the most NPV uncertainty → directs R&D budget.
> - **International capital budgeting** — Critical for any Indian engineering firm setting up a plant abroad (Bajaj Auto in Indonesia, Tata Steel in UK). Wrong FX assumption can wipe out 20–30% of NPV in a single year.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Capital Rationing by Profitability Index

> **KTU-Style Problem (8 marks):**
> A firm has a capital budget of **₹50 crore** and the following four independent projects. All projects are divisible. Determine the optimal project combination using the **Profitability Index method**.

| Project | Initial Investment (₹ cr) | NPV at 12% (₹ cr) |
|---|---|---|
| A (Robotic Assembly Line) | 20 | 9.0 |
| B (Solar Roof Plant) | 15 | 7.5 |
| C (Smart Inventory System) | 25 | 11.0 |
| D (R&D Pilot Lab) | 10 | 4.0 |

### Step 1 — Compute the Profitability Index for Each Project

Recall: $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{PV of inflows}}{I} = \dfrac{\text{NPV} + I}{I} = 1 + \dfrac{\text{NPV}}{I}$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since NPV = PV of inflows − I, then PV of inflows = NPV + I. Therefore PI = (NPV + I) ÷ I.

| Project | $\text{NPV}$ | $I$ | $\text{PV of inflows} = \text{NPV} + I$ | $\text{PI} = (\text{NPV}+I) \div I$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 9.0 | 20 | 29.0 | 29.0 / 20 = **1.45** |
| B | 7.5 | 15 | 22.5 | 22.5 / 15 = **1.50** |
| C | 11.0 | 25 | 36.0 | 36.0 / 25 = **1.44** |
| D | 4.0 | 10 | 14.0 | 14.0 / 10 = **1.40** |

### Step 2 — Rank Projects in Descending Order of PI

| Rank | Project | PI | Investment (₹ cr) | Cumulative Investment (₹ cr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | B | 1.50 | 15 | 15 |
| 2 | A | 1.45 | 20 | 35 |
| 3 | C | 1.44 | 25 | 60 (exceeds!) |
| 4 | D | 1.40 | 10 | — |

### Step 3 — Apply the Budget Constraint (₹50 cr)

- Accept B (cost 15) → cumulative = 15.
- Accept A (cost 20) → cumulative = 35.
- Budget left = 50 − 35 = **₹15 cr**.
- Project C costs 25 — cannot fully fund. *Since projects are divisible*, fund a **fraction** of C:

$$x_C = \frac{\text{Budget remaining}}{I_C} = \frac{15}{25} = 0.60$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A fraction $x_C$ of project C's NPV is realized, because NPV scales linearly with investment for divisible projects.

- NPV from fractional C = 0.60 × 11.0 = **₹6.60 cr**.

### Step 4 — Compute Total NPV

$$\text{Total NPV} = \text{NPV}_B + \text{NPV}_A + 0.60 \cdot \text{NPV}_C$$

$$\text{Total NPV} = 7.5 + 9.0 + 0.60 \times 11.0 = 7.5 + 9.0 + 6.6 = \mathbf{23.1 \text{ crore}}$$

### Step 5 — Decision Summary

> **Optimal Selection: B (full) + A (full) + 60% of C → Total NPV = ₹23.1 crore, Investment = ₹50 crore.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Sensitivity Analysis

> **Problem:** A project requires an initial investment of ₹100 lakh and generates expected cash inflows of ₹40 lakh per year for 4 years. Cost of capital is 12%. Find the NPV and then the sensitivity of NPV to a ±20% change in *sales revenue* (assuming sales = the only variable and CF scales linearly with sales).

### Step 1 — Base-Case NPV

We treat the annuity factor. The PV of an annuity of ₹40 lakh for 4 years at 12%:

$$\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 4) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-4}}{0.12}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{-4}$:
$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^4 = 1.2544^2 = 1.5735$$
$$(1.12)^{-4} = 1 \div 1.5735 = 0.6355$$

$$\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 4) = \frac{1 - 0.6355}{0.12} = \frac{0.3645}{0.12} = 3.0373$$

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{base}} = 40 \times 3.0373 - 100 = 121.49 - 100 = \mathbf{21.49 \text{ lakh}}$$

### Step 2 — Optimistic Case (+20% in CF)

New CF = 40 × 1.20 = ₹48 lakh

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{opt}} = 48 \times 3.0373 - 100 = 145.79 - 100 = 45.79 \text{ lakh}$$

### Step 3 — Pessimistic Case (−20% in CF)

New CF = 40 × 0.80 = ₹32 lakh

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{pess}} = 32 \times 3.0373 - 100 = 97.19 - 100 = -2.81 \text{ lakh}$$

### Step 4 — Sensitivity Coefficient

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta \text{NPV} / \text{NPV}_{\text{base}}}{\Delta X / X_{\text{base}}} = \frac{(-2.81 - 21.49) / 21.49}{(-20)\%} = \frac{-24.30 / 21.49}{-0.20}$$

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{-1.131}{-0.20} = \mathbf{5.65}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1% change in sales revenue causes a **5.65% change in NPV** — NPV is highly sensitive to sales. Management must invest in better sales forecasting.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Expected NPV & Standard Deviation (Risk)

> **Problem:** A project has three possible outcomes:

| Scenario | NPV (₹ lakh) | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| Boom | 60 | 0.30 |
| Normal | 30 | 0.50 |
| Recession | −10 | 0.20 |

> Compute $E[\text{NPV}]$, $\sigma_{\text{NPV}}$, and CV.

### Step 1 — Expected NPV

$$E[\text{NPV}] = \sum p_i \cdot \text{NPV}_i$$

$$E[\text{NPV}] = (0.30)(60) + (0.50)(30) + (0.20)(-10)$$

$$E[\text{NPV}] = 18.0 + 15.0 - 2.0 = \mathbf{31.0 \text{ lakh}}$$

### Step 2 — Variance

$$\sigma^2 = \sum p_i \cdot (\text{NPV}_i - E[\text{NPV}])^2$$

- Boom: $(60 - 31)^2 = 29^2 = 841$; weighted = $0.30 \times 841 = 252.30$
- Normal: $(30 - 31)^2 = 1$; weighted = $0.50 \times 1 = 0.50$
- Recession: $(-10 - 31)^2 = (-41)^2 = 1681$; weighted = $0.20 \times 1681 = 336.20$

$$\sigma^2 = 252.30 + 0.50 + 336.20 = 589.00$$

### Step 3 — Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{589.00} = \mathbf{24.27 \text{ lakh}}$$

### Step 4 — Coefficient of Variation

$$\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma}{E[\text{NPV}]} = \frac{24.27}{31.0} = \mathbf{0.783}$$

> **Interpretation:** NPV is 31 ± 24.27 lakh. The high CV (0.78) indicates the project is **relatively risky**. If another project has $E[\text{NPV}] = 20$ lakh and $\sigma = 10$ lakh → CV = 0.50 → the second project is **relatively safer**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — International Capital Budgeting (FC Approach)

> **Problem:** An Indian firm invests in a US project costing **\$1,000,000** today. Expected annual cash flow for 3 years = **\$400,000**. US risk-free rate = 4%, US market risk premium = 6%, project beta = 1.2, country risk premium (USA) = 0%. Current spot rate $S_0 = ₹83/\$$. Compute the NPV in rupees using the **Foreign-Currency Approach**.

### Step 1 — Compute Adjusted Discount Rate (in USD)

$$k_{\text{USD}} = R_{f,\text{US}} + \beta \cdot (\text{Market Risk Premium}) + \text{CRP}$$

$$k_{\text{USD}} = 4\% + 1.2 \times 6\% + 0\% = 4\% + 7.2\% = 11.2\%$$

### Step 2 — Compute NPV in USD

$$\text{PVIFA}(11.2\%, 3) = \frac{1 - (1.112)^{-3}}{0.112}$$

Compute $(1.112)^3$:
$$(1.112)^2 = 1.236544$$
$$(1.112)^3 = 1.236544 \times 1.112 = 1.375037$$
$$(1.112)^{-3} = 1 / 1.375037 = 0.72725$$

$$\text{PVIFA} = \frac{1 - 0.72725}{0.112} = \frac{0.27275}{0.112} = 2.4353$$

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{USD}} = 400{,}000 \times 2.4353 - 1{,}000{,}000 = 974{,}120 - 1{,}000{,}000 = -25{,}880 \text{ USD}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Home Currency (₹)

> **Conversion Logic:** Under the FC approach, we convert the NPV at the **current spot rate** because the entire project's risk is already embedded in the foreign discount rate.

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{INR}} = \frac{\text{NPV}_{\text{USD}}}{S_0} = \frac{-25{,}880}{83} = \mathbf{-311.81 \text{ lakh}}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> The NPV is **negative** — **REJECT** the project. A common KTU mistake: forgetting to *divide* by $S_0$ (the NPV in USD is positive in absolute terms even though it is negative — students sometimes confuse signs).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — International Capital Budgeting (HC Approach)

> **Same data** as Example 4, but now use the **Home-Currency Approach**. Indian risk-free rate = 7%, Indian market risk premium = 8%, project beta (from Indian perspective) = 1.5, country risk premium for USA exposure = 0%. Expected future spot rates: $E[S_1] = ₹85$, $E[S_2] = ₹87$, $E[S_3] = ₹89$.

### Step 1 — Adjusted Discount Rate (Indian Rupee)

$$k_{\text{INR,adj}} = 7\% + 1.5 \times 8\% + 0\% = 7\% + 12\% = 19\%$$

### Step 2 — Convert Each Future CF to INR

| Year | CF in USD | $E[S_t]$ | CF in INR (lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400,000 | 85 | 340.0 |
| 2 | 400,000 | 87 | 348.0 |
| 3 | 400,000 | 89 | 356.0 |

### Step 3 — Discount at $k = 19\%$

Compute discount factors:
- $1.19^{-1} = 0.8403$
- $1.19^{-2} = 0.7062$
- $1.19^{-3} = 0.5934$

| Year | CF (₹ lakh) | DF | PV (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 340.0 | 0.8403 | 285.71 |
| 2 | 348.0 | 0.7062 | 245.76 |
| 3 | 356.0 | 0.5934 | 211.25 |
| | | **Total PV** | **742.72** |

### Step 4 — NPV in INR

Initial investment in INR = $1{,}000{,}000 \times 83 = 8.30 \text{ crore} = 830.0 \text{ lakh}$

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{INR}} = 742.72 - 830.00 = \mathbf{-87.28 \text{ lakh}}$$

> **Note:** The two methods give *different* NPVs (−311.81 vs −87.28) because the assumed future exchange rates do **not** exactly match interest rate parity. In an *efficient FX market* with IRP holding, both would coincide.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Certainty Equivalent Method

> **Problem:** A project requires ₹100 lakh today and produces risky cash flows of ₹50 lakh, ₹60 lakh, ₹50 lakh at the end of years 1, 2, 3. Certainty equivalent coefficients are $\alpha_1 = 0.90$, $\alpha_2 = 0.80$, $\alpha_3 = 0.70$. Risk-free rate = 6%. Find NPV.

### Step 1 — Compute Certainty-Equivalent Cash Flows

$$\text{CE}_t = \alpha_t \cdot \text{CF}_t$$

| Year | CF | $\alpha$ | CE CF |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 0.90 | 45.0 |
| 2 | 60 | 0.80 | 48.0 |
| 3 | 50 | 0.70 | 35.0 |

### Step 2 — Discount at Risk-Free Rate 6%

- $1.06^{-1} = 0.9434$
- $1.06^{-2} = 0.8900$
- $1.06^{-3} = 0.8396$

| Year | CE CF | DF | PV |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 45.0 | 0.9434 | 42.45 |
| 2 | 48.0 | 0.8900 | 42.72 |
| 3 | 35.0 | 0.8396 | 29.39 |
| | | **Total PV** | **114.56** |

### Step 3 — NPV

$$\text{NPV} = 114.56 - 100.0 = \mathbf{+14.56 \text{ lakh}} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Rationing — Decision Flow

This block diagram maps the **algorithmic decision flow** of the Profitability Index ranking method when a finite budget is present.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: All Independent Projects Identified] --> B[Step 1: Compute NPV at Hurdle Rate for Each Project]
    B --> C[Step 2: Discard Negative NPV Projects]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute PI for Each Remaining Project]
    D --> E[Step 4: Rank Projects in Descending Order of PI]
    E --> F{Step 5: Divisible or Indivisible?}
    F -->|Divisible| G[Step 6a: Accept Projects in PI Order]
    G --> H[Budget Exhausted?]
    H -->|No| G
    H -->|Yes, Mid-Project| I[Step 7a: Fund Fractional Last Project]
    I --> J[Step 8: Sum Total NPV]
    F -->|Indivisible| K[Step 6b: Try All Combinations or Use ILP Solver]
    K --> L[Step 7b: Pick Combination with Max Total NPV within Budget]
    L --> J
    J --> M[End: Optimal Project Portfolio]
```

## 4.2 Risk Analysis — Method Selection Tree

The following block diagram helps a student pick the correct risk-analysis tool for a given situation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Start: Need to Assess Project Risk] --> RQ{Is Probability Distribution Available?}
    RQ -->|No, Only Point Estimates| RA[Sensitivity Analysis]
    RA --> RA1[Vary One Variable at a Time]
    RA1 --> RA2[Construct Tornado Diagram]
    RA2 --> RA3[Identify Most Critical Variable]
    RQ -->|Yes, Discrete Scenarios| RB[Scenario Analysis]
    RB --> RB1[Define Best, Base, Worst Cases]
    RB1 --> RB2[Compute NPV for Each Scenario]
    RB2 --> RB3[Expected NPV = Sum of p_s x NPV_s]
    RQ -->|Yes, Continuous Distribution| RC[Statistical Methods]
    RC --> RC1[Compute E of NPV]
    RC1 --> RC2[Compute Variance and Std Dev]
    RC2 --> RC3[Compute CV for Cross-Project Comparison]
    RC --> RD[Alternative: Certainty Equivalent or RADR]
    RD --> RD1[Pick alpha_t or Risk Premium]
    RD1 --> RD2[Discount at R_f or k = R_f + Premium]
    RD2 --> RD3[Compute NPV]
```

## 4.3 International Capital Budgeting — Functional Architecture

The block diagram below shows the **two parallel computational pathways** (FC vs HC) and how they converge to a single NPV decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Project Cash Flow Data"]
        ICF[Forecast CF_t in Foreign Currency]
        ISR[Spot Rate S_0 and Expected Future Rates E of S_t]
    end

    subgraph FCAPPROACH["Foreign Currency Pathway"]
        FDR[Foreign Discount Rate k_USD]
        FDR --> FNPV[Compute NPV in Foreign Currency]
        FNPV --> FCON[Divide NPV by Spot Rate S_0]
        FCON --> FCNPV[NPV in Home Currency FC]
    end

    subgraph HCAPPROACH["Home Currency Pathway"]
        CADJ[Adjusted Home Rate = R_f + Beta MRP + CRP]
        CADJ --> HCON[Convert Each CF_t to Home CF using E of S_t]
        HCON --> HDIS[Discount at Adjusted Home Rate]
        HDIS --> HCNPV[NPV in Home Currency HC]
    end

    ICF --> FNPV
    ICF --> HCON
    ISR --> FCON
    ISR --> HCON
    FCNPV --> DEC{NPV Decision}
    HCNPV --> DEC
    DEC -->|NPV > 0| ACC[Accept Foreign Project]
    DEC -->|NPV < 0| REJ[Reject Foreign Project]
```

## 4.4 Country Risk Premium — Estimation Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR[Country Risk Premium Required] --> CRM{Which Estimation Method?}
    CRM -->|Method 1| CDS[Sovereign CDS Spread of Host Country]
    CRM -->|Method 2| DB[Default Spread on USD Bonds of Host Country]
    CRM -->|Method 3| CRP[Damodaran Country Risk Premium Table]
    CDS --> CADDR[Add CRP to k = R_f + Beta x MRP]
    DB --> CADDR
    CRP --> CADDR
    CADDR --> KADJ[Final Adjusted Discount Rate k_adj]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not photos?** KTU 2024 answer sheets are evaluated on **logical flow** of concepts. The examiner is not looking for an artistic diagram — they want to see that you understand the *sequence* of operations. A clean Mermaid-style block diagram earns full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Question A.1
**Differentiate between Hard Capital Rationing and Soft Capital Rationing. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hard Capital Rationing | Soft Capital Rationing |
|---|---|---|
| Source of Constraint | **External** — markets, lenders, regulators. | **Internal** — management policy. |
| Lifting the Constraint | Requires market conditions to change or new capital source found. | Board resolution can lift it. |
| Example | A startup denied a bank loan due to a credit crunch. | A firm limits investment to ₹50 cr/year to maintain a 1:1 debt-equity ratio. |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition of each, 1 for example each.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question A.2
**What is the Coefficient of Variation? Why is it preferred over standard deviation when comparing risk across projects of different sizes?**

**Model Answer:**

$$\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{NPV}}}{E[\text{NPV}]}$$

Coefficient of Variation is a **dimensionless, scale-free** risk measure. Standard deviation is in the same unit as NPV (₹), so a project with ₹1,000 cr investment will have a larger $\sigma$ simply because of size — not necessarily because it is riskier. CV normalizes risk by expected return, enabling like-for-like comparison.

**Example:** Project X: $\sigma$ = 50, E[NPV] = 100 → CV = 0.50. Project Y: $\sigma$ = 60, E[NPV] = 200 → CV = 0.30. **Y is less risky per unit of return** even though $\sigma$ is higher.

**(3 marks — 1 formula, 1 explanation, 1 numerical example.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q11]**

---

### Question B.A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A firm has a capital budget of **₹40 lakh** and five independent projects as below. Use the **Profitability Index method** to determine the optimal project combination (projects are divisible). What is the maximum total NPV achievable?

| Project | Investment (₹ lakh) | NPV (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 10 | 5.0 |
| P2 | 15 | 6.0 |
| P3 | 20 | 7.0 |
| P4 | 12 | 4.5 |
| P5 | 8 | 3.6 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A project has the following outcomes:

| Scenario | NPV (₹ lakh) | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| Good | 50 | 0.25 |
| Average | 25 | 0.50 |
| Poor | −15 | 0.25 |

Compute $E[\text{NPV}]$, $\sigma_{\text{NPV}}$, and Coefficient of Variation. Comment on the risk profile.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Compute PI for each project:**

| Project | NPV | I | PV of inflows = NPV + I | PI = (NPV + I) / I |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 5.0 | 10 | 15.0 | **1.50** |
| P2 | 6.0 | 15 | 21.0 | **1.40** |
| P3 | 7.0 | 20 | 27.0 | **1.35** |
| P4 | 4.5 | 12 | 16.5 | **1.375** |
| P5 | 3.6 | 8 | 11.6 | **1.45** |

**Step 2 — Rank in descending order of PI:**

| Rank | Project | PI | Investment | Cumulative |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 1.50 | 10 | 10 |
| 2 | P5 | 1.45 | 8 | 18 |
| 3 | P2 | 1.40 | 15 | 33 |
| 4 | P4 | 1.375 | 12 | 45 (exceeds!) |
| 5 | P3 | 1.35 | 20 | — |

**Step 3 — Budget constraint application:**

Budget remaining after P1, P5, P2 = 40 − 33 = **₹7 lakh**.

P4 needs ₹12, so we fund fractionally:

$$x_{P4} = \frac{7}{12} = 0.5833$$

**Step 4 — Total NPV:**

$$\text{NPV}_{\text{total}} = 5.0 + 3.6 + 6.0 + 0.5833 \times 4.5 = 5.0 + 3.6 + 6.0 + 2.625 = \mathbf{17.225 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Decision: Accept P1, P5, P2 fully and 58.33% of P4 → Total NPV = ₹17.225 lakh, Investment = ₹40 lakh.** ✅

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Expected NPV:**

$$E[\text{NPV}] = (0.25)(50) + (0.50)(25) + (0.25)(-15)$$
$$E[\text{NPV}] = 12.5 + 12.5 - 3.75 = \mathbf{21.25 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Step 2 — Variance:**

| Scenario | $\text{NPV}_i - E[\text{NPV}]$ | Squared | Weighted ($p_i \times$ Sq) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Good | 50 − 21.25 = 28.75 | 826.5625 | 206.6406 |
| Average | 25 − 21.25 = 3.75 | 14.0625 | 7.0313 |
| Poor | −15 − 21.25 = −36.25 | 1314.0625 | 328.5156 |

$$\sigma^2 = 206.6406 + 7.0313 + 328.5156 = 542.1875$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{542.1875} = \mathbf{23.286 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$\text{CV} = \frac{23.286}{21.25} = \mathbf{1.096}$$

**Comment:** CV > 1 indicates the project is **highly risky** relative to its expected return. The "Poor" scenario leads to negative NPV (−15 lakh), so management should consider risk-mitigation strategies or demand a higher risk premium in the discount rate.

---

### Question B.B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Sensitivity Analysis** as a tool for risk assessment in capital budgeting. What is a **Tornado Diagram**? How is the **sensitivity coefficient** calculated, and what does a high value indicate?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An Indian MNC is evaluating a 3-year project in Germany costing **€500,000** with expected annual cash flows of **€200,000**. The euro discount rate is 9%, the current spot rate is **₹90/€**, and the expected future spot rates are ₹92, ₹94, ₹96 at year-ends 1, 2, 3. Use the **Home-Currency Approach** to compute the NPV in ₹. Take the Indian risk-adjusted discount rate as **15%** (assume no separate country risk premium needed).

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** Sensitivity Analysis (or "What-if Analysis") is a risk-assessment technique that measures how the NPV of a project changes in response to changes in **one input variable**, holding all other variables at their base-case (expected) values. The variable that produces the largest change in NPV is the **most critical** and warrants the most managerial attention and forecasting effort.

**Tornado Diagram (2 marks):** A Tornado Diagram is a horizontal bar chart that ranks the sensitivity of NPV to each input variable. The variable with the **longest bar (biggest impact)** is placed at the **top** of the chart, and the variable with the smallest impact is at the bottom — producing a "tornado" shape. The chart displays both the upside and downside swing of NPV for each variable.

**Sensitivity Coefficient Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{Sensitivity Coefficient}_{j} = \frac{\Delta \text{NPV} / \text{NPV}_{\text{base}}}{\Delta X_j / X_{j,\text{base}}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A high absolute value (e.g., 5.65 in our worked example) means that NPV is highly elastic with respect to that input — a 1% change in the input causes a >1% change in NPV. Management should focus on obtaining more accurate forecasts for that variable or hedge the risk.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Convert each future CF to INR:**

| Year | CF (€) | Expected Rate (₹/€) | CF (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200,000 | 92 | 184.0 |
| 2 | 200,000 | 94 | 188.0 |
| 3 | 200,000 | 96 | 192.0 |

**Step 2 — Initial investment in INR:**

$$I_0 = 500{,}000 \times 90 = 4.5 \text{ crore} = 450.0 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 3 — Discount factors at 15%:**

- $1.15^{-1} = 0.8696$
- $1.15^{-2} = 0.7561$
- $1.15^{-3} = 0.6575$

**Step 4 — Compute PV of each CF and total:**

| Year | CF (₹ lakh) | DF | PV (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 184.0 | 0.8696 | 160.01 |
| 2 | 188.0 | 0.7561 | 142.15 |
| 3 | 192.0 | 0.6575 | 126.24 |
| | | **Total PV** | **428.40** |

**Step 5 — NPV:**

$$\text{NPV} = 428.40 - 450.00 = \mathbf{-21.60 \text{ lakh}}$$

**Decision:** Since NPV < 0, **REJECT the project.** The expected cash flows in rupee terms, even with the assumed rupee depreciation, are insufficient to recover the initial investment at the 15% discount rate.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 (Read Before Writing Your Exam)**
>
> 1. **Mixing PI ranking with NPV ranking** — Many students rank by NPV under rationing. *Wrong.* NPV ranking ignores capital efficiency and can exhaust the budget on one big project. Always use **PI** for rationing unless explicitly asked otherwise.
> 2. **Sign error in NPV sensitivity** — When CF drops, NPV drops. The sensitivity coefficient is a *ratio* — the sign cancels. Don't write "negative sensitivity" as if it means something bad.
> 3. **Forgetting the spot-rate division in the FC approach** — The FC method requires dividing NPV(FC) by $S_0$, not multiplying. Multiplication by $S_0$ is the *most common* error in international NPV problems.
> 4. **Country Risk Premium sign error** — CRP is **always added** to the discount rate (it increases risk). Never subtract.
> 5. **PI = (NPV + I) / I, NOT NPV / I** — A shockingly common KTU error. PI must use *total* PV of inflows, not just NPV. If your PI is 0.45 instead of 1.45, you've fallen into this trap.
> 6. **Ignoring probability sum = 1** — In scenario analysis, the probabilities of all scenarios must sum to 1. If a student computes 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.10, they get 0.90 — and the answer is wrong by 10% of the weight. Always re-check $\sum p_s = 1$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This on Index Card)**
>
> ✅ **Capital Rationing** — occurs when a firm has more positive-NPV projects than its budget allows.
> ✅ **Hard Rationing** = external constraint (market refuses funds). **Soft Rationing** = internal management cap.
> ✅ **PI Method** = (NPV + I) ÷ I. Rank descending, accept until budget exhausted.
> ✅ **Divisible projects** → fund the *fraction* of the last project to exhaust the budget. **Indivisible** → use 0–1 Integer Programming.
> ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis** = vary ONE variable, watch NPV move. **Scenario Analysis** = vary MANY variables consistently. **Statistical** = E[NPV], σ, CV.
> ✅ **Coefficient of Variation** = σ ÷ E[NPV] — the only scale-free risk measure for cross-project comparison.
> ✅ **Certainty Equivalent Method** = replace each CF with $\alpha_t \cdot CF_t$, discount at $R_f$.
> ✅ **RADR Method** = use $k = R_f + \text{Risk Premium}$ to discount expected CFs.
> ✅ **International Capital Budgeting** = NPV calculation with **two extra risks**: FX risk + Country risk.
> ✅ **Adjusted Discount Rate (Foreign)** = $R_{f,\text{home}} + \beta(R_m - R_{f,\text{home}}) + \text{CRP}$.
> ✅ **FC Approach** = discount in foreign currency at foreign rate, divide final NPV by spot rate $S_0$.
> ✅ **HC Approach** = convert each future CF to home currency using $E[S_t]$, discount at home-adjusted rate.
> ✅ Both methods should theoretically give the same NPV if **Interest Rate Parity** holds — in practice they differ slightly.
> ✅ **Accept foreign project only if NPV > 0** in home currency.
> ✅ CRP estimated via **sovereign CDS spread**, **bond default spread**, or **Damodaran's published country risk tables**.
> ✅ Tornado diagram = visual output of sensitivity analysis; the longest bar is the most critical input.
> ✅ Variance is in (₹)² — std dev is in ₹ — CV is dimensionless. Don't confuse units.
> ✅ All probabilities in scenario analysis must sum to 1.
> ✅ PI > 1 → accept (unconstrained); under rationing, rank by PI, not by NPV.
